verses.transliteration: eipontes tō Aarōn, Poiēson hēmin theous hoi proporeusontai hēmōn; ho gar Mōysēs houtos, hos exēgagen hēmas ek gēs Aigyptou, ouk oidamen ti egeneto autō.
verses.study_note: Stephen recounts the golden calf incident (Exodus 32:1, 23), where the people demanded Aaron, 'Make us gods to go before us: for as for this Moses, which brought us out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him.' This act of idolatry, committed shortly after receiving the Law, epitomizes their rebellion and impatience. It underscores their tendency to replace God's invisible presence with tangible idols, a critique Stephen implicitly extends to their reliance on the physical temple.

verses.kjv_text: And they made a calf in those days, and offered sacrifice unto the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their own hands.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐμοσχοποίησαν ἐν ἐκείναις ταῖς ἡμέραις, καὶ ἀνήγαγον θυσίαν τῷ εἰδώλῳ, καὶ εὐφραίνοντο ἐν τοῖς ἔργοις τῶν χειρῶν αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: kai emoschopoiēsan en ekeinais tais hēmerais, kai anēgagon thysian tō eidōlō, kai euphrainonto en tois ergois tōn cheirōn autōn.
verses.study_note: The Israelites 'made a calf in those days, and offered sacrifice unto the idol, and rejoiced in the works of their own hands' (Exodus 32:4-6). This vivid description of idolatry highlights their self-worship and rejection of the true God. Stephen's emphasis on 'works of their own hands' is a subtle but powerful critique, suggesting that their reliance on man-made religious structures (like the temple) and traditions mirrors this ancient idolatry.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse exposes the essence of idolatry: worshipping 'the works of their own hands.' It reveals humanity's propensity to create gods in their own image or to rely on human achievements rather than on the living God. This historical example serves as a warning against any form of self-reliance or misplaced worship, subtly critiquing the Sanhedrin's potential over-reliance on the physical temple and their own traditions.

verses.kjv_text: Then God turned, and gave them up to worship the host of heaven; as it is written in the book of the prophets, O ye house of Israel, have ye offered to me slain beasts and sacrifices by the space of forty years in the wilderness?
verses.original_text: ἔστρεψεν δὲ ὁ Θεὸς καὶ παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς λατρεύειν τῇ στρατιᾷ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ, καθὼς γέγραπται ἐν βίβλῳ προφητῶν, Μὴ σφάγια καὶ θυσίας προσηνέγκατέ μοι ἔτη τεσσαράκοντα ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ, οἶκος Ἰσραήλ;
verses.transliteration: estrepsen de ho Theos kai paredōken autous latreuein tē stratiā tou ouranou, kathōs gegraptai en biblō prophētōn, Mē sphagia kai thysias prosēnenkaté moi etē tessarakonta en tē erēmō, oikos Israēl?
verses.study_note: Stephen quotes Amos 5:25-27, stating that 'God turned, and gave them up to worship the host of heaven.' This divine judgment of 'giving them up' to their idolatry is a severe consequence of their rebellion. The rhetorical question from Amos, 'Have ye offered to me slain beasts and sacrifices by the space of forty years in the wilderness, O ye house of Israel?' implies that their worship during that period was often insincere or mixed with idolatry, not solely directed to God.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse reveals a profound theological truth: God's judgment can manifest as 'giving them up' to their chosen idolatry. When people persistently reject Him, God may allow them to pursue their false gods, leading to deeper spiritual bondage. The quote from Amos challenges the assumption that outward religious acts (sacrifices) are sufficient without genuine heart-worship, highlighting the spiritual nature of true devotion.

verses.kjv_text: Yea, ye took up the tabernacle of Moloch, and the star of your god Remphan, figures which ye made to worship them: and I will carry you away beyond Babylon.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀνελάβετε τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ Μολὸχ καὶ τὸ ἄστρον τοῦ θεοῦ ὑμῶν Ῥομφάν, τοὺς τύπους οὓς ἐποιήσατε προσκυνεῖν αὐτοῖς· καὶ μετοικιῶ ὑμᾶς ἐπέκεινα Βαβυλῶνος.
verses.transliteration: kai anelabete tēn skēnēn tou Moloch kai to astron tou theou hymōn Romphan, tous typous hous epoiēsate proskynein autois; kai metoikiō hymas epekeina Babylōnos.
verses.study_note: Continuing the quote from Amos 5:26-27, Stephen accuses Israel of carrying 'the tabernacle of Moloch, and the star of your god Remphan' (or Rephan, a variant of Saturn). These were pagan deities worshipped by the Israelites, even during their wilderness journey. The consequence of this idolatry is God's judgment: 'I will carry you away beyond Babylon.' Stephen uses this prophecy to remind his audience of their ancestors' persistent idolatry and the resulting exile, connecting past judgment to their present spiritual state.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse exposes the pervasive nature of idolatry within Israel's history, even during the wilderness period. The worship of Moloch and Remphan represents a deep spiritual compromise and syncretism. God's response, 'I will carry you away beyond Babylon,' demonstrates that persistent idolatry leads to divine judgment and exile, underscoring the seriousness of unfaithfulness to the one true God.

verses.kjv_text: Our fathers had the tabernacle of witness in the wilderness, as he had appointed, speaking unto Moses, that he should make it according to the fashion that he had seen.
verses.original_text: Ἡ σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ἦν τοῖς πατράσιν ἡμῶν ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ, καθὼς διετάξατο ὁ λαλῶν τῷ Μωϋσῇ ποιῆσαι αὐτὴν κατὰ τὸν τύπον ὃν ἑωράκει·
verses.transliteration: Hē skēnē tou martyríou ēn tois patrasin hēmōn en tē erēmō, kathōs dietaxato ho lalōn tō Mōysē poiēsai autēn kata ton typon hon heōrakei;
verses.study_note: Stephen now shifts to the 'tabernacle of witness' (or 'tent of testimony'), emphasizing its divine origin and temporary nature. It was made 'according to the fashion that he had seen' (Exodus 25:9, 40), meaning it was a copy of a heavenly pattern. This highlights that even the most sacred structure was not an end in itself but a divinely ordained symbol. This prepares for his argument that God's presence is not confined to man-made structures.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'tabernacle of witness' (σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου) was a divinely appointed, portable sanctuary, built 'according to the fashion that he had seen' (κατὰ τὸν τύπον ὃν ἑωράκει). This emphasizes its heavenly origin and symbolic nature, serving as a temporary dwelling for God's presence among His people. It foreshadows the ultimate dwelling of God among humanity in Christ (John 1:14) and challenges the notion that God's presence is limited to fixed, earthly structures.

verses.kjv_text: Which also our fathers that came after brought in with Jesus into the possession of the Gentiles, whom God drave out before the face of our fathers, unto the days of David;
verses.original_text: ἣν καὶ εἰσήγαγον διαδεξάμενοι οἱ πατέρες ἡμῶν μετὰ Ἰησοῦ ἐν τῇ κατασχέσει τῶν ἐθνῶν, ὧν ἐξῶσεν ὁ Θεὸς ἀπὸ προσώπου τῶν πατέρων ἡμῶν, ἕως τῶν ἡμερῶν Δαυίδ·
verses.transliteration: Hēn kai eisēgagon diadexaménoi hoi pateres hēmōn meta Iēsou en tē kataschesei tōn ethnōn, hōn exōsen ho Theos apo prosōpou tōn paterōn hēmōn, heōs tōn hēmerōn Dauid;
verses.study_note: Stephen states that 'our fathers that came after brought in' the tabernacle 'with Jesus' (Joshua) into the possession of the Gentiles (Canaanites), whom God drove out. This continues the historical narrative, showing the tabernacle's journey through the conquest of Canaan 'unto the days of David.' This emphasizes the tabernacle's mobile nature and its role during the period of judges and early kings, before the temple was built.

verses.kjv_text: Who found favour before God, and desired to find a tabernacle for the God of Jacob.
verses.original_text: ὃς εὗρεν χάριν ἐνώπιον τοῦ Θεοῦ καὶ ᾐτήσατο εὑρεῖν σκήνωμα τῷ Θεῷ Ἰακώβ.
verses.transliteration: hos heuren charin enōpion tou Theou kai ēitēsato heurein skēnōma tō Theō Iakōb.
verses.study_note: David 'found favour before God' and 'desired to find a tabernacle for the God of Jacob' (2 Samuel 7:1-3; Psalm 132:4-5). This highlights David's piety and his desire to build a permanent dwelling place for God. Stephen acknowledges David's good intentions, setting up the contrast with Solomon, who actually built the temple, and ultimately with God's true dwelling place.

verses.kjv_text: But Solomon built him an house.
verses.original_text: Σολομὼν δὲ οἰκοδόμησεν αὐτῷ οἶκον.
verses.transliteration: Solomōn de oikodomēsen autō oikon.
verses.study_note: Stephen states simply, 'But Solomon built him an house' (1 Kings 6:1-38). This marks the transition from the mobile tabernacle to the fixed temple. While acknowledging the temple's construction, Stephen immediately moves to qualify its significance, preparing his audience for his radical theological assertion about God's true dwelling place.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit the most High dwelleth not in temples made with hands; as saith the prophet,
verses.original_text: Ἀλλ’ οὐχ ὁ ὕψιστος ἐν χειροποιήτοις κατοικεῖ, καθὼς ὁ προφήτης λέγει,
verses.transliteration: All' ouch ho hypsistos en cheiropoiētois katoikei, kathōs ho prophētēs legei,
verses.study_note: This is a pivotal statement in Stephen's defense: 'Howbeit the most High dwelleth not in temples made with hands.' This directly challenges the Sanhedrin's over-reliance on the physical temple as the exclusive dwelling place of God. Stephen asserts God's transcendence, quoting Isaiah to support his claim. This is a direct refutation of the charge that he spoke against the temple (Acts 6:13-14), by redefining God's presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is a foundational theological declaration: 'the most High dwelleth not in temples made with hands.' Stephen challenges the misconception that God's presence is confined to human-built structures. This truth, rooted in Old Testament prophecy, underscores God's transcendence and omnipresence, preparing the way for understanding Christ as the true temple (John 2:19-21) and the Holy Spirit dwelling in believers (1 Cor 6:19).

verses.kjv_text: Heaven is my throne, and earth is my footstool: what house will ye build me? saith the Lord: or what is the place of my rest?
verses.original_text: Ὁ οὐρανός μοι θρόνος, ἡ δὲ γῆ ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν μου· ποῖον οἶκον οἰκοδομήσετέ μοι, λέγει Κύριος, ἢ τίς τόπος τῆς καταπαύσεώς μου;
verses.transliteration: Ho ouranos moi thronos, hē de gē hypopodion tōn podōn mou; poion oikon oikodomēsete moi, legei Kyrios, ē tis topos tēs katapauseōs mou?
verses.study_note: Stephen quotes Isaiah 66:1-2: 'Heaven is my throne, and earth is my footstool: what house will ye build me? saith the Lord: or what is the place of my rest?' This prophetic passage powerfully emphasizes God's infinite majesty and His inability to be contained by any human-made structure. It reinforces the idea that true worship is not bound by physical location but by a spiritual reality, directly undermining the temple's ultimate significance.
verses.doctrinal_note: Quoting Isaiah 66:1-2, Stephen asserts God's infinite transcendence and omnipresence. If 'Heaven is my throne, and earth is my footstool,' then no human-built 'house' can contain or define Him. This challenges any anthropocentric view of God and any exclusive claim to His presence based on physical structures, emphasizing that God's true dwelling is spiritual and universal, not confined to a temple.

verses.kjv_text: Hath not my hand made all these things?
verses.original_text: οὐχὶ ἡ χείρ μου ἐποίησεν ταῦτα πάντα;
verses.transliteration: ouchi hē cheir mou epoiēsen tauta panta?
verses.study_note: The rhetorical question, 'Hath not my hand made all these things?' (Isaiah 66:2), concludes Stephen's argument about God's transcendence. It asserts God's sole creative power over all things, including the heavens and the earth. This implies that the Creator cannot be limited by His creation, nor can He be served by human hands as if He needed anything (Acts 17:24-25). This is a direct challenge to the Sanhedrin's temple-centric theology.
verses.doctrinal_note: This rhetorical question affirms God's absolute sovereignty as Creator of 'all these things.' It underscores His self-sufficiency and independence from human constructs or services. The Creator is not confined by His creation, nor does He require anything from human hands. This truth dismantles any notion that God is dependent on the temple or human rituals, redirecting worship to the transcendent Creator alone.

verses.kjv_text: Ye stiffnecked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, ye do always resist the Holy Ghost: as your fathers did, so do ye.
verses.original_text: Σκληροτράχηλοι καὶ ἀπερίτμητοι καρδίαις καὶ τοῖς ὠσίν, ὑμεῖς ἀεὶ τῷ Πνεύματι τῷ Ἁγίῳ ἀντιπίπτετε, ὡς οἱ πατέρες ὑμῶν καὶ ὑμεῖς.
verses.transliteration: Sklērotrachēloi kai aperitmētoi kardiāis kai tois ōsin, hymeis aei tō Pneumati tō Hagiō antipiptete, hōs hoi pateres hymōn kai hymeis.
verses.study_note: Stephen abruptly shifts from historical narrative to a direct, fiery accusation: 'Ye stiffnecked and uncircumcised in heart and ears, ye do always resist the Holy Ghost: as your fathers did, so do ye.' 'Stiffnecked' (σκληροτράχηλοι) is an Old Testament idiom for stubborn rebellion (Exodus 32:9). 'Uncircumcised in heart and ears' (ἀπερίτμητοι καρδίαις καὶ τοῖς ὠσίν) is a profound spiritual indictment, contrasting with the physical circumcision they valued. It means they are spiritually deaf and unreceptive to God's truth, perpetually resisting the Holy Spirit, just like their ancestors. This is the climax of his argument, directly accusing them of the very resistance he documented throughout Israel's history.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse contains a powerful indictment against spiritual stubbornness and resistance to God. The terms 'stiffnecked' and 'uncircumcised in heart and ears' denote a profound spiritual insensitivity and rebellion against divine truth. Stephen accuses his audience of perpetually 'resist[ing] the Holy Ghost,' highlighting that opposition to God's messengers and truth is ultimately opposition to the Spirit Himself. This reveals a continuity of spiritual rebellion from their ancestors to the present generation.

verses.kjv_text: Which of the prophets have not your fathers persecuted? and they have slain them which shewed before of the coming of the Just One; of whom ye have been now the betrayers and murderers:
verses.original_text: Τίνα τῶν προφητῶν οὐκ ἐδίωξαν οἱ πατέρες ὑμῶν; καὶ ἀπέκτειναν τοὺς προκαταγγείλαντας περὶ τῆς ἐλεύσεως τοῦ Δικαίου, οὗ νῦν ὑμεῖς προδόται καὶ φονεῖς ἐγένεσθε·
verses.transliteration: Tina tōn prophētōn ouk ediōxan hoi pateres hymōn? kai apekteinan tous prokatangeilantas peri tēs eleuseōs tou Dikaiou, hou nyn hymeis prodotai kai phoneis egenesthe;
verses.study_note: Stephen continues his accusation, asking rhetorically, 'Which of the prophets have not your fathers persecuted?' He then directly links this historical pattern to their present sin: 'and they have slain them which shewed before of the coming of the Just One; of whom ye have been now the betrayers and murderers.' 'The Just One' (τοῦ Δικαίου) is a clear reference to Jesus Christ (Acts 3:14; 22:14). Stephen accuses them of fulfilling the pattern of their ancestors by betraying and murdering the Messiah, the ultimate prophet.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is a direct Christological accusation. Stephen links the historical persecution of prophets, who foretold the Messiah's coming, to the Sanhedrin's recent actions. By calling Jesus 'the Just One' (τοῦ Δικαίου) and accusing them of being His 'betrayers and murderers,' Stephen declares their ultimate guilt in rejecting God's final and most perfect messenger. This highlights humanity's persistent sin of rejecting divine revelation, culminating in the crucifixion of Christ.

verses.kjv_text: Who have received the law by the disposition of angels, and have not kept it.
verses.original_text: οἵτινες ἐλάβετε τὸν νόμον εἰς διαταγὰς ἀγγέλων, καὶ οὐκ ἐφυλάξατε.
verses.transliteration: hoitines elabete ton nomon eis diatagas angelōn, kai ouk ephulaxate.
verses.study_note: Stephen concludes his indictment by reminding them that they 'received the law by the disposition of angels' (referring to the angelic mediation at Sinai, Galatians 3:19; Hebrews 2:2), 'and have not kept it.' This is the ultimate charge: despite receiving the Law through divine means and valuing it highly, they failed to obey it. Their hypocrisy and spiritual blindness are fully exposed, justifying Stephen's earlier arguments about God's transcendence over man-made institutions and their own history of rebellion.
verses.doctrinal_note: This final charge exposes the hypocrisy of Stephen's accusers: they prided themselves on possessing the Law, which they received through angelic mediation, yet they 'have not kept it.' This highlights the inadequacy of merely possessing divine revelation without genuine obedience. It underscores the spiritual failure of relying on external adherence to the Law while rejecting its ultimate fulfillment in Christ and resisting the Holy Spirit, revealing the need for a new covenant of grace.

verses.kjv_text: When they heard these things, they were cut to the heart, and they gnashed on him with their teeth.
verses.original_text: Ἀκούοντες δὲ ταῦτα διεπρίοντο ταῖς καρδίαις αὐτῶν καὶ ἔβρυχον τοὺς ὀδόντας ἐπ’ αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: Akouontes de tauta dieprionto tais kardiāis autōn kai ebrychon tous odontas ep' auton.
verses.study_note: Stephen's words had a visceral effect: 'When they heard these things, they were cut to the heart, and they gnashed on him with their teeth.' This intense reaction indicates extreme rage and indignation, not conviction. The phrase 'cut to the heart' (διεπρίοντο ταῖς καρδίαις) implies being sawn asunder, a metaphor for excruciating anger. Their gnashing of teeth signifies uncontrollable fury, foreshadowing their violent response.

verses.kjv_text: But he, being full of the Holy Ghost, looked up stedfastly into heaven, and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God,
verses.original_text: ὑπάρχων δὲ πλήρης Πνεύματος Ἁγίου ἀτενίσας εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν εἶδεν δόξαν Θεοῦ καὶ Ἰησοῦν ἑστῶτα ἐκ δεξιῶν τοῦ Θεοῦ,
verses.transliteration: hyparchōn de plērēs Pneumatos Hagiou atenisas eis ton ouranon eiden doxan Theou kai Iēsoun hestōta ek dexiōn tou Theou,
verses.study_note: In stark contrast to their rage, Stephen, 'being full of the Holy Ghost,' looked 'stedfastly into heaven' and 'saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God.' This vision is a divine affirmation of Stephen's message and a powerful revelation of Christ's exalted status. Jesus 'standing' (ἑστῶτα) is notable; typically, He is depicted as seated at God's right hand (Hebrews 10:12). His standing may signify His readiness to receive Stephen or to act on his behalf.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is a profound Christological and pneumatological affirmation. Stephen, 'full of the Holy Ghost,' receives a divine vision of 'the glory of God, and Jesus standing on the right hand of God.' This confirms Jesus' exalted divine status and His active intercession or readiness to receive Stephen. It demonstrates the Holy Spirit's role in revealing Christ's glory and empowering believers in the face of persecution, offering a glimpse into heavenly realities.

verses.kjv_text: And said, Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of man standing on the right hand of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν, Ἰδοὺ θεωρῶ τοὺς οὐρανοὺς ἀνεῳγμένους καὶ τὸν Υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐκ δεξιῶν ἑστῶτα τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai eipen, Idou theōrō tous ouranous aneōgmenous kai ton Huion tou anthrōpou ek dexiōn hestōta tou Theou.
verses.study_note: Stephen proclaims his vision: 'Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of man standing on the right hand of God.' The title 'Son of man' (Υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου), used by Jesus to refer to Himself (Daniel 7:13-14), is a powerful messianic claim, emphasizing both His humanity and His divine authority as the heavenly judge. This public declaration of Jesus' divine exaltation further enraged the Sanhedrin, who considered it blasphemy.
verses.doctrinal_note: Stephen's declaration of seeing 'the heavens opened, and the Son of man standing on the right hand of God' is a direct affirmation of Jesus' messianic identity and divine authority. The title 'Son of man' (Υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) evokes Daniel 7:13-14, portraying Christ as the exalted, heavenly judge. This vision serves as a powerful testimony to Jesus' resurrection and ascension, confirming His ultimate sovereignty and Stephen's faith in Him.

verses.kjv_text: Then they cried out with a loud voice, and stopped their ears, and ran upon him with one accord,
verses.original_text: Κράξαντες δὲ φωνῇ μεγάλῃ συνέσχον τὰ ὦτα αὐτῶν καὶ ὥρμησαν ὁμοθυμαδὸν ἐπ’ αὐτόν,
verses.transliteration: Kraxantes de phōnē megalē syneschon ta ōta autōn kai hōrmēsan homothymadon ep' auton,
verses.study_note: Their reaction to Stephen's vision was immediate and violent: 'Then they cried out with a loud voice, and stopped their ears, and ran upon him with one accord.' Stopping their ears was a symbolic act of rejecting what they considered blasphemy (Leviticus 24:16). Their unified, frenzied attack ('ran upon him with one accord') demonstrates their complete loss of control and their determination to silence Stephen, mirroring the rejection of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And cast him out of the city, and stoned him: and the witnesses laid down their clothes at a young man’s feet, whose name was Saul.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐκβαλόντες ἔξω τῆς πόλεως ἐλιθοβόλουν. καὶ οἱ μάρτυρες ἀπέθεντο τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτῶν παρὰ τοὺς πόδας νεανίου καλουμένου Σαύλου.
verses.transliteration: kai ekbalontes exō tēs poleōs elithoboloun. kai hoi martyres apethento ta himatia autōn para tous podas neaniou kaloumenou Saoulou.
verses.study_note: They 'cast him out of the city, and stoned him.' Stoning was the prescribed punishment for blasphemy (Leviticus 24:16). The detail that 'the witnesses laid down their clothes at a young man’s feet, whose name was Saul,' is highly significant. This introduces Saul (later Paul) into the narrative, showing his active participation and approval of Stephen's martyrdom. This moment sets the stage for Saul's dramatic conversion and future role in spreading the very faith he now persecutes.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse marks the first Christian martyrdom, demonstrating the ultimate cost of bearing witness to Christ. The detail of 'Saul' (later Paul) guarding the cloaks of the witnesses is profoundly significant. It highlights the intense opposition faced by early Christians and providentially introduces the future apostle, whose conversion would dramatically shift the trajectory of early church history, showcasing God's power to transform persecutors into fervent evangelists.

verses.kjv_text: And they stoned Stephen, calling upon God, and saying, Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλιθοβόλουν τὸν Στέφανον ἐπικαλούμενον καὶ λέγοντα, Κύριε Ἰησοῦ, δέξαι τὸ πνεῦμά μου.
verses.transliteration: kai elithoboloun ton Stephanon epikaloumenon kai legonta, Kyrie Iēsou, dexai to pneuma mou.
verses.study_note: As they stoned him, Stephen called upon God, saying, 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.' This echoes Jesus' own words on the cross (Luke 23:46). Stephen's direct prayer to Jesus demonstrates his profound faith in Christ's divinity and His ability to receive his spirit. This act of worship, directed to Jesus, further solidified the early church's understanding of Jesus' divine nature and His role as the exalted Lord.
verses.doctrinal_note: Stephen's dying prayer, 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit,' is a profound theological statement. It demonstrates his unwavering faith in Jesus' divinity and His authority to receive the spirits of the departed. This direct invocation of Jesus as Lord, mirroring Christ's own words on the cross, serves as a powerful testament to the early church's understanding of Jesus as God and the object of worship, even in the face of death.

verses.kjv_text: And he kneeled down, and cried with a loud voice, Lord, lay not this sin to their charge. And when he had said this, he fell asleep.
verses.original_text: θεὶς δὲ τὰ γόνατα ἔκραξεν φωνῇ μεγάλῃ, Κύριε, μὴ στήσῃς αὐτοῖς ταύτην τὴν ἁμαρτίαν. καὶ τοῦτο εἰπὼν ἐκοιμήθη.
verses.transliteration: theis de ta gonata ekraxen phōnē megalē, Kyrie, mē stēsēis autois tautēn tēn hamartian. kai touto eipōn ekoimēthē.
verses.study_note: Stephen's final words, 'Lord, lay not this sin to their charge,' spoken while kneeling, echo Jesus' prayer for His persecutors (Luke 23:34). This act of forgiveness in the face of brutal violence exemplifies Christ-like love and grace. The phrase 'he fell asleep' (ἐκοιμήθη) is a common biblical euphemism for death, particularly for believers, signifying peace and the hope of resurrection. Stephen's martyrdom, marked by Christ-like forgiveness, became a powerful witness.
verses.doctrinal_note: Stephen's final act of forgiveness, 'Lord, lay not this sin to their charge,' mirrors Christ's own prayer on the cross (Luke 23:34) and exemplifies the transformative power of the Gospel. It demonstrates the profound love and grace that characterize true discipleship, even in the face of extreme persecution. His peaceful 'falling asleep' underscores the Christian hope in resurrection and the ultimate triumph over death through Christ.

2.meaning: From 'dokeō' (to think, seem), meaning 'glory,' 'splendor,' 'honor,' or 'majesty.' In this context, 'The God of glory' refers to God's inherent majesty and radiant presence, emphasizing His transcendent nature and the awe-inspiring manifestation of His being. It signifies His divine essence and the visible manifestation of His power and holiness.

Aorist passive of 'horaō' (to see), meaning 'he appeared' or 'he was seen.' It denotes a divine manifestation or revelation, often implying a supernatural encounter. Stephen uses it to highlight God's initiative in revealing Himself to Abraham, underscoring that God actively intervenes in human history through visible manifestations of His presence.

From 'patēr' (father), meaning 'father' or 'ancestor.' Here, 'our father Abraham' emphasizes the foundational role of Abraham in Israel's lineage and covenant history. Stephen uses this term to connect with his Jewish audience, affirming their shared heritage while subtly reinterpreting its significance in light of God's broader plan.

8.meaning: From 'diatithēmi' (to arrange, dispose), meaning 'covenant,' 'testament,' or 'agreement.' It refers to a solemn, binding agreement, especially one initiated by God with humanity. The 'covenant of circumcision' was a physical sign of God's promise to Abraham and his descendants, marking them as His chosen people and signifying a relationship of mutual obligation and promise.

From 'peritemnō' (to cut around), meaning 'circumcision.' This physical ritual was the sign of the Abrahamic covenant, symbolizing the cutting away of the flesh and dedication to God. Stephen's mention acknowledges its historical importance while later contrasting it with the 'circumcision of the heart' (v. 51), highlighting a shift from external ritual to internal spiritual reality.

From 'patria' (family, lineage) and 'archō' (to rule), meaning 'patriarch' or 'head of a family/tribe.' It refers to the twelve sons of Jacob, who were the progenitors of the twelve tribes of Israel. Stephen uses this term to trace the lineage of God's chosen people, emphasizing their foundational role in Israel's history and the continuity of God's covenant promises through them.

35.meaning: Aorist middle of 'arneomai' (to deny, reject, refuse). It signifies a deliberate act of disowning or repudiating. Stephen uses this to highlight Israel's historical pattern of rejecting God's chosen leaders, specifically Moses, despite his divine appointment. This foreshadows their rejection of Jesus, the ultimate deliverer.

From 'archō' (to rule), meaning 'ruler,' 'leader,' or 'magistrate.' It denotes one who holds authority and exercises governance. Stephen emphasizes that God sent Moses as a divinely appointed 'ruler,' contrasting with the people's question, 'Who made thee a ruler?' This underscores God's sovereignty in appointing leaders, regardless of human acceptance.

From 'lytroō' (to redeem, set free), meaning 'redeemer' or 'deliverer.' It refers to one who pays a ransom or liberates from bondage. Stephen highlights Moses' role as God's instrument for delivering Israel from Egyptian slavery. This term is highly significant as it prefigures Jesus Christ as the ultimate 'Redeemer' from sin and death.

38.meaning: From 'ekkaléō' (to call out), meaning 'assembly,' 'congregation,' or 'church.' Here, it refers to the assembly of Israel in the wilderness, emphasizing their corporate identity as God's called-out people. Stephen's use of this term suggests a continuity between Old Testament Israel and the New Testament church, highlighting God's consistent purpose for His people.

Plural of 'logion' (a divine utterance, oracle). It refers to the sacred utterances or revelations of God, specifically the Law given to Moses at Sinai. Stephen describes them as 'lively oracles' (λόγια ζῶντα), emphasizing their divine origin, life-giving power, and enduring truth, which were meant to guide and sustain God's people.

Present participle of 'zaō' (to live), meaning 'living,' 'alive,' or 'life-giving.' When applied to 'oracles' (λόγια), it emphasizes their dynamic, active, and vital nature. The Law given through Moses was not merely a set of rules but a living word from God, intended to bring life and guide His people into a vibrant relationship with Him.

51.meaning: From 'sklēros' (hard) and 'trachēlos' (neck), meaning 'stiffnecked' or 'hard-necked.' This is an Old Testament idiom (e.g., Ex 32:9) describing stubbornness, obstinacy, and resistance to God's will. Stephen uses it to accuse his audience of their persistent rebellion, mirroring their ancestors' refusal to submit to divine authority.

From 'a' (not) and 'peritemnō' (to circumcise), meaning 'uncircumcised.' While physically circumcised, Stephen uses it metaphorically to mean 'uncircumcised in heart and ears,' signifying spiritual insensitivity, deafness to God's word, and an unrepentant heart. It implies a lack of true spiritual separation and dedication to God, despite outward religious observance.

From 'anti' (against) and 'piptō' (to fall), meaning 'to fall against,' 'to resist,' or 'to oppose.' Stephen uses this to accuse his audience of perpetually 'resist[ing] the Holy Ghost.' It denotes active, willful opposition to divine influence and truth, highlighting a continuous pattern of rebellion against God's Spirit and His messengers throughout Israel's history.

From 'pneō' (to breathe), meaning 'spirit,' 'wind,' or 'breath.' Here, 'the Holy Ghost' (τῷ Πνεύματι τῷ Ἁγίῳ) refers to the third person of the Trinity, the divine Spirit of God. Stephen's accusation that they 'always resist the Holy Ghost' underscores that their opposition to God's truth and messengers is ultimately opposition to God Himself.

52.meaning: From 'pro' (before) and 'phēmi' (to speak), meaning 'prophet' or 'one who speaks forth.' It refers to God's messengers who declared His will, often foretelling future events. Stephen highlights Israel's historical persecution of these prophets, establishing a pattern of rejecting divine revelation and those who delivered it, culminating in the rejection of Christ.

From 'dikē' (right, justice), meaning 'just,' 'righteous,' or 'innocent.' Here, 'the Just One' (τοῦ Δικαίου) is a messianic title for Jesus Christ, emphasizing His perfect righteousness and blamelessness. Stephen uses this title to underscore the profound injustice of His betrayal and murder, contrasting His perfect character with the unrighteousness of His persecutors.

From 'prodidōmi' (to give over, betray), meaning 'betrayers' or 'traitors.' Stephen directly accuses his audience of being 'betrayers' of the Just One, Jesus. This term highlights the act of handing over someone to an enemy, emphasizing the treachery involved in their role in Christ's crucifixion, aligning them with Judas Iscariot.

From 'phonos' (murder), meaning 'murderers.' Stephen's accusation that they are 'murderers' of the Just One is a direct and severe charge, holding them responsible for Christ's death. This term underscores the gravity of their sin and their active participation in the ultimate act of rejecting God's Son.

55.meaning: From 'plēroō' (to fill), meaning 'full,' 'complete,' or 'abundant.' Stephen is described as 'full of the Holy Ghost,' indicating a complete saturation and empowerment by the Spirit. This state of spiritual fullness enabled him to see the heavenly vision and speak with divine authority, contrasting with the spiritual emptiness of his accusers.

From 'a' (intensive) and 'teinō' (to stretch), meaning 'to gaze intently,' 'to fix one's eyes upon,' or 'to stare.' Stephen's 'stedfastly' looking into heaven signifies focused attention and spiritual perception, enabled by the Holy Spirit, allowing him to perceive the divine glory and the exalted Christ, unseen by his earthly persecutors.

Perfect active participle of 'histēmi' (to stand), meaning 'standing.' Jesus is typically depicted as 'seated' at God's right hand (Heb 10:12). His 'standing' here is often interpreted as a posture of readiness to receive Stephen, to intercede, or to act as judge. It conveys an active, dynamic presence rather than a static, passive one, offering comfort and assurance to the dying martyr.

From 'dexios' (right), meaning 'right hand' or 'right side.' In biblical and ancient cultures, the right hand symbolizes power, authority, honor, and favor. Jesus 'standing on the right hand of God' signifies His exalted position, sharing in God's divine authority and majesty, confirming His supreme sovereignty and divine status.

meta.overview: Acts Chapter 8 marks a pivotal expansion of the early Christian church beyond Jerusalem, driven by intense persecution following Stephen's martyrdom. The scattering of believers, particularly Philip's ministry in Samaria, demonstrates the gospel's power to transcend ethnic and religious barriers, fulfilling Jesus' commission in Acts 1:8. The chapter introduces Simon the Sorcerer, whose attempt to purchase spiritual power highlights the sacred and unpurchasable nature of God's gifts, serving as a cautionary tale against 'simony.' The subsequent narrative of Philip and the Ethiopian eunuch illustrates the Spirit-led, personal evangelism that brings the gospel to the Gentiles, emphasizing the importance of understanding scripture and the immediate call to baptism for new believers. This chapter underscores the Holy Spirit's role in guiding missionary efforts and confirming new converts, while also showcasing the church's resilience and adaptability in the face of adversity.
meta.theme: The Gospel Spreads: Persecution, Samaria, and the Ethiopian Eunuch
meta.keyWordMeaning: Derived from 'pneō' (to breathe, blow), 'Pneuma' refers to spirit, breath, or wind. In Acts 8, it predominantly signifies the Holy Spirit, the divine agent empowering evangelism (vv. 29, 39), confirming believers (vv. 15-17), and guiding the church's mission. It underscores the Spirit's active role in the expansion of God's kingdom.
outline: Section 1 (vv. 1-4): Persecution in Jerusalem and the Scattering of Believers
Section 2 (vv. 5-13): Philip's Ministry in Samaria and Simon the Sorcerer's Conversion
Section 3 (vv. 14-25): Peter and John Confirm Samaritan Believers; Simon's Sin of Simony
Section 4 (vv. 26-35): Philip is Led to the Ethiopian Eunuch and Explains Isaiah
Section 5 (vv. 36-38): The Eunuch's Confession and Baptism
Section 6 (vv. 39-40): Philip's Supernatural Departure and Continued Ministry

verses.kjv_text: And Saul was consenting unto his death. And at that time there was a great persecution against the church which was at Jerusalem; and they were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judaea and Samaria, except the apostles.
verses.original_text: Σαῦλος δὲ ἦν συνευδοκῶν τῇ ἀναιρέσει αὐτοῦ. Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ διωγμὸς μέγας ἐπὶ τὴν ἐκκλησίαν τὴν ἐν Ἱεροσολύμοις, πάντες τε διεσπάρησαν κατὰ τὰς χώρας τῆς Ἰουδαίας καὶ Σαμαρείας πλὴν τῶν ἀποστόλων.
verses.transliteration: Saulos de ēn syneudokōn tē anairesei autou. Egeneto de en ekeinē tē hēmerā diōgmos megas epi tēn ekklēsian tēn en Hierosolymois, pantes te diesparēsan kata tas chōras tēs Ioudaias kai Samareias plēn tōn apostolōn.
verses.study_note: This verse immediately links Saul (later Paul) to Stephen's death, highlighting his zealous opposition to the nascent church. The 'great persecution' (διωγμὸς μέγας) served as a catalyst, scattering believers from Jerusalem. This scattering, though intended to suppress the church, paradoxically fulfilled Jesus' command to spread the gospel beyond Jerusalem (Acts 1:8). The apostles, however, remained in Jerusalem, likely to provide leadership and stability during this turbulent time, demonstrating their commitment to the foundational community.
verses.doctrinal_note: Persecution, though painful, often serves as a divine instrument for the expansion of the gospel. The scattering of believers in Acts 8:1 illustrates how God can use adverse circumstances to fulfill His missionary purposes, demonstrating His sovereignty over human opposition and the resilience of His church.

verses.kjv_text: And devout men carried Stephen to his burial, and made great lamentation over him.
verses.original_text: συνεκόμισαν δὲ τὸν Στέφανον ἄνδρες εὐλαβεῖς καὶ ἐποιήσαντο κοπετὸν μέγαν ἐπ’ αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: Synekomisan de ton Stephanon andres eulabeis kai epoiēsanto kopeton megan ep' autō.
verses.study_note: The 'devout men' (ἄνδρες εὐλαβεῖς) who buried Stephen were likely God-fearing Jews, possibly even some who had not yet fully embraced Christianity but respected Stephen. Their 'great lamentation' (κοπετὸν μέγαν) was a public display of grief, perhaps a courageous act of solidarity in the face of intense persecution, contrasting with the hostile crowd that stoned him (Acts 7:58). This act of burial was significant, as it defied the persecutors and honored a martyr.

verses.kjv_text: As for Saul, he made havock of the church, entering into every house, and haling men and women committed them to prison.
verses.original_text: Σαῦλος δὲ ἐλυμαίνετο τὴν ἐκκλησίαν, κατὰ τοὺς οἴκους εἰσπορευόμενος σύρων τε ἄνδρας καὶ γυναῖκας παρεδίδου εἰς φυλακήν.
verses.transliteration: Saulos de elymaineto tēn ekklēsian, kata tous oikous eisporeuomenos syrōn te andras kai gynaikas paredidou eis phylakēn.
verses.study_note: Saul's relentless 'havock' (ἐλυμαίνετο, meaning 'to ravage' or 'destroy') against the church is vividly described. He actively sought out believers in their homes, dragging both men and women to prison. This detail underscores the severity of the persecution and Saul's personal zeal in attempting to eradicate the Christian movement. His actions here set the stage for his dramatic conversion later (Acts 9:1-19), highlighting the profound transformation God wrought in him.
verses.doctrinal_note: The intense persecution faced by the early church, spearheaded by Saul, demonstrates the spiritual warfare inherent in the expansion of God's kingdom. Yet, even in such hostility, God's plan for the church's growth and the conversion of its fiercest opponents (like Saul) remains sovereign.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore they that were scattered abroad went every where preaching the word.
verses.original_text: οἱ μὲν οὖν διασπαρέντες διῆλθον εὐαγγελιζόμενοι τὸν λόγον.
verses.transliteration: Hoi men oun diasparentes diēlthon euangelizomenoi ton logon.
verses.study_note: Despite the persecution, the scattered believers did not retreat in fear but actively 'went everywhere preaching the word' (εὐαγγελιζόμενοι τὸν λόγον). This verse highlights the organic, grassroots nature of early Christian evangelism. It demonstrates that the responsibility of sharing the gospel was not limited to the apostles but was embraced by ordinary believers, turning a moment of crisis into an opportunity for widespread proclamation of Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: The scattering of believers led to the widespread dissemination of the gospel, illustrating that God can use even persecution to advance His kingdom. Every believer, not just apostles, is called to share the 'word' (λόγον), demonstrating the universal priesthood and evangelistic mandate of the church.

verses.kjv_text: Then Philip went down to the city of Samaria, and preached Christ unto them.
verses.original_text: Φίλιππος δὲ κατελθὼν εἰς πόλιν τῆς Σαμαρείας ἐκήρυσσεν αὐτοῖς τὸν Χριστόν.
verses.transliteration: Philippos de katelthōn eis polin tēs Samareias ekēryssen autois ton Christon.
verses.study_note: Philip, one of the seven deacons appointed in Acts 6:5, is now seen as an evangelist. His journey 'down to the city of Samaria' is significant, as Jews typically avoided Samaritans due to long-standing ethnic and religious animosity (John 4:9). Philip's willingness to preach 'Christ' (τὸν Χριστόν) to them marks a crucial step in the gospel's expansion beyond Jewish boundaries, fulfilling Jesus' prophecy in Acts 1:8.
verses.doctrinal_note: Philip's ministry in Samaria demonstrates the breaking down of ethnic and social barriers through the gospel. Christ's message is universal, extending salvation to all peoples, including those historically marginalized or considered 'outsiders' by the dominant religious group.

verses.kjv_text: And the people with one accord gave heed unto those things which Philip spake, hearing and seeing the miracles which he did.
verses.original_text: προσεῖχόν τε οἱ ὄχλοι τοῖς λεγομένοις ὑπὸ τοῦ Φιλίππου ὁμοθυμαδὸν ἐν τῷ ἀκούειν αὐτοὺς καὶ βλέπειν τὰ σημεῖα ἃ ἐποίει.
verses.transliteration: Proseichon te hoi ochloi tois legomenois hypo tou Philippou homothymadon en tō akouein autous kai blepein ta sēmeia ha epoiei.
verses.study_note: The Samaritans 'with one accord' (ὁμοθυμαδὸν) gave heed to Philip, indicating a remarkable unity and openness to his message. Their belief was not solely based on his words but was powerfully confirmed by the 'miracles' (σημεῖα) he performed. These signs and wonders, such as healings and exorcisms, served as divine authentication of Philip's message, demonstrating God's power at work and preparing their hearts for the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: For unclean spirits, crying with loud voice, came out of many that were possessed with them: and many taken with palsies, and that were lame, were healed.
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ γὰρ τῶν ἐχόντων πνεύματα ἀκάθαρτα βοῶντα φωνῇ μεγάλῃ ἐξήρχοντο, πολλοὶ δὲ παραλελυμένοι καὶ χωλοὶ ἐθεραπεύθησαν.
verses.transliteration: Polloi gar tōn echontōn pneumata akatharta boōnta phōnē megalē exērchonto, polloi de paralelymenoi kai chōloi etherapeuthēsan.
verses.study_note: This verse details the specific miracles Philip performed: the expulsion of 'unclean spirits' (πνεύματα ἀκάθαρτα) with loud cries, and the healing of those afflicted with 'palsies' (παραλελυμένοι) and the 'lame' (χωλοί). These acts of power directly confronted the spiritual darkness and physical suffering prevalent in the region, demonstrating Christ's authority over evil and disease, and validating Philip's proclamation of the kingdom of God.

verses.kjv_text: And there was great joy in that city.
verses.original_text: ἐγένετο δὲ πολλὴ χαρὰ ἐν τῇ πόλει ἐκείνῃ.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de pollē chara en tē polei ekeinē.
verses.study_note: The immediate result of Philip's ministry and the accompanying miracles was 'great joy' (πολλὴ χαρὰ) in the city. This joy was a natural response to liberation from spiritual oppression, physical suffering, and the reception of the good news of Christ. It reflects the transformative power of the gospel, bringing hope and gladness where there was once despair, echoing the joy anticipated by the prophets (Isa 9:3).
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'great joy' in Samaria signifies the profound spiritual and physical liberation brought by the gospel. True joy is a fruit of the Spirit, accompanying salvation and the manifestation of God's power, demonstrating the transformative impact of Christ's kingdom.

verses.kjv_text: But there was a certain man, called Simon, which beforetime in the same city used sorcery, and bewitched the people of Samaria, giving out that himself was some great one:
verses.original_text: ἀνὴρ δέ τις ὀνόματι Σίμων προϋπῆρχεν ἐν τῇ πόλει μαγεύων καὶ ἐξιστάνων τὸ ἔθνος τῆς Σαμαρείας, λέγων εἶναί τινα ἑαυτὸν μέγαν.
verses.transliteration: Anēr de tis onomati Simōn proÿpērchen en tē polei mageuōn kai existanōn to ethnos tēs Samareias, legōn einai tina heauton megan.
verses.study_note: Simon, a prominent figure in Samaria, was known for 'sorcery' (μαγεύων), which involved magical practices and occult arts. He 'bewitched' (ἐξιστάνων, meaning 'to amaze' or 'astound') the people, claiming to be 'some great one' (τινα ἑαυτὸν μέγαν). This sets up a direct contrast between his deceptive power and the genuine, divine power demonstrated by Philip, highlighting the spiritual battle for the hearts and minds of the Samaritans.

verses.kjv_text: To whom they all gave heed, from the least to the greatest, saying, This man is the great power of God.
verses.original_text: ᾧ προσεῖχον πάντες ἀπὸ μικροῦ ἕως μεγάλου λέγοντες, Οὗτός ἐστιν ἡ δύναμις τοῦ Θεοῦ ἡ καλουμένη Μεγάλη.
verses.transliteration: Hō proseichon pantes apo mikrou heōs megalou legontes, Houtos estin hē dynamis tou Theou hē kaloumenē Megalē.
verses.study_note: Simon had captivated 'all' (πάντες), from the least to the greatest, who proclaimed him to be 'the great power of God' (ἡ δύναμις τοῦ Θεοῦ ἡ καλουμένη Μεγάλη). This title suggests a belief that Simon embodied a divine attribute or was a manifestation of God's power, reflecting a syncretistic religious environment where divine power was sought through various means. This widespread influence makes his eventual encounter with Philip and the apostles even more significant.

verses.kjv_text: And to him they had regard, because that of long time he had bewitched them with sorceries.
verses.original_text: προσεῖχον δὲ αὐτῷ διὰ τὸ ἱκανῷ χρόνῳ ταῖς μαγείαις αὐτοῦ ἐξεστακέναι αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: Proseichon de autō dia to hikanō chronō tais mageiais autou exestakenai autous.
verses.study_note: The Samaritans' long-standing regard for Simon was due to his prolonged use of 'sorceries' (μαγείαις), which had 'bewitched' (ἐξεστακέναι, 'astounded' or 'amazed') them. This highlights the deep spiritual deception and influence Simon had established over the community. The people were accustomed to his displays of power, making Philip's authentic miracles a direct challenge to Simon's authority and the spiritual climate of the city.

verses.kjv_text: But when they believed Philip preaching the things concerning the kingdom of God, and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women.
verses.original_text: ὅτε δὲ ἐπίστευσαν τῷ Φιλίππῳ εὐαγγελιζομένῳ περὶ τῆς βασιλείας τοῦ Θεοῦ καὶ τοῦ ὀνόματος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ, ἐβαπτίζοντο ἄνδρες τε καὶ γυναῖκες.
verses.transliteration: Hote de episteusan tō Philippō euangelizomenō peri tēs basileias tou Theou kai tou onomatos Iēsou Christou, ebaptizonto andres te kai gynaikes.
verses.study_note: The turning point comes when the Samaritans 'believed Philip's preaching' (ἐπίστευσαν τῷ Φιλίππῳ εὐαγγελιζομένῳ) concerning 'the kingdom of God' (τῆς βασιλείας τοῦ Θεοῦ) and 'the name of Jesus Christ' (τοῦ ὀνόματος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ). This belief led directly to their baptism, signifying their public confession of faith and entrance into the Christian community. The inclusion of both 'men and women' (ἄνδρες τε καὶ γυναῖκες) underscores the inclusive nature of the gospel.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse links belief in the 'kingdom of God' and 'Jesus Christ' directly to baptism, establishing a pattern for conversion. Baptism is presented as a public declaration of faith, symbolizing identification with Christ and entrance into His covenant community, available to both men and women.

verses.kjv_text: Then Simon himself believed also: and when he was baptized, he continued with Philip, and wondered, beholding the miracles and signs which were done.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Σίμων καὶ αὐτὸς ἐπίστευσεν, καὶ βαπτισθεὶς ἦν προσκαρτερῶν τῷ Φιλίππῳ, θεωρῶν τε δυνάμεις καὶ σημεῖα γινόμενα ἐξίστατο.
verses.transliteration: Ho de Simōn kai autos episteusen, kai baptistheis ēn proskarterōn tō Philippō, theorōn te dynameis kai sēmeia ginomena existato.
verses.study_note: Even Simon 'believed also' (καὶ αὐτὸς ἐπίστευσεν) and was baptized. His belief, however, appears to be superficial, driven more by amazement at Philip's superior 'miracles and signs' (δυνάμεις καὶ σημεῖα) than by genuine repentance and faith in Christ. He 'continued with Philip' (ἦν προσκαρτερῶν τῷ Φιλίππῳ) and was 'wondering' (ἐξίστατο), suggesting a fascination with the power rather than a transformation of heart. This sets the stage for his later spiritual failure.

verses.kjv_text: Now when the apostles which were at Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent unto them Peter and John:
verses.original_text: ἀκούσαντες δὲ οἱ ἐν Ἱεροσολύμοις ἀπόστολοι ὅτι δέδεκται ἡ Σαμάρεια τὸν λόγον τοῦ Θεοῦ, ἀπέστειλαν πρὸς αὐτοὺς Πέτρον καὶ Ἰωάννην.
verses.transliteration: Akousantes de hoi en Hierosolymois apostoloi hoti dedektai hē Samareia ton logon tou Theou, apesteilan pros autous Petron kai Iōannēn.
verses.study_note: Upon hearing that Samaria had 'received the word of God' (δέδεκται τὸν λόγον τοῦ Θεοῦ), the apostles in Jerusalem dispatched Peter and John. This action signifies the Jerusalem church's oversight and validation of the expanding mission. Sending two prominent apostles ensured doctrinal continuity and provided apostolic authority to confirm the new Samaritan believers, integrating them into the broader Christian community.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sending of Peter and John to Samaria highlights the importance of apostolic oversight and unity within the early church. It ensured that the new converts were properly integrated into the broader body of Christ and received the full spiritual blessings, including the Holy Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: Who, when they were come down, prayed for them, that they might receive the Holy Ghost:
verses.original_text: οἵτινες καταβάντες προσηύξαντο περὶ αὐτῶν ὅπως λάβωσιν Πνεῦμα Ἅγιον.
verses.transliteration: Hoitines katabantes prosēuxanto peri autōn hopōs labōsin Pneuma Hagion.
verses.study_note: Upon their arrival, Peter and John 'prayed for them' (προσηύξαντο περὶ αὐτῶν) specifically 'that they might receive the Holy Ghost' (ὅπως λάβωσιν Πνεῦμα Ἅγιον). This indicates that while the Samaritans had believed and been baptized, they had not yet experienced the manifest indwelling or empowering of the Holy Spirit in the same way as the Jewish believers at Pentecost (Acts 2:1-4). This sequence emphasizes the distinct roles of initial conversion/baptism and the reception of the Spirit.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse introduces a significant theological point: the reception of the Holy Spirit, distinct from initial belief and water baptism, often accompanied by apostolic laying on of hands. It underscores the Spirit's role in empowering believers and confirming their inclusion in the new covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: (For as yet he was fallen upon none of them: only they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.)
verses.original_text: οὐδέπω γὰρ ἦν ἐπ’ οὐδένα αὐτῶν πεπτωκός, μόνον δὲ βεβαπτισμένοι ὑπῆρχον εἰς τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ Κυρίου Ἰησοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Oudepō gar ēn ep' oudena autōn peptōkos, monon de bebaptismenoi hypērchon eis to onoma tou Kyriou Iēsou.
verses.study_note: This parenthetical explanation clarifies that 'as yet he was fallen upon none of them' (οὐδέπω γὰρ ἦν ἐπ’ οὐδένα αὐτῶν πεπτωκός), meaning the Holy Spirit had not yet come upon them in a discernible, empowering way. They had only been 'baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus' (βεβαπτισμένοι ὑπῆρχον εἰς τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ Κυρίου Ἰησοῦ). This passage is crucial for understanding the relationship between water baptism and the reception of the Holy Spirit in the early church, suggesting that sometimes these experiences were distinct in timing, especially in transitional periods.
verses.doctrinal_note: The distinction between water baptism and the reception of the Holy Spirit is evident here. While they were baptized into Christ, the Spirit had not yet 'fallen upon' them, indicating a subsequent, empowering experience often mediated through apostolic prayer and laying on of hands, signifying full inclusion and spiritual endowment.

verses.kjv_text: Then laid they their hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost.
verses.original_text: τότε ἐπετίθεσαν τὰς χεῖρας ἐπ’ αὐτοὺς καὶ ἐλάμβανον Πνεῦμα Ἅγιον.
verses.transliteration: Tote epetithesan tas cheiras ep' autous kai elambanon Pneuma Hagion.
verses.study_note: Following their prayer, Peter and John 'laid their hands on them' (ἐπετίθεσαν τὰς χεῖρας ἐπ’ αὐτοὺς), and then 'they received the Holy Ghost' (ἐλάμβανον Πνεῦμα Ἅγιον). The laying on of hands was a common practice signifying blessing, commissioning, or the impartation of spiritual gifts. In this context, it served as the visible means through which the Holy Spirit was bestowed upon the Samaritan believers, confirming their full inclusion into the body of Christ and validating the gospel's expansion to this new group.
verses.doctrinal_note: The laying on of hands by the apostles facilitated the reception of the Holy Spirit, demonstrating the Spirit's power and the apostles' authority in confirming new believers. This act symbolized the impartation of divine grace and spiritual empowerment, integrating the Samaritans fully into the Christian community.

verses.kjv_text: And when Simon saw that through laying on of the apostles’ hands the Holy Ghost was given, he offered them money,
verses.original_text: ἰδὼν δὲ ὁ Σίμων ὅτι διὰ τῆς ἐπιθέσεως τῶν χειρῶν τῶν ἀποστόλων δίδοται τὸ Πνεῦμα, προσήνεγκεν αὐτοῖς χρήματα,
verses.transliteration: Idōn de ho Simōn hoti dia tēs epitheseōs tōn cheirōn tōn apostolōn didotai to Pneuma, prosēnenken autois chrēmata,
verses.study_note: Simon, observing that the Holy Spirit 'was given' (δίδοται τὸ Πνεῦμα) through the 'laying on of the apostles’ hands' (διὰ τῆς ἐπιθέσεως τῶν χειρῶν τῶν ἀποστόλων), offered them 'money' (χρήματα). This reveals his continued misunderstanding of spiritual power. He perceived the Holy Spirit as a commodity or a magical ability that could be bought and sold, rather than a divine gift freely given by God. This incident gives rise to the term 'simony,' referring to the buying or selling of ecclesiastical privileges.
verses.doctrinal_note: Simon's attempt to purchase the Holy Spirit reveals a profound spiritual misunderstanding: God's gifts are not commodities to be bought or sold. This act, known as 'simony,' underscores the sacred, unmerited nature of divine grace and the danger of approaching spiritual power with worldly motives.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, Give me also this power, that on whomsoever I lay hands, he may receive the Holy Ghost.
verses.original_text: λέγων, Δότε κἀμοὶ τὴν ἐξουσίαν ταύτην, ἵνα ᾧ ἐὰν ἐπιθῶ τὰς χεῖρας λαμβάνῃ Πνεῦμα Ἅγιον.
verses.transliteration: Legōn, Dote kamoi tēn exousian tautēn, hina hō ean epithō tas cheiras lambanē Pneuma Hagion.
verses.study_note: Simon explicitly requested, 'Give me also this power' (Δότε κἀμοὶ τὴν ἐξουσίαν ταύτην), so that he too could impart the Holy Spirit through the laying on of hands. His desire was not for spiritual transformation or genuine ministry but for the 'authority' (ἐξουσίαν) and prestige associated with such a miraculous ability. This confirms his self-centered motives and his failure to grasp the true nature of God's Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: But Peter said unto him, Thy money perish with thee, because thou hast thought that the gift of God may be purchased with money.
verses.original_text: Πέτρος δὲ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτόν, Τὸ ἀργύριόν σου σὺν σοὶ εἴη εἰς ἀπώλειαν, ὅτι τὴν δωρεὰν τοῦ Θεοῦ ἐνόμισας διὰ χρημάτων κτᾶσθαι.
verses.transliteration: Petros de eipen pros auton, To argyrion sou syn soi eiē eis apōleian, hoti tēn dōrean tou Theou enomisas dia chrēmatōn ktasthai.
verses.study_note: Peter's response to Simon was sharp and unequivocal: 'Thy money perish with thee' (Τὸ ἀργύριόν σου σὺν σοὶ εἴη εἰς ἀπώλειαν). This severe rebuke highlights the gravity of Simon's sin – attempting to 'purchase' (κτᾶσθαι) 'the gift of God' (τὴν δωρεὰν τοῦ Θεοῦ) with money. Peter's words emphasize that divine grace and spiritual power are not commodities to be bought or sold, but are freely given by God, and any attempt to commercialize them is an affront to His holiness.
verses.doctrinal_note: Peter's condemnation of Simon's attempt to buy spiritual power ('simony') powerfully asserts that God's gifts are unpurchasable. Salvation, the Holy Spirit, and spiritual authority are freely given by grace, not earned or acquired through monetary means, emphasizing the sanctity and sovereignty of divine bestowal.

verses.kjv_text: Thou hast neither part nor lot in this matter: for thy heart is not right in the sight of God.
verses.original_text: οὐκ ἔστιν σοι μερὶς οὐδὲ κλῆρος ἐν τῷ λόγῳ τούτῳ, ἡ γὰρ καρδία σου οὐκ ἔστιν εὐθεῖα ἔναντι τοῦ Θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Ouk estin soi meris oude klēros en tō logō toutō, hē gar kardia sou ouk estin eutheia enanti tou Theou.
verses.study_note: Peter declares that Simon has 'neither part nor lot' (οὐκ ἔστιν σοι μερὶς οὐδὲ κλῆρος) in this spiritual matter, indicating his exclusion from genuine spiritual inheritance. The reason given is profound: 'thy heart is not right in the sight of God' (ἡ γὰρ καρδία σου οὐκ ἔστιν εὐθεῖα ἔναντι τοῦ Θεοῦ). This exposes the superficiality of Simon's earlier 'belief' and baptism, revealing that his inner motives were corrupt and unaligned with God's will, despite outward religious acts.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse underscores the critical importance of a 'right heart' before God for genuine spiritual participation. Outward religious acts like baptism or professed belief are insufficient if the heart remains unrepentant and motivated by selfish desires, revealing that true faith involves inner transformation.

verses.kjv_text: Repent therefore of this thy wickedness, and pray God, if perhaps the thought of thine heart may be forgiven thee.
verses.original_text: μετανόησον οὖν ἀπὸ τῆς κακίας σου ταύτης, καὶ δεήθητι τοῦ Κυρίου εἰ ἄρα γε ἀφεθήσεταί σοι ἡ ἐπίνοια τῆς καρδίας σου.
verses.transliteration: Metanoēson oun apo tēs kakias sou tautēs, kai deēthēti tou Kyriou ei ara ge aphethēsetai soi hē epinoia tēs kardias sou.
verses.study_note: Despite the harsh rebuke, Peter offers Simon a path to repentance: 'Repent therefore of this thy wickedness' (μετανόησον οὖν ἀπὸ τῆς κακίας σου ταύτης) and 'pray God' (δεήθητι τοῦ Κυρίου). The phrase 'if perhaps' (εἰ ἄρα γε) suggests the gravity of Simon's sin and the uncertainty of forgiveness for such a profound spiritual offense, emphasizing the need for genuine, heartfelt repentance for his 'thought of thine heart' (ἡ ἐπίνοια τῆς καρδίας σου).
verses.doctrinal_note: Peter's call to repentance, even for a grave spiritual sin, highlights God's offer of forgiveness. However, the 'if perhaps' clause underscores the seriousness of Simon's heart condition, emphasizing that true repentance must be profound and sincere for forgiveness to be granted.

verses.kjv_text: For I perceive that thou art in the gall of bitterness, and in the bond of iniquity.
verses.original_text: εἰς γὰρ χολὴν πικρίας καὶ σύνδεσμον ἀδικίας ὁρῶ σε ὄντα.
verses.transliteration: Eis gar cholēn pikrias kai syndesmon adikias horō se onta.
verses.study_note: Peter's spiritual discernment reveals the depth of Simon's corruption: 'For I perceive that thou art in the gall of bitterness, and in the bond of iniquity' (εἰς γὰρ χολὴν πικρίας καὶ σύνδεσμον ἀδικίας ὁρῶ σε ὄντα). 'Gall of bitterness' (χολὴν πικρίας) is an idiom for deep-seated malice or extreme wickedness, possibly referencing Deuteronomy 29:18. 'Bond of iniquity' (σύνδεσμον ἀδικίας) signifies being enslaved by sin. These powerful metaphors illustrate Simon's spiritual state as one of profound moral corruption and bondage.
verses.doctrinal_note: Peter's prophetic insight into Simon's heart reveals the spiritual reality of being consumed by 'gall of bitterness' and 'bond of iniquity.' This emphasizes that unrepentant sin leads to spiritual enslavement and a corrupted inner state, highlighting the need for divine intervention and genuine transformation.

verses.kjv_text: Then answered Simon, and said, Pray ye to the LORD for me, that none of these things which ye have spoken come upon me.
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ Σίμων εἶπεν, Δεήθητε ὑμεῖς ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ πρὸς τὸν Κύριον, ὅπως μηδὲν ἐπέλθῃ ἐπ’ ἐμὲ τῶν εἰρημένων ὧν εἰρήκατε.
verses.transliteration: Apokritheis de ho Simōn eipen, Deēthēte hymeis hyper emou pros ton Kyrion, hopōs mēden epelthē ep' eme tōn eirēmenōn hōn eirēkate.
verses.study_note: Simon's response, 'Pray ye to the LORD for me' (Δεήθητε ὑμεῖς ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ πρὸς τὸν Κύριον), indicates fear of the consequences of Peter's words rather than genuine repentance for his sin. He desires to avoid the 'things which ye have spoken' (τῶν εἰρημένων ὧν εἰρήκατε), suggesting a concern for self-preservation from divine judgment rather than a change of heart. This further confirms the superficiality of his conversion and the unrighteousness of his motives.

verses.kjv_text: And they, when they had testified and preached the word of the Lord, returned to Jerusalem, and preached the gospel in many villages of the Samaritans.
verses.original_text: οἱ μὲν οὖν διαμαρτυράμενοι καὶ λαλήσαντες τὸν λόγον τοῦ Κυρίου ὑπέστρεφον εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα, πολλὰς δὲ κώμας τῶν Σαμαρειτῶν εὐηγγελίζοντο.
verses.transliteration: Hoi men oun diamartyramenoi kai lalēsantes ton logon tou Kyriou hypestrephon eis Hierosolyma, pollas de kōmas tōn Samareitōn euēggelizonto.
verses.study_note: After their mission in Samaria, Peter and John 'testified and preached the word of the Lord' (διαμαρτυράμενοι καὶ λαλήσαντες τὸν λόγον τοῦ Κυρίου) and returned to Jerusalem. Significantly, on their way back, they 'preached the gospel in many villages of the Samaritans' (πολλὰς δὲ κώμας τῶν Σαμαρειτῶν εὐηγγελίζοντο). This demonstrates the lasting impact of Philip's initial ministry and the apostles' commitment to spreading the gospel throughout the Samaritan region, further fulfilling the Acts 1:8 mandate.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel of the Lord spake unto Philip, saying, Arise, and go toward the south unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert.
verses.original_text: Ἄγγελος δὲ Κυρίου ἐλάλησεν πρὸς Φίλιππον λέγων, Ἀνάστηθι καὶ πορεύου κατὰ μεσημβρίαν ἐπὶ τὴν ὁδὸν τὴν καταβαίνουσαν ἀπὸ Ἱερουσαλὴμ εἰς Γάζαν, αὕτη ἐστὶν ἔρημος.
verses.transliteration: Angelos de Kyriou elalēsen pros Philippon legōn, Anastēthi kai poreuou kata mesēmbrian epi tēn hodon tēn katabainousan apo Hierousalēm eis Gazan, hautē estin erēmos.
verses.study_note: An 'angel of the Lord' (Ἄγγελος δὲ Κυρίου) supernaturally directed Philip to leave his successful ministry in Samaria and go 'toward the south unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert' (αὕτη ἐστὶν ἔρημος). This divine intervention highlights the Spirit's active guidance in missionary endeavors, leading Philip to a seemingly unproductive, desolate route for a specific, divinely appointed encounter. The mention of 'desert' emphasizes the unlikely nature of this new assignment.
verses.doctrinal_note: This divine directive to Philip illustrates the Holy Spirit's sovereign guidance in evangelism. God often leads His servants to unexpected places and encounters, demonstrating that His plans transcend human logic and that He orchestrates divine appointments for the spread of the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And he arose and went: and, behold, a man of Ethiopia, an eunuch of great authority under Candace queen of the Ethiopians, who had the charge of all her treasure, and had come to Jerusalem for to worship,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀναστὰς ἐπορεύθη. καὶ ἰδοὺ ἀνὴρ Αἰθίοψ εὐνοῦχος δυνάστης Κανδάκης βασιλίσσης Αἰθιόπων, ὃς ἦν ἐπὶ πάσης τῆς γάζης αὐτῆς, ὃς ἐληλύθει προσκυνήσων εἰς Ἱερουσαλήμ,
verses.transliteration: Kai anastas eporeuthē. Kai idou anēr Aithiops eunouchos dynastēs Kandakēs basilissēs Aithiopōn, hos ēn epi pasēs tēs gazēs autēs, hos elēlythei proskynēsōn eis Hierousalēm,
verses.study_note: Philip immediately obeyed. He encountered an 'Ethiopian eunuch' (ἀνὴρ Αἰθίοψ εὐνοῦχος), a man of 'great authority' (δυνάστης) under 'Candace queen of the Ethiopians' (Κανδάκης βασιλίσσης Αἰθιόπων), responsible for all her 'treasure' (τῆς γάζης). The eunuch had traveled to Jerusalem 'for to worship' (προσκυνήσων), indicating he was a God-fearer, possibly a proselyte to Judaism. His status as a eunuch would have excluded him from full participation in the temple (Deut 23:1), making his spiritual seeking poignant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Ethiopian eunuch, a high-ranking Gentile seeker, represents the global reach of the gospel. His journey to Jerusalem for worship, despite his physical limitations for full temple participation, highlights his spiritual hunger and God's initiative in sending Philip to meet him, demonstrating divine grace transcends social and religious barriers.

verses.kjv_text: Was returning, and sitting in his chariot read Esaias the prophet.
verses.original_text: ἦν τε ὑποστρέφων καὶ καθήμενος ἐπὶ τοῦ ἅρματος αὐτοῦ ἀνεγίνωσκεν τὸν προφήτην Ἡσαΐαν.
verses.transliteration: Ēn te hypostrephōn kai kathēmenos epi tou harmatos autou aneginōsken ton prophētēn Hēsïan.
verses.study_note: As the eunuch was 'returning' (ὑποστρέφων), he was 'sitting in his chariot' (καθήμενος ἐπὶ τοῦ ἅρματος αὐτοῦ) and 'read Esaias the prophet' (ἀνεγίνωσκεν τὸν προφήτην Ἡσαΐαν). This detail is significant: he was not only literate but also engaged with scripture, specifically the prophet Isaiah, a book rich in messianic prophecy. His diligent study, even while traveling, underscores his earnest spiritual quest and sets the perfect stage for Philip's intervention.

verses.kjv_text: Then the Spirit said unto Philip, Go near, and join thyself to this chariot.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ τὸ Πνεῦμα τῷ Φιλίππῳ, Πρόσελθε καὶ κολλήθητι τῷ ἅρματι τούτῳ.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de to Pneuma tō Philippō, Proselthe kai kollēthēti tō harmati toutō.
verses.study_note: The Holy 'Spirit' (τὸ Πνεῦμα) directly instructed Philip, 'Go near, and join thyself to this chariot' (Πρόσελθε καὶ κολλήθητι τῷ ἅρματι τούτῳ). This is another clear instance of the Spirit's immediate and specific guidance in evangelism, confirming that Philip's journey to the desert road was divinely orchestrated for this particular encounter. It emphasizes the Spirit's active role in connecting seekers with those who can share the gospel.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Spirit's direct command to Philip exemplifies divine orchestration in evangelism. God actively guides His servants to specific individuals, demonstrating that effective outreach is often a Spirit-led endeavor, connecting those who seek truth with those who bear it.

verses.kjv_text: And Philip ran thither to him, and heard him read the prophet Esaias, and said, Understandest thou what thou readest?
verses.original_text: προσδραμὼν δὲ ὁ Φίλιππος ἤκουσεν αὐτοῦ ἀναγινώσκοντος Ἡσαΐαν τὸν προφήτην, καὶ εἶπεν, Ἆρά γε γινώσκεις ἃ ἀναγινώσκεις;
verses.transliteration: Prosdramōn de ho Philippos ēkousen autou anaginōskontos Hēsïan ton prophētēn, kai eipen, Ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis?
verses.study_note: Philip 'ran thither to him' (προσδραμὼν δὲ ὁ Φίλιππος), demonstrating his eagerness to obey the Spirit. He heard the eunuch 'read the prophet Esaias' (ἀναγινώσκοντος Ἡσαΐαν τὸν προφήτην) aloud, a common practice in antiquity. Philip's direct question, 'Understandest thou what thou readest?' (Ἆρά γε γινώσκεις ἃ ἀναγινώσκεις;), was a polite yet probing invitation to engage in spiritual conversation, perfectly timed to the eunuch's current activity.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, How can I, except some man should guide me? And he desired Philip that he would come up and sit with him.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν, Πῶς γὰρ ἂν δυναίμην ἐὰν μή τις ὁδηγήσει με; παρεκάλεσέν τε τὸν Φίλιππον ἀναβάντα καθίσαι σὺν αὐτῷ.
verses.transliteration: Ho de eipen, Pōs gar an dynaimēn ean mē tis hodēgēsei me? Parekalesen te ton Philippon anabanta kathisai syn autō.
verses.study_note: The eunuch's humble reply, 'How can I, except some man should guide me?' (Πῶς γὰρ ἂν δυναίμην ἐὰν μή τις ὁδηγήσει με;), reveals his recognition of his need for interpretation and his openness to instruction. He then 'desired Philip that he would come up and sit with him' (παρεκάλεσέν τε τὸν Φίλιππον ἀναβάντα καθίσαι σὺν αὐτῷ). This invitation signifies his eagerness to learn and his trust in Philip, setting the stage for a powerful evangelistic encounter.
verses.doctrinal_note: The eunuch's admission of needing guidance highlights the necessity of human instrumentality in understanding divine revelation. While scripture is inspired, its interpretation often benefits from Spirit-led teaching, underscoring the role of teachers and the church in discipleship.

verses.kjv_text: The place of the scripture which he read was this, He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened he not his mouth:
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ περιοχὴ τῆς γραφῆς ἣν ἀνεγίνωσκεν ἦν αὕτη, Ὡς πρόβατον ἐπὶ σφαγὴν ἤχθη, καὶ ὡς ἀμνὸς ἐναντίον τοῦ κείραντος αὐτὸν ἄφωνος, οὕτως οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Hē de periochē tēs graphēs hēn aneginōsken ēn hautē, Hōs probaton epi sphagēn ēchthē, kai hōs amnos enantion tou keirantos auton aphōnos, houtōs ouk anoigei to stoma autou.
verses.study_note: The specific passage the eunuch was reading was from Isaiah 53:7-8 (Septuagint version). This section, describing a suffering servant led 'as a sheep to the slaughter' (Ὡς πρόβατον ἐπὶ σφαγὴν ἤχθη) and 'like a lamb dumb before his shearer' (ὡς ἀμνὸς ἐναντίον τοῦ κείραντος αὐτὸν ἄφωνος), is one of the most profound messianic prophecies in the Old Testament. Its focus on vicarious suffering and silent endurance perfectly prefigures Jesus Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: The eunuch's reading of Isaiah 53, a profound messianic prophecy, highlights the Old Testament's foreshadowing of Christ's atoning work. This passage, describing the suffering servant, is central to understanding Jesus' sacrificial death and His silent submission to God's will for humanity's redemption.

verses.kjv_text: In his humiliation his judgment was taken away: and who shall declare his generation? for his life is taken from the earth.
verses.original_text: ἐν τῇ ταπεινώσει αὐτοῦ ἡ κρίσις αὐτοῦ ἤρθη· τὴν γενεὰν αὐτοῦ τίς διηγήσεται; ὅτι αἴρεται ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς ἡ ζωή αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: En tē tapeinōsei autou hē krisis autou ērtē; tēn genean autou tis diēgēsetai? Hoti airetai apo tēs gēs hē zōē autou.
verses.study_note: Continuing the Isaiah 53 passage (v. 8), this verse speaks of the servant's 'humiliation' (ταπεινώσει) and how 'his judgment was taken away' (ἡ κρίσις αὐτοῦ ἤρθη). The question 'who shall declare his generation?' (τὴν γενεὰν αὐτοῦ τίς διηγήσεται;) can refer to his posterity or the injustice of his trial. The phrase 'for his life is taken from the earth' (ὅτι αἴρεται ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς ἡ ζωή αὐτοῦ) clearly points to his premature death. This passage directly sets up Philip's opportunity to explain Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And the eunuch answered Philip, and said, I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ ὁ εὐνοῦχος τῷ Φιλίππῳ εἶπεν, Δέομαί σου, περὶ τίνος ὁ προφήτης λέγει τοῦτο; περὶ ἑαυτοῦ ἢ περὶ ἑτέρου τινός;
verses.transliteration: Apokritheis de ho eunouchos tō Philippō eipen, Deomai sou, peri tinos ho prophētēs legei touto? Peri heautou ē peri heterou tinos?
verses.study_note: The eunuch's question, 'I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?' (περὶ τίνος ὁ προφήτης λέγει τοῦτο; περὶ ἑαυτοῦ ἢ περὶ ἑτέρου τινός;), is the crucial moment. It demonstrates his earnest desire for understanding and provides Philip with the perfect opening to present the gospel. This question was a common point of debate among Jewish interpreters regarding Isaiah 53, and the eunuch's inquiry shows his intellectual and spiritual engagement with the text.

verses.kjv_text: Then Philip opened his mouth, and began at the same scripture, and preached unto him Jesus.
verses.original_text: ἀνοίξας δὲ ὁ Φίλιππος τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἀρξάμενος ἀπὸ τῆς γραφῆς ταύτης εὐηγγελίσατο αὐτῷ τὸν Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: Anoixas de ho Philippos to stoma autou kai arxamenos apo tēs graphēs tautēs euēggelīsato autō ton Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: Philip, seizing the opportunity, 'opened his mouth' (ἀνοίξας δὲ ὁ Φίλιππος τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ) and, 'beginning at the same scripture' (ἀρξάμενος ἀπὸ τῆς γραφῆς ταύτης), 'preached unto him Jesus' (εὐηγγελίσατο αὐτῷ τὸν Ἰησοῦν). This is a classic example of Christ-centered interpretation of the Old Testament. Philip showed how Isaiah 53, with its prophecy of a suffering servant, was fulfilled in the person and work of Jesus Christ, explaining His death, resurrection, and redemptive purpose.
verses.doctrinal_note: Philip's evangelism, starting from Old Testament prophecy to preach Jesus, exemplifies the continuity of God's redemptive plan. It demonstrates that Christ is the fulfillment of scripture, and effective gospel proclamation connects ancient promises with their ultimate realization in Him.

verses.kjv_text: And as they went on their way, they came unto a certain water: and the eunuch said, See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized?
verses.original_text: πορευομένων δὲ αὐτῶν ἐπὶ τῆς ὁδοῦ ἦλθον ἐπί τι ὕδωρ· καί φησιν ὁ εὐνοῦχος, Ἰδοὺ ὕδωρ· τί κωλύει με βαπτισθῆναι;
verses.transliteration: Poreuomenōn de autōn epi tēs hodou ēlthen epi ti hydōr; kai phēsin ho eunouchos, Idou hydōr; ti kōlyei me baptisthēnai?
verses.study_note: As they continued their journey, they came upon 'a certain water' (ἐπί τι ὕδωρ). The eunuch, having heard Philip's explanation of Jesus and likely about baptism as a response to faith, immediately asked, 'See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized?' (Ἰδοὺ ὕδωρ· τί κωλύει με βαπτισθῆναι;). This spontaneous request indicates his genuine conversion and his desire to publicly identify with Christ through baptism, demonstrating the immediate call to obedience for new believers.
verses.doctrinal_note: The eunuch's immediate request for baptism after hearing the gospel demonstrates the natural response of a converted heart. It highlights the early church's practice of believer's baptism as a public declaration of faith and obedience, signifying new life in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And Philip said, If thou believest with all thine heart, thou mayest. And he answered and said, I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God.
verses.original_text: [εἶπεν δὲ ὁ Φίλιππος, Εἰ πιστεύεις ἐξ ὅλης τῆς καρδίας, ἔξεστιν. ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ εἶπεν, Πιστεύω τὸν Υἱὸν τοῦ Θεοῦ εἶναι τὸν Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν.]
verses.transliteration: [Eipen de ho Philippos, Ei pisteueis ex holēs tēs kardias, exestin. Apokritheis de eipen, Pisteuō ton Huion tou Theou einai ton Iēsoun Christon.]
verses.study_note: This verse, though present in the KJV, is absent from many of the earliest and most reliable Greek manuscripts (e.g., Vaticanus, Sinaiticus, Alexandrinus) and is often bracketed or omitted in modern translations. If authentic, it emphasizes the prerequisite of 'believing with all thine heart' (πιστεύεις ἐξ ὅλης τῆς καρδίας) for baptism. The eunuch's confession, 'I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God' (Πιστεύω τὸν Υἱὸν τοῦ Θεοῦ εἶναι τὸν Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν), articulates a foundational Christian doctrine.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse (though textually debated) articulates the essential prerequisite for baptism: a heartfelt belief that 'Jesus Christ is the Son of God.' It underscores that baptism is for believers who have made a conscious confession of faith, affirming Christ's divine identity and authority.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded the chariot to stand still: and they went down both into the water, both Philip and the eunuch; and he baptized him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐκέλευσεν στῆναι τὸ ἅρμα, καὶ κατέβησαν ἀμφότεροι εἰς τὸ ὕδωρ, ὅ τε Φίλιππος καὶ ὁ εὐνοῦχος, καὶ ἐβάπτισεν αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: Kai ekeleusen stēnai to harma, kai katebēsan amphoteroi eis to hydōr, ho te Philippos kai ho eunouchos, kai ebaptisen auton.
verses.study_note: The eunuch 'commanded the chariot to stand still' (ἐκέλευσεν στῆναι τὸ ἅρμα). Then, 'they went down both into the water, both Philip and the eunuch' (κατέβησαν ἀμφότεροι εἰς τὸ ὕδωρ, ὅ τε Φίλιππος καὶ ὁ εὐνοῦχος), and Philip 'baptized him' (ἐβάπτισεν αὐτόν). The description of going 'into the water' strongly suggests immersion, the common mode of baptism in the early church. This act completed the eunuch's conversion, publicly signifying his new faith and cleansing.
verses.doctrinal_note: The act of both Philip and the eunuch going 'down into the water' and the eunuch being 'baptized' strongly implies immersion, the prevalent mode of baptism in the early church. This symbolizes death to sin and resurrection to new life in Christ, a public declaration of faith.

verses.kjv_text: And when they were come up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip, that the eunuch saw him no more: and he went on his way rejoicing.
verses.original_text: ὅτε δὲ ἀνέβησαν ἐκ τοῦ ὕδατος, Πνεῦμα Κυρίου ἥρπασεν τὸν Φίλιππον, καὶ οὐκ εἶδεν αὐτὸν οὐκέτι ὁ εὐνοῦχος, ἐπορεύετο γὰρ τὴν ὁδὸν αὐτοῦ χαίρων.
verses.transliteration: Hote de anebēsan ek tou hydatos, Pneuma Kyriou hērpasen ton Philippon, kai ouk eiden auton ouketi ho eunouchos, eporeueto gar tēn hodon autou chairōn.
verses.study_note: Immediately after the baptism, 'the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip' (Πνεῦμα Κυρίου ἥρπασεν τὸν Φίλιππον) in a supernatural manner, so that 'the eunuch saw him no more' (οὐκ εἶδεν αὐτὸν οὐκέτι ὁ εὐνοῦχος). This miraculous transportation highlights the divine orchestration of the entire encounter. The eunuch, however, 'went on his way rejoicing' (ἐπορεύετο γὰρ τὴν ὁδὸν αὐτοῦ χαίρων), indicating the profound joy and peace he found in his new faith, a testament to the transformative power of the gospel.
verses.doctrinal_note: Philip's miraculous disappearance, orchestrated by the 'Spirit of the Lord,' underscores the supernatural dimension of God's work in evangelism. The eunuch's subsequent 'rejoicing' signifies the profound joy and peace that accompany genuine conversion and the reception of salvation.

verses.kjv_text: But Philip was found at Azotus: and passing through he preached in all the cities, till he came to Caesarea.
verses.original_text: Φίλιππος δὲ εὑρέθη εἰς Ἄζωτον, καὶ διερχόμενος εὐηγγελίζετο τὰς πόλεις πάσας ἕως τοῦ ἐλθεῖν αὐτὸν εἰς Καισάρειαν.
verses.transliteration: Philippos de heurethē eis Azōton, kai dierchomenos euēggelizeto tas poleis pasas heōs tou elthein auton eis Kaisareian.
verses.study_note: Philip 'was found at Azotus' (εὑρέθη εἰς Ἄζωτον), an ancient Philistine city, some 20 miles north of Gaza.
