verses.doctrinal_note: Spirit-led ministry submits without objection to the Spirit's mission. The "why" of each gospel call is often revealed only after the obedience has been rendered.

verses.kjv_text: And Cornelius said, Four days ago I was fasting until this hour; and at the ninth hour I prayed in my house, and, behold, a man stood before me in bright clothing,
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ Κορνήλιος ἔφη· ἀπὸ τετάρτης ἡμέρας μέχρι ταύτης τῆς ὥρας ἤμην τὴν ἐνάτην προσευχόμενος ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ μου, καὶ ἰδοὺ ἀνὴρ ἔστη ἐνώπιόν μου ἐν ἐσθῆτι λαμπρᾷ
verses.transliteration: Kai ho Kornēlios ephē; apo tetartēs hēmeras mechri tautēs tēs hōras ēmēn tēn enatēn proseuchomenos en tō oikō mou, kai idou anēr estē enōpion mou en esthēti lamprā
verses.study_note: The four-day timeline is precise: day 1 — vision; day 2 — messengers travel; day 3 — Peter travels; day 4 — arrival. "Bright clothing" (esthēti lamprā) signals heavenly origin (cf. Luke 24:4).
verses.doctrinal_note: God honors fasting and prayer with fresh revelation. Humble disciplines produce spiritual vision not by merit but by preparing the heart to recognize heavenly presence when it arrives.

verses.kjv_text: And said, Cornelius, thy prayer is heard, and thine alms are had in remembrance in the sight of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ φησίν· Κορνήλιε, εἰσηκούσθη σου ἡ προσευχὴ καὶ αἱ ἐλεημοσύναι σου ἐμνήσθησαν ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Kai phēsin; Kornēlie, eisēkousthē sou hē proseuchē kai hai eleēmosynai sou emnēsthēsan enōpion tou theou.
verses.study_note: "Eisēkousthē" ("was heard") and "emnēsthēsan" ("were remembered") are divine passives. Cornelius's history of prayer and giving is recorded in heaven's ledger; now answered, not earned.
verses.doctrinal_note: God keeps faithful record of his people's prayers and alms, even before formal conversion. No godly act is lost; the remembrance of heaven gathers every seeking deed toward the day of encounter with Christ.

verses.kjv_text: Send therefore to Joppa, and call hither Simon, whose surname is Peter; he is lodged in the house of one Simon a tanner by the sea side: who, when he cometh, shall speak unto thee.
verses.original_text: πέμψον οὖν εἰς Ἰόππην καὶ μετακάλεσαι Σίμωνα ὃς ἐπικαλεῖται Πέτρος· οὗτος ξενίζεται ἐν οἰκίᾳ Σίμωνος βυρσέως παρὰ θάλασσαν.
verses.transliteration: Pempson oun eis Ioppēn kai metakalesai Simōna hos epikaleitai Petros; houtos xenizetai en oikia Simōnos byrseōs para thalassan.
verses.study_note: Cornelius's recall of the angelic instructions is precise, demonstrating his careful obedience. The repetition of the address for the public hearing creates a paired testimony confirming the divine appointment before everyone present.
verses.doctrinal_note: Testimony recited publicly strengthens corporate faith. What God did privately is meant to be shared openly; Christian witness often multiplies by its telling.

verses.kjv_text: Immediately therefore I sent to thee; and thou hast well done that thou art come. Now therefore are we all here present before God, to hear all things that are commanded thee of God.
verses.original_text: ἐξαυτῆς οὖν ἔπεμψα πρὸς σέ, σύ τε καλῶς ἐποίησας παραγενόμενος. νῦν οὖν πάντες ἡμεῖς ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ πάρεσμεν ἀκοῦσαι πάντα τὰ προστεταγμένα σοι ὑπὸ τοῦ κυρίου.
verses.transliteration: Exautēs oun epempsa pros se, sy te kalōs epoiēsas paragenomenos. Nyn oun pantes hēmeis enōpion tou theou paresmen akousai panta ta prostetagmena soi hypo tou kyriou.
verses.study_note: "Enōpion tou theou" ("in the presence of God") — the gathering is explicitly coram Deo. The Gentiles describe themselves as standing before God to receive his word; this is an extraordinary self-description for a hearer before the first gospel sermon they will ever hear.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ideal Christian hearing is conscious of God's presence. When the ears are opened in the fear of the Lord, the word that comes is received as from God himself — exactly the response that produces Pentecostal fruit.

verses.kjv_text: Then Peter opened his mouth, and said, Of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons:
verses.original_text: ἀνοίξας δὲ Πέτρος τὸ στόμα εἶπεν· ἐπ’ ἀληθείας καταλαμβάνομαι ὅτι οὐκ ἔστιν προσωπολήμπτης ὁ θεός,
verses.transliteration: Anoixas de Petros to stoma eipen; ep' alētheias katalambanomai hoti ouk estin prosōpolēmptēs ho theos,
verses.study_note: "Prosōpolēmptēs" (literally "face-receiver") is a Septuagint idiom for partial judgment — God does not judge by outward appearance (cf. 1 Sam 16:7; Deut 10:17). The negation announces the programmatic doctrine of Acts: the gospel is for every nation without ethnic partiality.
verses.doctrinal_note: The impartiality of God grounds the worldwide mission. Because God does not show favoritism, neither may his church; ethnic, social, and national prejudice is excluded from the gospel by the very nature of the God who gives it.

verses.kjv_text: But in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him.
verses.original_text: ἀλλ’ ἐν παντὶ ἔθνει ὁ φοβούμενος αὐτὸν καὶ ἐργαζόμενος δικαιοσύνην δεκτὸς αὐτῷ ἐστιν.
verses.transliteration: All' en panti ethnei ho phoboumenos auton kai ergazomenos dikaiosynēn dektos autō estin.
verses.study_note: "Dektos" ("acceptable, welcome") does not mean already saved apart from Christ; it means that God accepts for further gospel-hearing all who genuinely seek him. Cornelius is the living example: accepted for hearing, not yet saved — but about to be.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's welcoming providence precedes his saving grace. He draws God-fearers from every nation toward the gospel proclamation that alone can save them; acceptance for hearing is not confused with justification.

verses.kjv_text: The word which God sent unto the children of Israel, preaching peace by Jesus Christ: (he is Lord of all:)
verses.original_text: τὸν λόγον ὃν ἀπέστειλεν τοῖς υἱοῖς Ἰσραὴλ εὐαγγελιζόμενος εἰρήνην διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ· οὗτός ἐστιν πάντων κύριος,
verses.transliteration: Ton logon hon apesteilen tois huiois Israēl euangelizomenos eirēnēn dia Iēsou Christou; houtos estin pantōn kyrios,
verses.study_note: "Pantōn kyrios" ("Lord of all") — an emphatic parenthesis: the Christ proclaimed to Israel is the Lord of every people, including Romans. This single phrase anchors the universality of Christ's dominion.
verses.doctrinal_note: Jesus Christ is the Lord of every nation, not merely the Messiah of the Jews. The ethnic particularity of Israel's history served the universal kingship of its promised King; now the confession of Christ is offered to and required of all.

verses.kjv_text: That word, I say, ye know, which was published throughout all Judaea, and began from Galilee, after the baptism which John preached;
verses.original_text: ὑμεῖς οἴδατε τὸ γενόμενον ῥῆμα καθ’ ὅλης τῆς Ἰουδαίας, ἀρξάμενος ἀπὸ τῆς Γαλιλαίας μετὰ τὸ βάπτισμα ὃ ἐκήρυξεν Ἰωάννης,
verses.transliteration: Hymeis oidate to genomenon rhēma kath' holēs tēs Ioudaias, arxamenos apo tēs Galilaias meta to baptisma ho ekēryxen Iōannēs,
verses.study_note: Peter appeals to public knowledge: "you know" the recent events concerning Jesus. The gospel is not esoteric revelation but verifiable public history. The sermon follows the rough outline of the Gospel of Mark, beginning with John the Baptist.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian preaching is grounded in public facts. The apostolic gospel is not a private revelation but the announcement of historical events, and it always remains falsifiable in principle — and confirmed in fact.

verses.kjv_text: How God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Ghost and with power: who went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil; for God was with him.
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦν τὸν ἀπὸ Ναζαρέθ, ὡς ἔχρισεν αὐτὸν ὁ θεὸς πνεύματι ἁγίῳ καὶ δυνάμει, ὃς διῆλθεν εὐεργετῶν καὶ ἰώμενος πάντας τοὺς καταδυναστευομένους ὑπὸ τοῦ διαβόλου, ὅτι ὁ θεὸς ἦν μετ’ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Iēsoun ton apo Nazareth, hōs echrisen auton ho theos pneumati hagiō kai dynamei, hos diēlthen euergetōn kai iōmenos pantas tous katadynasteuomenous hypo tou diabolou, hoti ho theos ēn met' autou.
verses.study_note: "Echrisen" ("anointed") puns on "Christos" — Jesus is the anointed one par excellence. The summary of his ministry — "went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil" — is the most compressed biography of Christ's public work in the NT.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ's earthly ministry was a concerted assault on satanic oppression, authorized by the Father's anointing and empowered by the Holy Spirit. This trinitarian pattern of ministry is paradigmatic for the church that follows him.

verses.kjv_text: And we are witnesses of all things which he did both in the land of the Jews, and in Jerusalem; whom they slew and hanged on a tree:
verses.original_text: καὶ ἡμεῖς μάρτυρες πάντων ὧν ἐποίησεν ἔν τε τῇ χώρᾳ τῶν Ἰουδαίων καὶ ἐν Ἰερουσαλήμ, ὃν καὶ ἀνεῖλαν κρεμάσαντες ἐπὶ ξύλου.
verses.transliteration: Kai hēmeis martyres pantōn hōn epoiēsen en te tē chōra tōn Ioudaiōn kai en Ierousalēm, hon kai aneilan kremasantes epi xylou.
verses.study_note: "Epi xylou" ("on a tree") evokes Deuteronomy 21:23 — the one hanged is cursed of God. Peter freely admits the shame of the cross; the hideous death is not hidden but placed at the center of the proclamation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The crucifixion, which appeared as God's curse, is proclaimed as God's redemption. Christian preaching does not minimize but magnifies the cross, finding in its scandal the very heart of the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: Him God raised up the third day, and shewed him openly;
verses.original_text: τοῦτον ὁ θεὸς ἤγειρεν τῇ τρίτῃ ἡμέρᾳ καὶ ἔδωκεν αὐτὸν ἐμφανῆ γενέσθαι,
verses.transliteration: Touton ho theos ēgeiren tē tritē hēmera kai edōken auton emphanē genesthai,
verses.study_note: "Ēgeiren" ("raised") is God the Father's act. "Emphanē" ("manifest, visible") — the Father granted the risen Christ to appear publicly (though not to all; see v.41).
verses.doctrinal_note: The resurrection is the Father's vindication of the crucified Son and the foundation of every gospel offer. Without this historical, visible rising, Christianity has no message at all.

verses.kjv_text: Not to all the people, but unto witnesses chosen before of God, even to us, who did eat and drink with him after he rose from the dead.
verses.original_text: οὐ παντὶ τῷ λαῷ, ἀλλὰ μάρτυσιν τοῖς προκεχειροτονημένοις ὑπὸ τοῦ θεοῦ, ἡμῖν, οἵτινες συνεφάγομεν καὶ συνεπίομεν αὐτῷ μετὰ τὸ ἀναστῆναι αὐτὸν ἐκ νεκρῶν·
verses.transliteration: Ou panti tō laō, alla martysin tois prokecheirotonēmenois hypo tou theou, hēmin, hoitines synephagomen kai synepiomen autō meta to anastēnai auton ek nekrōn;
verses.study_note: "Synephagomen kai synepiomen" ("we ate and drank with him") — evidence of the bodily resurrection; a phantom cannot share food (Luke 24:42-43). The risen Christ is appointed to appear not to the curious world but to chosen witnesses whose testimony carries divine authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The resurrection is historically verified by chosen witnesses whose table-fellowship with the risen Lord anchors the church's apostolic confession. Christianity is not a myth but a testimony to events eaten and drunk with the Risen One.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded us to preach unto the people, and to testify that it is he which was ordained of God to be the Judge of quick and dead.
verses.original_text: καὶ παρήγγειλεν ἡμῖν κηρύξαι τῷ λαῷ καὶ διαμαρτύρασθαι ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ὡρισμένος ὑπὸ τοῦ θεοῦ κριτὴς ζώντων καὶ νεκρῶν.
verses.transliteration: Kai parēngeilen hēmin kēryxai tō laō kai diamartyrasthai hoti houtos estin ho hōrismenos hypo tou theou kritēs zōntōn kai nekrōn.
verses.study_note: "Kritēs zōntōn kai nekrōn" ("judge of the living and the dead") — the comprehensive eschatological function of Christ. Peter does not offer a consumer gospel but announces a Judge whom every human being will meet.
verses.doctrinal_note: Preaching the gospel includes preaching coming judgment by Christ. The same Jesus who invites is the Judge who comes; the gospel that offers mercy also announces universal accountability at the hands of the crucified-risen Lord.

verses.kjv_text: To him give all the prophets witness, that through his name whosoever believeth in him shall receive remission of sins.
verses.original_text: τούτῳ πάντες οἱ προφῆται μαρτυροῦσιν ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν λαβεῖν διὰ τοῦ ὀνόματος αὐτοῦ πάντα τὸν πιστεύοντα εἰς αὐτόν.
verses.transliteration: Toutō pantes hoi prophētai martyrousin aphesin hamartiōn labein dia tou onomatos autou panta ton pisteuonta eis auton.
verses.study_note: "Panta ton pisteuonta" ("everyone who believes") — this universal offer is the hinge on which the Spirit falls. "Aphesin hamartiōn" ("remission of sins") through his name alone pinpoints the one way of salvation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Salvation is by faith alone, through the name of Christ alone, available to every believer without ethnic qualification. The prophetic Scriptures and the apostolic kerygma converge on this one proposition — and the Spirit thunders its "Amen."

verses.kjv_text: While Peter yet spake these words, the Holy Ghost fell on all them which heard the word.
verses.original_text: Ἔτι λαλοῦντος τοῦ Πέτρου τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα ἐπέπεσεν τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον ἐπὶ πάντας τοὺς ἀκούοντας τὸν λόγον.
verses.transliteration: Eti lalountos tou Petrou ta rhēmata tauta epepesen to pneuma to hagion epi pantas tous akouontas ton logon.
verses.study_note: "Eti lalountos" ("while he was yet speaking") — the Spirit does not wait for the sermon's conclusion or a formal altar call. The word of faith, once heard, is answered by the Spirit before any human response has time to organize itself.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Holy Spirit sovereignly applies the gospel to whom and when he wills. No human mediation — not even apostolic order — controls the Spirit's timing; at Caesarea he interrupts the preacher to ratify the message.

verses.kjv_text: And they of the circumcision which believed were astonished, as many as came with Peter, because that on the Gentiles also was poured out the gift of the Holy Ghost.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξέστησαν οἱ ἐκ περιτομῆς πιστοὶ ὅσοι συνῆλθαν τῷ Πέτρῳ, ὅτι καὶ ἐπὶ τὰ ἔθνη ἡ δωρεὰ τοῦ ἁγίου πνεύματος ἐκκέχυται·
verses.transliteration: Kai exestēsan hoi ek peritomēs pistoi hosoi synēlthan tō Petrō, hoti kai epi ta ethnē hē dōrea tou hagiou pneumatos ekkechytai;
verses.study_note: "Ek peritomēs" ("of the circumcision") is Luke's technical term for Jewish Christians who still held to circumcision-distinction. "Ekkechytai" ("has been poured out") is the same verb used of Pentecost (2:17, 33); this is the Gentile Pentecost, theologically equivalent.
verses.doctrinal_note: God gave the gift of the Spirit to Gentiles on the same terms as to Jews. The sovereign pouring-out of the Spirit on uncircumcised hearers is God's own decisive settlement of the question that would occupy Acts 15 — already answered from heaven.

verses.kjv_text: For they heard them speak with tongues, and magnify God. Then answered Peter,
verses.original_text: ἤκουον γὰρ αὐτῶν λαλούντων γλώσσαις καὶ μεγαλυνόντων τὸν θεόν. τότε ἀπεκρίθη Πέτρος·
verses.transliteration: Ēkouon gar autōn lalountōn glōssais kai megalynontōn ton theon. Tote apekrithē Petros;
verses.study_note: "Glōssais" ("with tongues") — the identical Pentecostal sign of Acts 2. Its purpose here is evidential rather than missional: it authenticates the Gentile reception of the Spirit for the Jewish witnesses who needed to see it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The tongues at Caesarea are God's own public seal of Gentile inclusion. Where the Spirit duplicates Pentecost, the church has no choice but to duplicate baptism; divine precedent reshapes ecclesial practice.

verses.kjv_text: Can any man forbid water, that these should not be baptized, which have received the Holy Ghost as well as we?
verses.original_text: μήτι τὸ ὕδωρ δύναται κωλῦσαί τις τοῦ μὴ βαπτισθῆναι τούτους, οἵτινες τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον ἔλαβον ὡς καὶ ἡμεῖς;
verses.transliteration: Mēti to hydōr dynatai kōlysai tis tou mē baptisthēnai toutous, hoitines to pneuma to hagion elabon hōs kai hēmeis?
verses.study_note: The rhetorical question is the argument: if God has granted the Spirit, no man dare withhold the water. The Caesarean Gentiles receive the Spirit before baptism — reversing the sequence of Samaria (8:15-17) — to demonstrate that God, not the rite, grants the gift.
verses.doctrinal_note: Baptism follows the Spirit's sovereign granting; the rite acknowledges what God has already done. No human gatekeeper may withhold the visible sign when the invisible substance has been bestowed.

verses.kjv_text: And he commanded them to be baptized in the name of the Lord. Then prayed they him to tarry certain days.
verses.original_text: προσέταξεν δὲ αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ βαπτισθῆναι. τότε ἠρώτησαν αὐτὸν ἐπιμεῖναι ἡμέρας τινάς.
verses.transliteration: Prosetaxen de autous en tō onomati Iēsou Christou baptisthēnai. Tote ērōtēsan auton epimeinai hēmeras tinas.
verses.study_note: Peter "commanded" — the language of apostolic authority, now deployed on behalf of Gentile inclusion. Baptism "in the name of Jesus Christ" is the characteristic Acts formula, functionally equivalent to the trinitarian form (Matt 28:19). The request to "tarry" indicates ongoing instruction and fellowship.
verses.doctrinal_note: Water baptism publicly seals what the Spirit has wrought invisibly. The Caesarean household enters the visible church under the same ordinance received by Jewish believers — one body, one baptism, one Lord.

1.meaning: Centurion, commander of a century (~80-100 men). Key officer rank of the Roman army; six centurions appear favorably in the NT.

2.meaning: Devout, pious. The civic virtue of reverence toward deity; characterizes Cornelius's pre-conversion piety.
God-fearer. Technical designation for Gentiles who worshipped Israel's God without full proselyte conversion.

4.meaning: Memorial, remembrance-offering. LXX cultic term for grain offering; Cornelius's prayers ascend as incense-memorial.

10.meaning: Trance, ecstasy, standing-outside-oneself. State of prophetic rapture in which ordinary consciousness yields to divine revelation.

11.meaning: Sheet, linen cloth. Large sail-like fabric used here as image of creation-container.

12.meaning: Four-footed creatures. Genesis 1 and Leviticus 11 category of land animals, both clean and unclean.

13.meaning: Kill, slaughter, sacrifice. Sacral verb; more than 'butcher,' it carries liturgical overtones.

14.meaning: Common, ritually defiling. Later Jewish term for what pollutes ceremonially; contrasted with 'holy.'
Unclean (ritually). Levitical term for forbidden foods and persons; now redefined in Christ.

15.meaning: He cleansed. Divine act of purification; redraws what may and may not be called common.

19.meaning: Pondering, deeply reflecting. Intensified cognition as the Spirit interrupts with direct speech.

20.meaning: Doubting, discriminating, hesitating. Dual-sense: do not hesitate, and do not make ethnic distinctions.

22.meaning: Was divinely warned/instructed. Technical verb for formal revelation from God.

25.meaning: Worshipped, prostrated. Highest Roman civic honor, here appropriately refused.

28.meaning: Unlawful, forbidden. Refers to social barriers erected by rabbinic halakha, not Torah proper.
Foreigner, one of another tribe. LXX term for Philistine; here denotes ethnic outsider.

34.meaning: Respecter of persons, partial judge. Hebraism 'face-receiver'; the negation names God as impartial.

35.meaning: Acceptable, welcome, received. Indicates God's openness to the seeker, not the seeker's justification.

36.meaning: Lord of all. Emphatic ascription of universal sovereignty to Jesus Christ.

38.meaning: He anointed. Cognate with Christos; Jesus is the anointed one par excellence.
Doing good, bestowing benefit. The compressed summary of Jesus' ministry.
Oppressed, tyrannized. Describes the demonic tyranny Christ came to break.

42.meaning: Appointed, determined. Perfect passive; Christ's role as judge is an abiding, decreed reality.
Judge. Comprehensive eschatological function; Christ judges both living and dead.

43.meaning: Remission, release, forgiveness. Core soteriological term; release from debt of sin through Christ's name.

44.meaning: Fell upon. Sovereign descent of the Spirit, as at Pentecost (2:3).

45.meaning: Gift, free gift. The unearned character of the Spirit's coming is stressed.
Has been poured out. Perfect tense of Joel 2:28-29 fulfillment; the Spirit's abiding effusion.

46.meaning: Magnifying, extolling. Spirit-inspired worship; mirror of Mary's Magnificat.

meta.overview: Acts 11 consolidates the Gentile breakthrough of chapter 10 through two strategic developments. First, Peter's apostolic conduct is challenged by "they of the circumcision" in Jerusalem, who protest not the preaching of the gospel to Gentiles but Peter's eating with them. Peter recounts the vision, the Spirit's direction, the arrival of the messengers, the six witnessing brethren, and above all the sovereign descent of the Spirit on Cornelius's household — a Pentecost he neither asked for nor controlled. His defense is irresistibly simple: if God has given them the same gift he gave us, "who was I, that I could withstand God?" The Jerusalem church, remarkable in its teachability, glorifies God with the epoch-making conclusion: "Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life." Second, Luke traces a parallel, unforeseen gospel advance. The diaspora from Stephen's persecution had reached as far as Phenice, Cyprus, and Antioch, preaching to Jews only. But some Cypriots and Cyrenians at Antioch begin to preach the Lord Jesus to the Greeks, and "the hand of the Lord was with them." When the Jerusalem church sends Barnabas to investigate, he sees "the grace of God" and rejoices, exhorts the converts to steadfastness, and — in an act that will shape the worldwide mission — travels to Tarsus to fetch Saul. For a whole year Barnabas and Saul teach the Antioch church, and here the believers are first called "Christians" — a name probably coined as a term of derision by outsiders but divinely appropriated as the enduring badge of the new community. Finally, Agabus foretells a great famine (fulfilled under Claudius, c. AD 45-47), and the Antioch church — freshly Gentile and newly named — sends relief back to Jerusalem "by the hands of Barnabas and Saul," demonstrating mature, reciprocal koinonia between Jewish and Gentile Christians.
meta.theme: God Grants Repentance Unto Life to the Gentiles; the Church at Antioch Is Born
meta.keyWordMeaning: Christians (lit. ones of Christ, Christ-followers)
outline: Peter defends the Gentile mission before the Jerusalem church (vv.1-18)
The gospel reaches Antioch and Greeks believe (vv.19-21)
Barnabas at Antioch; he fetches Saul from Tarsus (vv.22-26)
Agabus prophesies famine; relief sent to Jerusalem (vv.27-30)

verses.kjv_text: And the apostles and brethren that were in Judaea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God.
verses.original_text: Ἤκουσαν δὲ οἱ ἀπόστολοι καὶ οἱ ἀδελφοὶ οἱ ὄντες κατὰ τὴν Ἰουδαίαν ὅτι καὶ τὰ ἔθνη ἐδέξαντο τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Ēkousan de hoi apostoloi kai hoi adelphoi hoi ontes kata tēn Ioudaian hoti kai ta ethnē edexanto ton logon tou theou.
verses.study_note: News of Caesarea reaches Judean congregations quickly. "Edexanto ton logon tou theou" ("received the word of God") is Luke's technical phrase for genuine conversion (cf. 8:14; 17:11). The epoch-making fact is framed simply: the nations have received the gospel.
verses.doctrinal_note: Authentic conversion consists in receiving the word of God. The test of every alleged spiritual movement is whether Scripture has been welcomed as the authoritative voice of God over one's life.

verses.kjv_text: And when Peter was come up to Jerusalem, they that were of the circumcision contended with him,
verses.original_text: Ὅτε δὲ ἀνέβη Πέτρος εἰς Ἰερουσαλήμ, διεκρίνοντο πρὸς αὐτὸν οἱ ἐκ περιτομῆς
verses.transliteration: Hote de anebē Petros eis Ierousalēm, diekrinonto pros auton hoi ek peritomēs
verses.study_note: "Hoi ek peritomēs" ("those of the circumcision") — Jewish Christians zealous for Torah observance. "Diekrinonto" ("contended, disputed") is plural and aggressive. Even apostolic authority does not exempt Peter from congregational scrutiny in the early church.
verses.doctrinal_note: Apostolic conduct was subject to congregational accountability. Early Christianity is a fellowship of mutual scrutiny, not a hierarchy above question; Peter does not claim immunity but explains his actions.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them.
verses.original_text: λέγοντες ὅτι εἰσῆλθες πρὸς ἄνδρας ἀκροβυστίαν ἔχοντας καὶ συνέφαγες αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: Legontes hoti eisēlthes pros andras akrobystian echontas kai synephages autois.
verses.study_note: "Synephages" ("didst eat with") is the specific grievance, not evangelism per se. Table fellowship with Gentiles breached the halakhic boundary that separated Israel; this is the very issue Galatians 2:11-14 will later revisit.
verses.doctrinal_note: Table fellowship between Jew and Gentile believers was the concrete frontier where gospel inclusion had to be worked out. The issue was not merely doctrinal but practical — who sits at whose table publicly embodies who belongs to whom.

verses.kjv_text: But Peter rehearsed the matter from the beginning, and expounded it by order unto them, saying,
verses.original_text: ἀρξάμενος δὲ Πέτρος ἐξετίθετο αὐτοῖς καθεξῆς λέγων·
verses.transliteration: Arxamenos de Petros exetitheto autois kathexēs legōn;
verses.study_note: "Kathexēs" ("in order") is a favorite Lukan word for orderly narration (Luke 1:3). Peter defends by narration, not argument: he recounts what God did, and the narrative is its own theology.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine acts, rightly recounted, carry their own argument. The most powerful defense of God's surprising work is faithful narrative of what was seen and heard.

verses.kjv_text: I was in the city of Joppa praying: and in a trance I saw a vision, A certain vessel descend, as it had been a great sheet, let down from heaven by four corners; and it came even to me:
verses.original_text: ἐγὼ ἤμην ἐν πόλει Ἰόππῃ προσευχόμενος, καὶ εἶδον ἐν ἐκστάσει ὅραμα, καταβαῖνον σκεῦός τι ὡς ὀθόνην μεγάλην τέσσαρσιν ἀρχαῖς καθιεμένην ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ, καὶ ἦλθεν ἄχρι ἐμοῦ·
verses.transliteration: Egō ēmēn en polei Ioppē proseuchomenos, kai eidon en ekstasei horama, katabainon skeuos ti hōs othonēn megalēn tessarsin archais kathiemenēn ek tou ouranou, kai ēlthen achri emou;
verses.study_note: Peter retells chapter 10 compactly. "Achri emou" ("as far as me") personalizes the divine address; the sheet came right to his feet.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine revelations come to particular persons in particular places. The universal implications of God's acts are always delivered through specific personal encounters Peter retells his own encounter with God to explain a doctrine that will reshape the church.

verses.kjv_text: Upon the which when I had fastened mine eyes, I considered, and saw fourfooted beasts of the earth, and wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls of the air.
verses.original_text: εἰς ἣν ἀτενίσας κατενόουν καὶ εἶδον τὰ τετράποδα τῆς γῆς καὶ τὰ θηρία καὶ τὰ ἑρπετὰ καὶ τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Eis hēn atenisas katenooun kai eidon ta tetrapoda tēs gēs kai ta thēria kai ta herpeta kai ta peteina tou ouranou.
verses.study_note: "Atenisas katenooun" ("fastening my gaze, I was observing") — Peter is careful to emphasize the deliberate, conscious inspection. This was no dream or hallucination; he inspected the vision.
verses.doctrinal_note: True spiritual perception requires close attention. Mystery is not the enemy of observation; what God shows is meant to be carefully examined and understood.

verses.kjv_text: And I heard a voice saying unto me, Arise, Peter; slay and eat.
verses.original_text: ἤκουσα δὲ καὶ φωνῆς λεγούσης μοι· ἀναστάς, Πέτρε, θῦσον καὶ φάγε.
verses.transliteration: Ēkousa de kai phōnēs legousēs moi; anastas, Petre, thyson kai phage.
verses.study_note: The repetition of the command strengthens the theological point. In narration, Peter is inviting the Jerusalem brethren to hear what he heard — to let the voice of God overwrite their received tradition.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine speech is the supreme court of theological revision. No ancestral pattern, however venerable, outranks a clear command of God newly spoken.

verses.kjv_text: But I said, Not so, Lord: for nothing common or unclean hath at any time entered into my mouth.
verses.original_text: εἶπον δέ· μηδαμῶς, κύριε, ὅτι κοινὸν ἢ ἀκάθαρτον οὐδέποτε εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸ στόμα μου.
verses.transliteration: Eipon de; mēdamōs, kyrie, hoti koinon ē akatharton oudepote eisēlthen eis to stoma mou.
verses.study_note: Peter highlights his own initial resistance — a brilliant rhetorical move. If he, a lifelong Torah-observant Jew, resisted but was corrected by God, the Jerusalem hearers can follow his example.
verses.doctrinal_note: Confession of one's prior resistance is a powerful tool of persuasion. The preacher who admits his own slowness to grasp divine revelation disarms the objections of those who still resist.

verses.kjv_text: But the voice answered me again from heaven, What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common.
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίθη δὲ φωνὴ ἐκ δευτέρου ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ· ἃ ὁ θεὸς ἐκαθάρισεν, σὺ μὴ κοίνου.
verses.transliteration: Apekrithē de phōnē ek deuterou ek tou ouranou; ha ho theos ekatharisen, sy mē koinou.
verses.study_note: The decisive theological sentence is repeated verbatim from 10:15 — a refrain that Peter, and now Jerusalem, cannot unhear. Divine cleansing defines what is clean; human categories must yield.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine cleansing redefines all prior categories of clean and unclean. The authority to call something common resides with the Creator alone, and where he has cleansed, his people have no veto.

verses.kjv_text: And this was done three times: and all were drawn up again into heaven.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἐγένετο ἐπὶ τρίς, καὶ ἀνεσπάσθη πάλιν ἅπαντα εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν.
verses.transliteration: Touto de egeneto epi tris, kai anespasthē palin hapanta eis ton ouranon.
verses.study_note: The threefold sign now functions as authenticating witness to the Jerusalem court. In Mosaic law, "in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established" (Deut 19:15); three heavenly iterations exceed that evidentiary threshold.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine repetition authenticates revelation for a skeptical audience. The threefold sign that overcame Peter's resistance now becomes the legal warrant his hearers must heed.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, immediately there were three men already come unto the house where I was, sent from Caesarea unto me.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἐξαυτῆς τρεῖς ἄνδρες ἐπέστησαν ἐπὶ τὴν οἰκίαν ἐν ᾗ ἤμην, ἀπεσταλμένοι ἀπὸ Καισαρείας πρός με.
verses.transliteration: Kai idou exautēs treis andres epestēsan epi tēn oikian en hē ēmēn, apestalmenoi apo Kaisareias pros me.
verses.study_note: Three men match the three heavenly iterations — Luke's numeric symmetry underscores providential orchestration. The messengers arrive at the precise moment the vision ends.
verses.doctrinal_note: God coordinates his revelations with his providential arrivals. The word he speaks inwardly is confirmed by what appears at the door; the seer learns the meaning of the sign by its exact correspondence to the circumstance.

verses.kjv_text: And the Spirit bade me go with them, nothing doubting. Moreover these six brethren accompanied me, and we entered into the man's house:
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ τὸ πνεῦμά μοι συνελθεῖν αὐτοῖς μηδὲν διακρίναντα. ἦλθον δὲ σὺν ἐμοὶ καὶ οἱ ἓξ ἀδελφοὶ οὗτοι, καὶ εἰσήλθομεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον τοῦ ἀνδρός.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de to pneuma moi synelthein autois mēden diakrinanta. Ēlthon de syn emoi kai hoi hex adelphoi houtoi, kai eisēlthomen eis ton oikon tou andros.
verses.study_note: "Hoi hex adelphoi houtoi" ("these six brethren") — Peter has brought his Joppa witnesses with him to Jerusalem. Seven witnesses total (Peter plus six) make the testimony unassailable under Jewish legal standards.
verses.doctrinal_note: God provides his servants with corroborating witnesses at the hour of their greatest need. Disputed decisions in the church are best settled by a plurality of qualified eyewitnesses acting together.

verses.kjv_text: And he shewed us how he had seen an angel in his house, which stood and said unto him, Send men to Joppa, and call for Simon, whose surname is Peter;
verses.original_text: ἀπήγγειλεν δὲ ἡμῖν πῶς εἶδεν τὸν ἄγγελον ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ αὐτοῦ σταθέντα καὶ εἰπόντα· ἀπόστειλον εἰς Ἰόππην καὶ μετάπεμψαι Σίμωνα τὸν ἐπικαλούμενον Πέτρον,
verses.transliteration: Apēngeilen de hēmin pōs eiden ton angelon en tō oikō autou stathenta kai eiponta; aposteilon eis Ioppēn kai metapempsai Simōna ton epikaloumenon Petron,
verses.study_note: Peter piles on the evidence: the Gentile side of the story has its own angelic visitation. The independence of the two revelations — neither party could have fabricated the convergence — is the strongest proof of divine orchestration.
verses.doctrinal_note: Independent, converging divine witness is irrefutable. When two parties unknown to each other are given coordinated revelations, the work is unmistakably God's.

verses.kjv_text: Who shall tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved.
verses.original_text: ὃς λαλήσει ῥήματα πρὸς σέ, ἐν οἷς σωθήσῃ σὺ καὶ πᾶς ὁ οἶκός σου.
verses.transliteration: Hos lalēsei rhēmata pros se, en hois sōthēsē sy kai pas ho oikos sou.
verses.study_note: "Rhēmata… en hois sōthēsē" ("words in which you will be saved") — salvation comes through spoken words, not private intuition. The household dimension ("pas ho oikos") is pronounced: salvation spreads by the word through relational units.
verses.doctrinal_note: Salvation requires hearing the specific words of the gospel. No amount of piety or yearning saves apart from the proclaimed message of Christ; this is why preaching is irreplaceable, and why God sends preachers to seekers.

verses.kjv_text: And as I began to speak, the Holy Ghost fell on them, as on us at the beginning.
verses.original_text: ἐν δὲ τῷ ἄρξασθαί με λαλεῖν ἐπέπεσεν τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον ἐπ’ αὐτοὺς ὥσπερ καὶ ἐφ’ ἡμᾶς ἐν ἀρχῇ.
verses.transliteration: En de tō arxasthai me lalein epepesen to pneuma to hagion ep' autous hōsper kai eph' hēmas en archē.
verses.study_note: "En archē" ("in the beginning") Peter refers to Pentecost; "hōsper" ("just as") binds the two events theologically. The Gentile Pentecost is identified with the Jewish Pentecost by the very Spirit who authors both.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gentile and Jewish believers share the same Spirit, the same gift, and the same church. The Spirit does not dispense subsidiary or lesser blessings upon the Gentiles; he pours out the identical Pentecost.

verses.kjv_text: Then remembered I the word of the Lord, how that he said, John indeed baptized with water; but ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost.
verses.original_text: ἐμνήσθην δὲ τοῦ ῥήματος τοῦ κυρίου ὡς ἔλεγεν· Ἰωάννης μὲν ἐβάπτισεν ὕδατι, ὑμεῖς δὲ βαπτισθήσεσθε ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ.
verses.transliteration: Emnēsthēn de tou rhēmatos tou kyriou hōs elegen; Iōannēs men ebaptisen hydati, hymeis de baptisthēsesthe en pneumati hagiō.
verses.study_note: Peter remembers Jesus' words (Acts 1:5). The phenomenon at Cornelius's house is identified as the "baptism with the Holy Ghost." This is the interpretive key: the divine event at Caesarea is the Lord's own fulfillment of his promise.
verses.doctrinal_note: The words of Christ are the interpretive grid for all subsequent work of the Spirit. When a new divine act occurs, the faithful ask: where did the Lord say he would do this?

verses.kjv_text: Forasmuch then as God gave them the like gift as he did unto us, who believed on the Lord Jesus Christ; what was I, that I could withstand God?
verses.original_text: εἰ οὖν τὴν ἴσην δωρεὰν ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ὁ θεὸς ὡς καὶ ἡμῖν πιστεύσασιν ἐπὶ τὸν κύριον Ἰησοῦν Χριστόν, ἐγὼ τίς ἤμην δυνατὸς κωλῦσαι τὸν θεόν;
verses.transliteration: Ei oun tēn isēn dōrean edōken autois ho theos hōs kai hēmin pisteusasin epi ton kyrion Iēsoun Christon, egō tis ēmēn dynatos kōlysai ton theon?
verses.study_note: "Tēn isēn dōrean" ("the equal gift") is categorical: the gift given to the Gentiles is identical to what Jews received. "Tis ēmēn dynatos kōlysai ton theon?" ("who was I to withstand God?") is the inescapable conclusion; the argument cannot be answered.
verses.doctrinal_note: To oppose the Gentile inclusion is to oppose God himself. The distinction between Jew and Gentile in saving grace has been erased by divine fiat, and no ecclesial authority may re-erect it.

verses.kjv_text: When they heard these things, they held their peace, and glorified God, saying, Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσαντες δὲ ταῦτα ἡσύχασαν καὶ ἐδόξασαν τὸν θεὸν λέγοντες· ἄρα καὶ τοῖς ἔθνεσιν ὁ θεὸς τὴν μετάνοιαν εἰς ζωὴν ἔδωκεν.
verses.transliteration: Akousantes de tauta hēsychasan kai edoxasan ton theon legontes; ara kai tois ethnesin ho theos tēn metanoian eis zōēn edōken.
verses.study_note: The Jerusalem church's response is remarkable: "hēsychasan" ("they became silent") — the disputation ceased. Then they glorified God. Their theological conclusion — "repentance unto life" for Gentiles — is the first programmatic affirmation of Gentile inclusion by the apostolic church.
verses.doctrinal_note: True teachability falls silent before the clear working of God and then bursts into praise. The Jerusalem church models a posture every generation of believers must learn: to revise inherited categories when heaven clarifies its own purpose.

verses.kjv_text: Now they which were scattered abroad upon the persecution that arose about Stephen travelled as far as Phenice, and Cyprus, and Antioch, preaching the word to none but unto the Jews only.
verses.original_text: Οἱ μὲν οὖν διασπαρέντες ἀπὸ τῆς θλίψεως τῆς γενομένης ἐπὶ Στεφάνῳ διῆλθον ἕως Φοινίκης καὶ Κύπρου καὶ Ἀντιοχείας, μηδενὶ λαλοῦντες τὸν λόγον εἰ μὴ μόνον Ἰουδαίοις.
verses.transliteration: Hoi men oun diasparentes apo tēs thlipseōs tēs genomenēs epi Stephanō diēlthon heōs Phoinikēs kai Kyprou kai Antiocheias, mēdeni lalountes ton logon ei mē monon Ioudaiois.
verses.study_note: Luke resumes the narrative thread from 8:1, now expanding it geographically. Phoenicia (modern Lebanon coast), Cyprus, and Antioch (Syrian Antioch — "queen of the East," the third-largest city of the Roman Empire at ~500,000) become the new fronts. The Stephen-refugees are initially Jewish-only in their preaching.
verses.doctrinal_note: God uses persecution to scatter the gospel into unforeseen territories. What the persecutors intended to silence, the Lord amplifies; ordinary displaced believers become the first missionaries to Asia, Africa, and Syria.

verses.kjv_text: And some of them were men of Cyprus and Cyrene, which, when they were come to Antioch, spake unto the Grecians, preaching the Lord Jesus.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν δέ τινες ἐξ αὐτῶν ἄνδρες Κύπριοι καὶ Κυρηναῖοι, οἵτινες ἐλθόντες εἰς Ἀντιόχειαν ἐλάλουν καὶ πρὸς τοὺς Ἑλληνιστάς, εὐαγγελιζόμενοι τὸν κύριον Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: Ēsan de tines ex autōn andres Kyprioi kai Kyrēnaioi, hoitines elthontes eis Antiocheian elaloun kai pros tous Hellēnistas, euangelizomenoi ton kyrion Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: The manuscripts divide between "Hellēnistas" (Greek-speaking Jews) and "Hellēnas" (Greeks, i.e. Gentiles); the narrative context strongly suggests Gentiles are meant. These unnamed Cypriot and Cyrenian believers — not apostles, not named leaders — take the momentous step of preaching to uncircumcised Gentiles.
verses.doctrinal_note: God often advances his mission through anonymous, ordinary disciples. The watershed transition at Antioch is led by unnamed brothers from Cyprus and Cyrene, proving that the kingdom does not wait upon official hierarchy.

verses.kjv_text: And the hand of the Lord was with them: and a great number believed, and turned unto the Lord.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦν χεὶρ κυρίου μετ’ αὐτῶν, πολύς τε ἀριθμὸς ὁ πιστεύσας ἐπέστρεψεν ἐπὶ τὸν κύριον.
verses.transliteration: Kai ēn cheir kyriou met' autōn, polys te arithmos ho pisteusas epestrepsen epi ton kyrion.
verses.study_note: "Cheir kyriou" ("hand of the Lord") is OT idiom for divine power at work (1 Kgs 18:46; Ezek 37:1). The missional initiative is authenticated by the same divine power that backed the original apostolic preaching.
verses.doctrinal_note: God owns the courageous step by visible empowerment. When his people risk obedience into unfamiliar territory, the hand of the Lord is with them, converting by the same divine power that drove the original Pentecost.

verses.kjv_text: Then tidings of these things came unto the ears of the church which was in Jerusalem: and they sent forth Barnabas, that he should go as far as Antioch.
verses.original_text: ἠκούσθη δὲ ὁ λόγος εἰς τὰ ὦτα τῆς ἐκκλησίας τῆς οὔσης ἐν Ἰερουσαλὴμ περὶ αὐτῶν, καὶ ἐξαπέστειλαν Βαρναβᾶν ἕως Ἀντιοχείας.
verses.transliteration: Ēkousthē de ho logos eis ta ōta tēs ekklēsias tēs ousēs en Ierousalēm peri autōn, kai exapesteilan Barnaban heōs Antiocheias.
verses.study_note: Barnabas is the inspired choice: a Levite of Cyprus (4:36), therefore a countryman of the Antioch pioneers; a man "full of the Holy Ghost and of faith" (v.24); and the one who had already vouched for Saul (9:27). Jerusalem's oversight is pastoral, not controlling.
verses.doctrinal_note: Healthy church oversight walks alongside new works, not over them. Jerusalem sends a son of encouragement, not an inspector, to the new Gentile frontier — recognizing that new wine requires new, receptive wineskins.

verses.kjv_text: Who, when he came, and had seen the grace of God, was glad, and exhorted them all, that with purpose of heart they would cleave unto the Lord.
verses.original_text: ὃς παραγενόμενος καὶ ἰδὼν τὴν χάριν τοῦ θεοῦ ἐχάρη, καὶ παρεκάλει πάντας τῇ προθέσει τῆς καρδίας προσμένειν τῷ κυρίῳ,
verses.transliteration: Hos paragenomenos kai idōn tēn charin tou theou echarē, kai parekalei pantas tē prothesei tēs kardias prosmenein tō kyriō,
verses.study_note: "Idōn tēn charin tou theou" ("seeing the grace of God") — Barnabas recognizes authentic conversion by its visible fruit, not by ceremonial marks. His exhortation — "prothesei tēs kardias" ("with purpose of heart") — is not against Torah but for persevering attachment to Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: Grace is visible where it is genuine; its presence can be recognized by spiritual discernment. The mature believer rejoices at the fruit of the Spirit wherever it grows, and exhorts new believers to resolute continuance in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: For he was a good man, and full of the Holy Ghost and of faith: and much people was added unto the Lord.
verses.original_text: ὅτι ἦν ἀνὴρ ἀγαθὸς καὶ πλήρης πνεύματος ἁγίου καὶ πίστεως. καὶ προσετέθη ὄχλος ἱκανὸς τῷ κυρίῳ.
verses.transliteration: Hoti ēn anēr agathos kai plērēs pneumatos hagiou kai pisteōs. Kai prosetethē ochlos hikanos tō kyriō.
verses.study_note: "Anēr agathos" ("a good man") — a rare accolade in Acts, granted also to Cornelius (10:22). Barnabas's character is the prerequisite of his ministry fruitfulness; the church grows by him because he is himself being shaped by the Spirit.
verses.doctrinal_note: Character is the basis of effective ministry. Fullness of Holy Spirit and faith are not decorative qualifications but the substantive source of fruitful pastoral impact on new believers.

verses.kjv_text: Then departed Barnabas to Tarsus, for to seek Saul:
verses.original_text: ἐξῆλθεν δὲ εἰς Ταρσὸν ἀναζητῆσαι Σαῦλον,
verses.transliteration: Exēlthen de eis Tarson anazētēsai Saulon,
verses.study_note: "Anazētēsai" ("to search out, look up") — Saul had been in Tarsus for several years (c. AD 38-45), out of public ministry. Barnabas, discerning that the predominantly Gentile Antioch work requires Saul's unique calling, travels to retrieve him. This is among the most consequential journeys in church history.
verses.doctrinal_note: God prepares specific servants in obscurity for specific works in the spotlight. The hidden years are not wasted years; they are formation time, and their fruit becomes visible only when the Lord appoints the hour of deployment.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had found him, he brought him unto Antioch. And it came to pass, that a whole year they assembled themselves with the church, and taught much people. And the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὑρὼν ἤγαγεν εἰς Ἀντιόχειαν. ἐγένετο δὲ αὐτοῖς καὶ ἐνιαυτὸν ὅλον συναχθῆναι ἐν τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ καὶ διδάξαι ὄχλον ἱκανόν, χρηματίσαι τε πρώτως ἐν Ἀντιοχείᾳ τοὺς μαθητὰς Χριστιανούς.
verses.transliteration: Kai heurōn ēgagen eis Antiocheian. Egeneto de autois kai eniauton holon synachthēnai en tē ekklēsia kai didaxai ochlon hikanon, chrēmatisai te prōtōs en Antiocheia tous mathētas Christianous.
verses.study_note: "Christianous" ("Christians") is formed on the Latinate pattern Herodianoi, Caesarianoi — "partisans of Christ." The name probably originated as a popular or official designation (Antioch was a city famous for sharp nicknames); Agrippa II uses it in 26:28, and 1 Peter 4:16 describes it as a name to suffer under. Luke presents it as providential: the multi-ethnic church needs a multi-ethnic name.
verses.doctrinal_note: The church's enduring name is Christ-centered. Believers are not primarily Jews, Greeks, Romans, or moderns — they are "ones of Christ," and this identity transcends and relativizes every prior categorization.

verses.kjv_text: And in these days came prophets from Jerusalem unto Antioch.
verses.original_text: Ἐν ταύταις δὲ ταῖς ἡμέραις κατῆλθον ἀπὸ Ἱεροσολύμων προφῆται εἰς Ἀντιόχειαν·
verses.transliteration: En tautais de tais hēmerais katēlthon apo Hierosolymōn prophētai eis Antiocheian;
verses.study_note: "Prophētai" ("prophets") are a recognized order in the early church (13:1; 21:9-10; 1 Cor 12:28; Eph 4:11). Jerusalem sends its gifts to the daughter congregation; the traffic of ministry now flows in both directions.
verses.doctrinal_note: Spiritual gifts circulate between mother and daughter churches for mutual strengthening. No congregation is spiritually self-sufficient; each receives from and contributes to the wider body.

verses.kjv_text: And there stood up one of them named Agabus, and signified by the Spirit that there should be great dearth throughout all the world: which came to pass in the days of Claudius Caesar.
verses.original_text: ἀναστὰς δὲ εἷς ἐξ αὐτῶν ὀνόματι Ἄγαβος ἐσήμανεν διὰ τοῦ πνεύματος λιμὸν μέγαν μέλλειν ἔσεσθαι ἐφ’ ὅλην τὴν οἰκουμένην· ἥτις ἐγένετο ἐπὶ Κλαυδίου.
verses.transliteration: Anastas de heis ex autōn onomati Agabos esēmanen dia tou pneumatos limon megan mellein esesthai eph' holēn tēn oikoumenēn; hētis egeneto epi Klaudiou.
verses.study_note: Agabus reappears in 21:10-11 foretelling Paul's arrest. The famine under Claudius (AD 41-54) is attested by Josephus, Suetonius, and Tacitus — a series of crop failures affected the entire Mediterranean basin in the mid-40s, with Judea especially hard hit c. AD 45-47.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Holy Spirit grants predictive revelation that is verified by subsequent history. Biblical prophecy is not vague sentiment but specific foretelling corroborated by extra-biblical record; Christianity is a religion anchored in falsifiable history.

verses.kjv_text: Then the disciples, every man according to his ability, determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judaea:
verses.original_text: τῶν δὲ μαθητῶν καθὼς εὐπορεῖτό τις ὥρισαν ἕκαστος αὐτῶν εἰς διακονίαν πέμψαι τοῖς κατοικοῦσιν ἐν τῇ Ἰουδαίᾳ ἀδελφοῖς·
verses.transliteration: Tōn de mathētōn kathōs euporeito tis hōrisan hekastos autōn eis diakonian pempsai tois katoikousin en tē Ioudaia adelphois;
verses.study_note: "Kathōs euporeito" ("according to his ability") establishes the principle of proportional giving (cf. 2 Cor 8:12). The young Gentile Antioch church gives to the older Jewish Jerusalem church — a reversal of expected flow that embodies the new covenant fellowship.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gospel fellowship is expressed in proportional, cross-cultural generosity. Christian giving is not flat equality but equitable proportion, each person contributing according to actual ability; and love crosses ethnic lines to sustain the saints.

verses.kjv_text: Which also they did, and sent it to the elders by the hands of Barnabas and Saul.
verses.original_text: ὃ καὶ ἐποίησαν ἀποστείλαντες πρὸς τοὺς πρεσβυτέρους διὰ χειρὸς Βαρναβᾶ καὶ Σαύλου.
verses.transliteration: Ho kai epoiēsan aposteilantes pros tous presbyterous dia cheiros Barnaba kai Saulou.
verses.study_note: First mention of "presbyterous" ("elders") in Acts for a Christian ordering of leadership, modeled on the Jewish synagogue. Barnabas and Saul, now partnered, make the first of several Jerusalem trips together — a relational bond that will be stressed in the first missionary journey.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gospel leadership is pluriform and plural: apostles, prophets, evangelists, elders. The church matures into an ordered structure of service, and the relief fund flows through trusted hands to responsible offices.

1.meaning: Received, welcomed. Luke's technical verb for authentic reception of the gospel word.

2.meaning: Disputed, contended. Middle-imperfect; an ongoing back-and-forth disputation, not a brief question.

3.meaning: Uncircumcision. Physical mark distinguishing Gentiles from Jews under the old covenant.
Ate together with. The specific grievance—table fellowship, not evangelism, was the scandal.

4.meaning: In order, sequentially. Lukan term for orderly narration (cf. Luke 1:3).

5.meaning: Trance, ecstasy. State of heightened spiritual reception.

12.meaning: Hesitating, discriminating. Same verb as v.2; Spirit commands Peter not to do what his critics are doing.

14.meaning: Words, utterances. Salvation is conveyed through specific spoken proclamation.
You will be saved. Future passive; saving act is divine, reception is human.

15.meaning: Fell upon. Sovereign descent of the Spirit, identical verb to Pentecost (10:44).

16.meaning: I remembered. Peter's Spirit-aided recall of the Lord's saying becomes hermeneutical key.

17.meaning: Equal gift. The Spirit bestowed on Gentiles is categorically identical with that given to Jews.
To hinder, prevent. The rhetorical point: no one can lawfully obstruct what God himself has done.

18.meaning: Became silent, were quieted. Teachable response to divine evidence.
Repentance, change of mind. Fundamental response to the gospel; granted by God to Gentiles.

19.meaning: Scattered. Persecution-driven diaspora; etymological root of 'diaspora.'

20.meaning: Preaching good news. Participle of continuous evangelistic activity.

21.meaning: Hand of the Lord. OT idiom for divine power at work (1 Kgs 18:46).
Turned. Conversion language; reorientation of life toward the Lord.

23.meaning: Grace. The divine enabling power visibly operative among the Antioch believers.
Purpose, resolve, determination. Settled intentional commitment, not mere feeling.
To remain close to, abide by. Perseverance in attachment to Christ.

24.meaning: Good, intrinsically excellent. Rare predicate of a person in Acts; Barnabas shares it with Cornelius.

25.meaning: To search out, look up carefully. Barnabas actively hunts Saul down in Tarsus.

26.meaning: Christians, partisans of Christ. First occurrence of the name that will define the movement.
Called, named. Same verb used of divine revelation earlier; here used of public naming.

28.meaning: Signified, made known by sign. Prophetic verb often with symbolic revelation.
Famine, hunger. Great famine during Claudius (AD 41-54), attested by multiple secular sources.
The inhabited world. Luke uses it of the Roman Empire; the famine was Mediterranean-wide.

29.meaning: Was prospering, had the means. Economic capacity varies; giving proportions accordingly.
Service, ministry, relief. The word for table-service becomes the word for diaconal aid.

30.meaning: Elders. First mention of Christian elders; office inherited from synagogue leadership.

meta.overview: Acts 12 is a study in two kings: Herod Agrippa I, the grandson of Herod the Great, and the unseen King of heaven who presides over his imprisoned servants. Eager to curry favor with the Jews, Herod seizes James the son of Zebedee and beheads him — the first martyrdom among the twelve — and then arrests Peter during the Passover, intending a public execution. But Luke inserts the pivot: "prayer was made without ceasing of the church unto God for him." On the very eve of his trial, with Peter chained between two soldiers and sixteen guards stationed, an angel of the Lord strikes Peter's side, raises him, leads him past three watches and through an iron gate that opens of its own accord, and departs — leaving Peter, astonished, to find himself free on a Jerusalem street. He goes to the house of Mary the mother of John Mark, where many are gathered praying. The young servant Rhoda recognizes his voice at the door and, in her joy, forgets to open the gate but runs to tell the others — who refuse to believe her until Peter's persistent knocking silences their unbelief. Peter recounts the deliverance, charges them to tell James (the Lord's brother, now rising in Jerusalem leadership), and departs to another place. At daybreak, Herod, finding Peter gone, executes the guards and retires to Caesarea. There, at a civic festival, he accepts divine acclamation from the gathered crowd, is struck by the angel of the Lord, and is eaten of worms and dies. The chapter ends with a triumphant couplet: "But the word of God grew and multiplied." Luke closes by noting that Barnabas and Saul return from Jerusalem, bringing with them John Mark — a transition that sets up the first missionary journey. The chapter is Luke's most vivid demonstration that earthly thrones rise and fall, persecutors strut and die, but the church prays and the word advances.
meta.theme: The Tyrant Dies, the Church Prays, the Word Grows
meta.keyWordMeaning: prayer, supplication
outline: Herod kills James and arrests Peter (vv.1-5)
Peter delivered from prison by an angel (vv.6-11)
The church's unbelieving prayer answered at Mary's house (vv.12-17)
Herod investigates and relocates to Caesarea (vv.18-19)
Herod's death at Caesarea (vv.20-23)
The word grows; Barnabas, Saul, and Mark return (vv.24-25)

verses.kjv_text: Now about that time Herod the king stretched forth his hands to vex certain of the church.
verses.original_text: Κατ’ ἐκεῖνον δὲ τὸν καιρὸν ἐπέβαλεν Ἡρῴδης ὁ βασιλεὺς τὰς χεῖρας κακῶσαί τινας τῶν ἀπὸ τῆς ἐκκλησίας.
verses.transliteration: Kat' ekeinon de ton kairon epebalen Hērōdēs ho basileus tas cheiras kakōsai tinas tōn apo tēs ekklēsias.
verses.study_note: Herod Agrippa I (reigned AD 41-44) ruled a united Judea under Claudius's favor, the most territory held by a Herodian since his grandfather. Josephus (Ant. 19.343-350) confirms his calculated cultivation of Jewish religious sentiment. "Epebalen… tas cheiras" ("laid hands on") is OT persecution idiom.
verses.doctrinal_note: Political power often exploits religious piety for its own advancement. Herod persecutes not from theological conviction but from political calculation, revealing how rulers have always weaponized popular religion against Christ's servants.

verses.kjv_text: And he killed James the brother of John with the sword.
verses.original_text: ἀνεῖλεν δὲ Ἰάκωβον τὸν ἀδελφὸν Ἰωάννου μαχαίρῃ.
verses.transliteration: Aneilen de Iakōbon ton adelphon Iōannou machairē.
verses.study_note: James the son of Zebedee is the first of the twelve martyred, fulfilling Jesus' prophecy to the sons of Zebedee in Mark 10:39. Execution "with the sword" under Roman/Herodian jurisdiction usually meant beheading. Luke's stark single sentence — uncommented — is theologically eloquent: the apostolic church knew both deliverance and martyrdom.
verses.doctrinal_note: God permits apostolic martyrdom as well as apostolic deliverance. The same faithfulness that delivers Peter takes James; divine sovereignty is never obligated to uniform outcomes, and the faith that honors God at the sword is as precious as the faith that walks out of prison.

verses.kjv_text: And because he saw it pleased the Jews, he proceeded further to take Peter also. (Then were the days of unleavened bread.)
verses.original_text: ἰδὼν δὲ ὅτι ἀρεστόν ἐστιν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις, προσέθετο συλλαβεῖν καὶ Πέτρον (ἦσαν δὲ αἱ ἡμέραι τῶν ἀζύμων)·
verses.transliteration: Idōn de hoti areston estin tois Ioudaiois, prosetheto syllabein kai Petron (ēsan de hai hēmerai tōn azymōn);
verses.study_note: "Areston tois Ioudaiois" ("pleasing to the Jews") exposes the political motive. "Hēmerai tōn azymōn" ("days of unleavened bread") — Passover season, when Jerusalem's population swelled with pilgrims; Herod plans maximum public impact. The echo of Jesus' Passover arrest is deliberate.
verses.doctrinal_note: Herod's plan to execute Peter at Passover deliberately echoes the Passion. But where the Father handed Jesus over to death, he here delivers Peter; the pattern of Passover deliverance is reasserted for the sake of the unfolding mission.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had apprehended him, he put him in prison, and delivered him to four quaternions of soldiers to keep him; intending after Easter to bring him forth to the people.
verses.original_text: ὃν καὶ πιάσας ἔθετο εἰς φυλακήν, παραδοὺς τέσσαρσιν τετραδίοις στρατιωτῶν φυλάσσειν αὐτόν, βουλόμενος μετὰ τὸ πάσχα ἀναγαγεῖν αὐτὸν τῷ λαῷ.
verses.transliteration: Hon kai piasas etheto eis phylakēn, paradous tessarsin tetradiois stratiōtōn phylassein auton, boulomenos meta to pascha anagagein auton tō laō.
verses.study_note: "Tessarsin tetradiois" ("four quaternions") — four squads of four soldiers, rotating the four watches of the night. Sixteen soldiers for one fisherman is a statement of Herod's paranoia after Peter's earlier miraculous prison release (5:19). The KJV's "Easter" is a 17th-c. translational anachronism — Greek "pascha" means Passover.
verses.doctrinal_note: God is not intimidated by overwhelming security measures. The more elaborate the human bulwark against God's purposes, the more spectacular his overturning of them; sixteen soldiers prove no match for one angel.

verses.kjv_text: Peter therefore was kept in prison: but prayer was made without ceasing of the church unto God for him.
verses.original_text: ὁ μὲν οὖν Πέτρος ἐτηρεῖτο ἐν τῇ φυλακῇ· προσευχὴ δὲ ἦν ἐκτενῶς γινομένη ὑπὸ τῆς ἐκκλησίας πρὸς τὸν θεὸν περὶ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Ho men oun Petros etēreito en tē phylakē; proseuchē de ēn ektenōs ginomenē hypo tēs ekklēsias pros ton theon peri autou.
verses.study_note: "Ektenōs" ("without ceasing, fervently") literally "stretched out" — prayer that strains toward God. The hinge of the entire chapter: while Herod's machinery clamps down, the church's prayer reaches up. The narrative contrast sets the stage for the deliverance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The unseen, unregarded prayer of the church is the decisive political force in history. Kings tighten grips; congregations tighten intercessions; heaven answers the latter and overturns the former.

verses.kjv_text: And when Herod would have brought him forth, the same night Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains: and the keepers before the door kept the prison.
verses.original_text: Ὅτε δὲ ἤμελλεν προαγαγεῖν αὐτὸν ὁ Ἡρῴδης, τῇ νυκτὶ ἐκείνῃ ἦν ὁ Πέτρος κοιμώμενος μεταξὺ δύο στρατιωτῶν δεδεμένος ἁλύσεσιν δυσίν, φύλακές τε πρὸ τῆς θύρας ἐτήρουν τὴν φυλακήν.
verses.transliteration: Hote de ēmellen proagagein auton ho Hērōdēs, tē nykti ekeinē ēn ho Petros koimōmenos metaxy dyo stratiōtōn dedemenos halysesin dysin, phylakes te pro tēs thyras etēroun tēn phylakēn.
verses.study_note: Peter "sleeping" (koimōmenos) on the night before his execution is astonishing evidence of faith-rest (cf. Ps 4:8). Chained to two soldiers and with two more at the door, he should be sweating; instead he sleeps like a man under fatherly protection.
verses.doctrinal_note: Peace in mortal danger is the signature of deep faith. The Christian does not sleep because the threat is trivial but because the Father is faithful; gospel tranquility shines brightest on the eve of crucifying days.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, the angel of the Lord came upon him, and a light shined in the prison: and he smote Peter on the side, and raised him up, saying, Arise up quickly. And his chains fell off from his hands.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄγγελος κυρίου ἐπέστη, καὶ φῶς ἔλαμψεν ἐν τῷ οἰκήματι· πατάξας δὲ τὴν πλευρὰν τοῦ Πέτρου ἤγειρεν αὐτὸν λέγων· ἀνάστα ἐν τάχει. καὶ ἐξέπεσαν αὐτοῦ αἱ ἁλύσεις ἐκ τῶν χειρῶν.
verses.transliteration: Kai idou angelos kyriou epestē, kai phōs elampsen en tō oikēmati; pataxas de tēn pleuran tou Petrou ēgeiren auton legōn; anasta en tachei. Kai exepesan autou hai halyseis ek tōn cheirōn.
verses.study_note: The angel's physical blow ("pataxas") is needed to wake the soundly sleeping Peter. "Phōs elampsen" ("light shone") — divine presence illumines the dungeon. Chains "fall off" without explanation; the deliverance is supernatural at every point.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's deliverance is irresistible when he chooses to act. Locks, chains, and soldiers are equally powerless against divine intervention; when heaven sends an angel, nothing earthly can detain heaven's servant.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel said unto him, Gird thyself, and bind on thy sandals. And so he did. And he saith unto him, Cast thy garment about thee, and follow me.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ ἄγγελος πρὸς αὐτόν· ζῶσαι καὶ ὑπόδησαι τὰ σανδάλιά σου. ἐποίησεν δὲ οὕτως. καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ· περιβαλοῦ τὸ ἱμάτιόν σου καὶ ἀκολούθει μοι.
verses.transliteration: Eipen de ho angelos pros auton; zōsai kai hypodēsai ta sandalia sou. Epoiēsen de houtōs. Kai legei autō; peribalou to himation sou kai akolouthei moi.
verses.study_note: The series of mundane commands — gird, put on sandals, wrap cloak — is deliberately unspectacular. The angel does not teleport Peter but walks him through ordinary dressing. Divine deliverance does not abolish creaturely sequence.
verses.doctrinal_note: Heavenly rescue still operates through earthly sequence. God delivers by commanding the rescued to take the next obedient step, not by abolishing human action but by directing it.

verses.kjv_text: And he went out, and followed him; and wist not that it was true which was done by the angel; but thought he saw a vision.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξελθὼν ἠκολούθει, καὶ οὐκ ᾔδει ὅτι ἀληθές ἐστιν τὸ γινόμενον διὰ τοῦ ἀγγέλου, ἐδόκει δὲ ὅραμα βλέπειν.
verses.transliteration: Kai exelthōn ēkolouthei, kai ouk ēdei hoti alēthes estin to ginomenon dia tou angelou, edokei de horama blepein.
verses.study_note: Peter's confusion is candidly reported: he thought he was dreaming. The candid admission authenticates the account — invented heroics do not cast their protagonists as half-asleep.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's works frequently outpace his servants' understanding. Obedient following in apparent unreality is often the pattern of discipleship; clarity arrives retrospectively, after the deliverance is complete.

verses.kjv_text: When they were past the first and the second ward, they came unto the iron gate that leadeth unto the city; which opened to them of his own accord: and they went out, and passed on through one street; and forthwith the angel departed from him.
verses.original_text: διελθόντες δὲ πρώτην φυλακὴν καὶ δευτέραν ἦλθαν ἐπὶ τὴν πύλην τὴν σιδηρᾶν τὴν φέρουσαν εἰς τὴν πόλιν, ἥτις αὐτομάτη ἠνοίγη αὐτοῖς, καὶ ἐξελθόντες προῆλθον ῥύμην μίαν, καὶ εὐθέως ἀπέστη ὁ ἄγγελος ἀπ’ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Dielthontes de prōtēn phylakēn kai deuteran ēlthan epi tēn pylēn tēn sidēran tēn pherousan eis tēn polin, hētis automatē ēnoigē autois, kai exelthontes proēlthon rhymēn mian, kai eutheōs apestē ho angelos ap' autou.
verses.study_note: "Automatē" ("of its own accord") is the only NT use of this word. The same term is used in the LXX for the land that yields crop without plowing (Lev 25:5); divinity operates without mechanism. The angel withdraws once Peter is safely in the city.
verses.doctrinal_note: God provides what is necessary and no more. Angels assist in what cannot be done without them, then withdraw; believers are returned to the ordinary means of grace and human prudence once the crisis is past.

verses.kjv_text: And when Peter was come to himself, he said, Now I know of a surety, that the Lord hath sent his angel, and hath delivered me out of the hand of Herod, and from all the expectation of the people of the Jews.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ Πέτρος ἐν ἑαυτῷ γενόμενος εἶπεν· νῦν οἶδα ἀληθῶς ὅτι ἐξαπέστειλεν κύριος τὸν ἄγγελον αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐξείλατό με ἐκ χειρὸς Ἡρῴδου καὶ πάσης τῆς προσδοκίας τοῦ λαοῦ τῶν Ἰουδαίων.
verses.transliteration: Kai ho Petros en heautō genomenos eipen; nyn oida alēthōs hoti exapesteilen kyrios ton angelon autou kai exeilato me ek cheiros Hērōdou kai pasēs tēs prosdokias tou laou tōn Ioudaiōn.
verses.study_note: "En heautō genomenos" ("coming to himself") — the same phrase used of the prodigal son (Luke 15:17); Peter recognizes reality. His confession echoes Psalm 34:7 ("The angel of the LORD encampeth round about them that fear him") and Daniel 6:22 ("My God hath sent his angel").
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine deliverance is identified only in retrospect. The pilgrim often walks through miracles before he recognizes them; thanksgiving must follow perception, but perception is itself a gift of grace.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had considered the thing, he came to the house of Mary the mother of John, whose surname was Mark; where many were gathered together praying.
verses.original_text: συνιδών τε ἦλθεν ἐπὶ τὴν οἰκίαν τῆς Μαρίας τῆς μητρὸς Ἰωάννου τοῦ ἐπικαλουμένου Μάρκου, οὗ ἦσαν ἱκανοὶ συνηθροισμένοι καὶ προσευχόμενοι.
verses.transliteration: Synidōn te ēlthen epi tēn oikian tēs Marias tēs mētros Iōannou tou epikaloumenou Markou, hou ēsan hikanoi synēthroismenoi kai proseuchomenoi.
verses.study_note: Mary's house appears to be a wealthier Jerusalem property (it had a gate, a servant, and enough rooms for "many" to gather). Her son John Mark will become the author of the Second Gospel and Paul's companion. The household is evidently a hub of early Jerusalem Christianity.
verses.doctrinal_note: God raises households that sustain the mission. Mary's home becomes a fixed place of prayer; generations of faithful laity, often female heads of households, have provided the physical infrastructure of the church's intercessory life.

verses.kjv_text: And as Peter knocked at the door of the gate, a damsel came to hearken, named Rhoda.
verses.original_text: κρούσαντος δὲ αὐτοῦ τὴν θύραν τοῦ πυλῶνος προσῆλθεν παιδίσκη ὑπακοῦσαι ὀνόματι Ῥόδη,
verses.transliteration: Krousantos de autou tēn thyran tou pylōnos prosēlthen paidiskē hypakousai onomati Rhodē,
verses.study_note: "Rhodē" means "rose." The fact that Luke preserves the name of this servant girl — an otherwise socially invisible figure — suggests she was still known in the early church. The "pylōn" is the outer gate of a larger courtyard, distinct from the inner door.
verses.doctrinal_note: God preserves the names of the humble who witness his great works. The roll of gospel history is not filled only with apostles and prophets; a servant girl at a door has her name remembered in Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: And when she knew Peter's voice, she opened not the gate for gladness, but ran in, and told how Peter stood before the gate.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπιγνοῦσα τὴν φωνὴν τοῦ Πέτρου ἀπὸ τῆς χαρᾶς οὐκ ἤνοιξεν τὸν πυλῶνα, εἰσδραμοῦσα δὲ ἀπήγγειλεν ἑστάναι τὸν Πέτρον πρὸ τοῦ πυλῶνος.
verses.transliteration: Kai epignousa tēn phōnēn tou Petrou apo tēs charas ouk ēnoixen ton pylōna, eisdramousa de apēngeilen hestanai ton Petron pro tou pylōnos.
verses.study_note: Rhoda's joy-driven forgetfulness — she leaves Peter standing outside while rushing to tell — is a masterstroke of human-scale realism. The scene could be invented, but few hagiographers would have their hero awkwardly left knocking.
verses.doctrinal_note: Luke's candid touches of human comedy authenticate sacred narrative. Scripture loses no dignity by recording domestic vignettes; on the contrary, the Spirit's inspiration includes the grain of ordinary life in which he delights.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto her, Thou art mad. But she constantly affirmed that it was even so. Then said they, It is his angel.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ πρὸς αὐτὴν εἶπαν· μαίνῃ. ἡ δὲ διϊσχυρίζετο οὕτως ἔχειν. οἱ δὲ ἔλεγον· ὁ ἄγγελος ἐστὶν αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Hoi de pros autēn eipan; mainē. Hē de diischyrizeto houtōs echein. Hoi de elegon; ho angelos estin autou.
verses.study_note: "Mainē" ("you are mad") — the prayerful company refuses to believe their prayer has been answered. "Ho angelos… autou" ("his angel") reflects a Jewish belief in guardian angels (cf. Matt 18:10). The irony is stinging: they would sooner believe a ghost than a deliverance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The church's prayers often outrun her faith. God answers supplications that the supplicants cannot quite credit; unbelief is possible even among the faithful, and the Lord graciously overtakes it with evidence.

verses.kjv_text: But Peter continued knocking: and when they had opened the door, and saw him, they were astonished.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Πέτρος ἐπέμενεν κρούων· ἀνοίξαντες δὲ εἶδαν αὐτὸν καὶ ἐξέστησαν.
verses.transliteration: Ho de Petros epemenen krouōn; anoixantes de eidan auton kai exestēsan.
verses.study_note: "Epemenen" ("kept on") — Peter's persistent knocking forces the disputed issue. "Exestēsan" ("they were astonished, beside themselves") is the same word as the earthquake-trance vocabulary; joy has the same effect as prophetic rapture.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ graciously overcomes the half-faith of his church. The Savior persists at the door with answers we have scarcely believed were possible; perseverance on his side meets hesitation on ours.

verses.kjv_text: But he, beckoning unto them with the hand to hold their peace, declared unto them how the Lord had brought him out of the prison. And he said, Go shew these things unto James, and to the brethren. And he departed, and went into another place.
verses.original_text: κατασείσας δὲ αὐτοῖς τῇ χειρὶ σιγᾶν διηγήσατο αὐτοῖς πῶς ὁ κύριος αὐτὸν ἐξήγαγεν ἐκ τῆς φυλακῆς, εἶπέν τε· ἀπαγγείλατε Ἰακώβῳ καὶ τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς ταῦτα. καὶ ἐξελθὼν ἐπορεύθη εἰς ἕτερον τόπον.
verses.transliteration: Kataseisas de autois tē cheiri sigan diēgēsato autois pōs ho kyrios auton exēgagen ek tēs phylakēs, eipen te; apangeilate Iakōbō kai tois adelphois tauta. Kai exelthōn eporeuthē eis heteron topon.
verses.study_note: "Iakōbō" is James the brother of Jesus (not James son of Zebedee, just killed). He emerges here as a leader of the Jerusalem church (cf. 15:13; 21:18; Gal 1:19; 2:9). "Heteron topon" ("another place") Luke leaves intentionally vague — prudent silence about Peter's location protects him.
verses.doctrinal_note: Faithful discipleship includes prudent discretion. Not every gracious deliverance is broadcast; sometimes the proper response to divine rescue is quiet movement to a safer place, leaving the glory to God without advertising our own survival.

verses.kjv_text: Now as soon as it was day, there was no small stir among the soldiers, what was become of Peter.
verses.original_text: Γενομένης δὲ ἡμέρας ἦν τάραχος οὐκ ὀλίγος ἐν τοῖς στρατιώταις, τί ἄρα ὁ Πέτρος ἐγένετο.
verses.transliteration: Genomenēs de hēmeras ēn tarachos ouk oligos en tois stratiōtais, ti ara ho Petros egeneto.
verses.study_note: "Tarachos ouk oligos" ("no small stir") is Lukan litotes for extreme panic. Under Roman military law, guards whose prisoner escaped suffered the prisoner's sentence (cf. 27:42). The soldiers face execution.
verses.doctrinal_note: The consequences of divine intervention often reverberate painfully in the lives of those bound to systems of injustice. God's mercy to one can mean calamity for another; the moral weight of tyrannical regimes falls on their human instruments.

verses.kjv_text: And when Herod had sought for him, and found him not, he examined the keepers, and commanded that they should be put to death. And he went down from Judaea to Caesarea, and there abode.
verses.original_text: Ἡρῴδης δὲ ἐπιζητήσας αὐτὸν καὶ μὴ εὑρὼν ἀνακρίνας τοὺς φύλακας ἐκέλευσεν ἀπαχθῆναι, καὶ κατελθὼν ἀπὸ τῆς Ἰουδαίας εἰς Καισάρειαν διέτριβεν.
verses.transliteration: Hērōdēs de epizētēsas auton kai mē heurōn anakrinas tous phylakas ekeleusen apachthēnai, kai katelthōn apo tēs Ioudaias eis Kaisareian dietriben.
verses.study_note: "Apachthēnai" ("to be led away") is customary shorthand for capital execution. Herod's retreat to Caesarea (the Roman provincial capital) is narrative preparation for the judgment scene that follows; the tyrant removes himself to the place of his own death.
verses.doctrinal_note: Wicked rulers tighten cruelty when humiliated, but God hems their course toward judgment. Herod's murderous response is itself a step on his providential path to the divine verdict soon to fall upon him.

verses.kjv_text: And Herod was highly displeased with them of Tyre and Sidon: but they came with one accord to him, and, having made Blastus the king's chamberlain their friend, desired peace; because their country was nourished by the king's country.
verses.original_text: Ἦν δὲ θυμομαχῶν Τυρίοις καὶ Σιδωνίοις· ὁμοθυμαδὸν δὲ παρῆσαν πρὸς αὐτόν, καὶ πείσαντες Βλάστον τὸν ἐπὶ τοῦ κοιτῶνος τοῦ βασιλέως ᾐτοῦντο εἰρήνην, διὰ τὸ τρέφεσθαι αὐτῶν τὴν χώραν ἀπὸ τῆς βασιλικῆς.
verses.transliteration: Ēn de thymomachōn Tyriois kai Sidōniois; homothymadon de parēsan pros auton, kai peisantes Blaston ton epi tou koitōnos tou basileōs ētounto eirēnēn, dia to trephesthai autōn tēn chōran apo tēs basilikēs.
verses.study_note: Tyre and Sidon, coastal Phoenician cities, depended on Judean grain (cf. 1 Kgs 5:11; Ezek 27:17). "Blastus the king's chamberlain" is literally "the one over the king's bedchamber" — a high courtier. A trade embargo has reduced the cities to courtly bribery for peace.
verses.doctrinal_note: Political power and economic dependence entangle nations. Behind the glittering royal levee lie mundane realities of supply chains and palace intrigue; and over all, God superintends the moment he will expose the king's vanity.

verses.kjv_text: And upon a set day Herod, arrayed in royal apparel, sat upon his throne, and made an oration unto them.
verses.original_text: τακτῇ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ ὁ Ἡρῴδης ἐνδυσάμενος ἐσθῆτα βασιλικὴν καὶ καθίσας ἐπὶ τοῦ βήματος ἐδημηγόρει πρὸς αὐτούς·
verses.transliteration: Taktē de hēmera ho Hērōdēs endysamenos esthēta basilikēn kai kathisas epi tou bēmatos edēmēgorei pros autous;
verses.study_note: Josephus (Ant. 19.343-350) confirms this scene in remarkable detail: a festival in Caesarea in honor of Claudius, Herod appearing in a robe of silver that flashed in the morning sun, an oration from the platform. Luke's narrative and Josephus independently corroborate each other.
verses.doctrinal_note: Scripture and secular history confirm each other on the major public events of the apostolic age. The faith rests on verifiable occurrences, not mythic invention; historians like Josephus preserve the scaffolding Luke assumes.

verses.kjv_text: And the people gave a shout, saying, It is the voice of a god, and not of a man.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ δῆμος ἐπεφώνει· θεοῦ φωνὴ καὶ οὐκ ἀνθρώπου.
verses.transliteration: Ho de dēmos epephōnei; theou phōnē kai ouk anthrōpou.
verses.study_note: The Caesarea crowd, mostly Gentile, acclaims Herod in the language of imperial apotheosis. Josephus records that the flatterers "addressed him as a god." Herod does not refuse the acclaim, violating even Jewish monotheism in a public forum.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine honor stolen by human rulers is the ancient idolatry that God decisively refuses. No ruler, however powerful, may accept worship; the glory of God is jealous beyond tolerance.

verses.kjv_text: And immediately the angel of the Lord smote him, because he gave not God the glory: and he was eaten of worms, and gave up the ghost.
verses.original_text: παραχρῆμα δὲ ἐπάταξεν αὐτὸν ἄγγελος κυρίου ἀνθ’ ὧν οὐκ ἔδωκεν τὴν δόξαν τῷ θεῷ, καὶ γενόμενος σκωληκόβρωτος ἐξέψυξεν.
verses.transliteration: Parachrēma de epataxen auton angelos kyriou anth' hōn ouk edōken tēn doxan tō theō, kai genomenos skōlēkobrōtos exepsyxen.
verses.study_note: The same "angel of the Lord" who struck Peter to save him strikes Herod to destroy him. Josephus reports Herod suffering a gut disorder for five days before dying — likely hydatid cyst rupture with intestinal perforation and worm infestation, consistent with "skōlēkobrōtos" ("eaten of worms"). A chillingly precise cause of death echoing OT judgments on Antiochus IV (2 Macc 9:5-12).
verses.doctrinal_note: God strikes those who usurp his glory. The quick fate of Herod Agrippa — applauded as a god one moment, corpse the next — is the perennial warning to every would-be deity and every dazzled crowd.

verses.kjv_text: But the word of God grew and multiplied.
verses.original_text: Ὁ δὲ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ηὔξανεν καὶ ἐπληθύνετο.
verses.transliteration: Ho de logos tou theou ēuxanen kai eplēthyneto.
verses.study_note: One of Luke's summary refrains (cf. 6:7; 12:24; 19:20). The contrast is total: Herod dies, the word grows. Earthly power fades; divine proclamation propagates. The two imperfect verbs connote continuous advance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The word of God is the only power that outlasts every earthly regime. Kingdoms crumble, tyrants expire, and empires recycle, but the gospel continues to grow and multiply as the uninterrupted operation of the eternal God.

verses.kjv_text: And Barnabas and Saul returned from Jerusalem, when they had fulfilled their ministry, and took with them John, whose surname was Mark.
verses.original_text: Βαρναβᾶς δὲ καὶ Σαῦλος ὑπέστρεψαν εἰς Ἱερουσαλὴμ πληρώσαντες τὴν διακονίαν, συμπαραλαβόντες Ἰωάννην τὸν ἐπικληθέντα Μάρκον.
verses.transliteration: Barnabas de kai Saulos hypestrepsan eis Hierousalēm plērōsantes tēn diakonian, symparalabontes Iōannēn ton epiklēthenta Markon.
verses.study_note: Manuscript traditions vary between "eis" and "ex" Jerusalem; the UBS reading "to Jerusalem" may be a scribal confusion, and context argues for return "from Jerusalem" back to Antioch. John Mark, Mary's son (v.12) and Barnabas's cousin (Col 4:10), is introduced — the future companion of missionaries and evangelist.
verses.doctrinal_note: God matures the next generation of workers alongside his current leaders. Young John Mark travels with Barnabas and Saul not as a project of the work but as a son being trained — the pattern of faithful succession.

1.meaning: To mistreat, harm, oppress. Aorist infinitive conveying harmful intent.

2.meaning: Killed, took away. The standard verb for judicial execution.
Sword, dagger. Instrument of Roman-style beheading.
