verses.study_note: Litotes — "no little time," probably about a year prior to the Jerusalem Council (ch. 15). Paul and Barnabas minister among their sending church.
verses.doctrinal_note: Missionaries need seasons of settled fellowship as well as seasons of frontier toil. The sending church and the sent apostle belong to one another before the Lord.

1.meaning: Synagogue — the Jewish assembly and gathering house; Paul's consistent first platform in each city (Rom 1:16).
Multitude, great number — here of mixed Jews and Greeks, prefiguring one new man in Christ.

2.meaning: Having refused to be persuaded, disobedient — unbelief as active rejection, not mere absence of faith.
Embittered, poisoned — literally "made evil"; moral corruption of the mind by slander.

3.meaning: Speaking boldly, with full freedom — a Lukan signature of Spirit-filled preaching (cf. 4:31; 9:27).
Signs and wonders — the attesting miracles that authenticate apostolic preaching (Heb 2:4).

4.meaning: Was divided, split — root of "schism"; the gospel cleaves cities and households (Matt 10:34-36).
Apostles, sent ones — here Paul and Barnabas are so called in the broader sense of commissioned witnesses.

5.meaning: Rush, violent impulse, concerted assault — an onrush of hostile intent.
To stone — the Jewish capital punishment for blasphemy; foreshadowing v.19.

6.meaning: Fully perceiving, realizing — Spirit-guided discernment leading to tactical withdrawal.

7.meaning: Preaching good news, evangelizing — the present participle stresses continuous ongoing proclamation.

8.meaning: Without strength, impotent — used of physical and moral inability; here of a man lame from birth.

9.meaning: Fixed his eyes intently — a Lukan word for Spirit-quickened gaze that sees beyond surfaces.
Faith to be saved — physical healing framed as a picture of whole-person salvation.

10.meaning: Stand up! — a resurrection imperative; creative word of power from the risen Christ through His apostle.
Leaped up — echoes Isa 35:6 LXX, the Messianic sign of the kingdom.

11.meaning: In the Lycaonian language — a local dialect unintelligible to Paul, delaying his awareness of the crowd's intent.
Made like, likened — the pagan notion of gods assuming human form.

12.meaning: Zeus (Jupiter) — chief god of the Greek pantheon; Lystra had a temple of Zeus outside the city.
Hermes (Mercury) — messenger of the gods, patron of eloquence; identified with Paul for his speaking.

13.meaning: Garlands, wreaths — adornments placed on sacrificial animals and on the worshippers.

14.meaning: Having torn through — the Jewish gesture of horror at blasphemy (cf. Matt 26:65).

15.meaning: Of like feelings/passions — sharing the same nature; cf. Jas 5:17 of Elijah.
Vain, empty things — LXX vocabulary for idols (Jer 10:15); idolatry is nothingness.
To turn, convert — the radical reorientation from idols to the living God (1 Thess 1:9).
The living God — the covenant self-designation (Deut 5:26), set in contrast to lifeless idols.

17.meaning: Without witness — God's creation and providence are His ceaseless self-testimony (Rom 1:19-20).
Doing good — common grace summarized; God gives rain and harvest to all without distinction.
Gladness, joy — the affective fruit of providential provision, revealing a good Giver.

19.meaning: Having stoned — the execution Saul once consented to is now inflicted upon him (cf. 7:58; 2 Cor 11:25).
To have died — "supposing him dead"; some suggest an actual near-death vision (2 Cor 12:1-4).

20.meaning: Having risen up — the resurrection verb; Paul's getting up is a parable of Christ's power in his flesh.

21.meaning: Having made disciples — the same word as Matt 28:19; mission is disciple-making, not decision-counting.

22.meaning: Strengthening upon, fortifying — pastoral reinforcement of young believers' faith.
To remain in, continue — perseverance is the evidence of genuine faith.
Tribulations, pressures — the squeezing trials ordained as the pathway into the kingdom.

23.meaning: Having appointed/ordained — originally by show of hands; here apostolic appointment of elders.
Elders — the NT governing office in local churches, always in the plural for each congregation.
Fastings — self-denial joined to prayer for weighty spiritual decisions.
Entrusted, deposited — a banking term; the church is laid up in safe-keeping with the Lord.

26.meaning: Grace — the unmerited enabling favour of God, the alpha and omega of Christian ministry.
They fulfilled — completed, brought to the full measure; the work was finished, not merely attempted.

27.meaning: Door of faith — God's sovereign opening of saving access; key image of the chapter.
He opened — God is the opener of doors no man can shut (Rev 3:7-8).

meta.overview: Acts 15 stands as the theological watershed of the book. Men from Judea come down to Antioch teaching that Gentiles must be circumcised and keep the Law of Moses to be saved. Paul and Barnabas are sent up to Jerusalem with the question. In council the apostles and elders hear Peter recount the Spirit-wrought conversion of Cornelius, receive the missionary report of Paul and Barnabas, and hear James adjudicate by Amos 9 that God is taking out of the Gentiles a people for His name. The council issues a decree refusing the yoke of circumcision and specifying four abstinences that preserve table-fellowship with Jewish brethren. The letter is carried to Antioch by Judas and Silas, who confirm and exhort the church. A sharp contention then arises between Paul and Barnabas over John Mark, leading to their separation: Barnabas takes Mark to Cyprus, while Paul chooses Silas and is commended to the grace of God for the second missionary journey.
meta.theme: Saved by the Grace of the Lord Jesus — the Gospel Defined and Defended
meta.keyWordMeaning: grace (charis) — the unmerited saving favour of the Lord Jesus, by which both Jew and Gentile alike are saved (v.11).

outline.title: The Controversy at Antioch and the Journey to Jerusalem
Peter's Testimony: Saved by Grace, Not by the Yoke
Barnabas, Paul, and James's Judgment from Amos 9
The Apostolic Letter and Decree
Reception at Antioch: Joy and Exhortation
Paul and Barnabas Part; the Second Journey Begins

verses.kjv_text: And certain men which came down from Judaea taught the brethren, and said, Except ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, ye cannot be saved.
verses.original_text: Καί τινες κατελθόντες ἀπὸ τῆς Ἰουδαίας ἐδίδασκον τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς ὅτι Ἐὰν μὴ περιτμηθῆτε τῷ ἔθει τῷ Μωϋσέως, οὐ δύνασθε σωθῆναι.
verses.transliteration: Kai tines katelthontes apo tēs Ioudaias edidaskon tous adelphous hoti Ean mē peritmēthēte tō ethei tō Mōuseōs, ou dynasthe sōthēnai.
verses.study_note: "Certain men" — unauthorised teachers (cf. v.24, "we gave no such commandment"). Their error adds a legal condition to faith in Christ, turning grace into debt. This is the Galatian heresy (Gal 1:6-9; 5:2-4).
verses.doctrinal_note: Any addition to the gospel — circumcision, ritual, law-keeping — destroys the gospel. Salvation is "through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ" (v.11) plus nothing.

verses.kjv_text: When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them, they determined that Paul and Barnabas, and certain other of them, should go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders about this question.
verses.original_text: γενομένης δὲ στάσεως καὶ ζητήσεως οὐκ ὀλίγης τῷ Παύλῳ καὶ τῷ Βαρναβᾷ πρὸς αὐτοὺς ἔταξαν ἀναβαίνειν Παῦλον καὶ Βαρναβᾶν καί τινας ἄλλους ἐξ αὐτῶν πρὸς τοὺς ἀποστόλους καὶ πρεσβυτέρους εἰς Ἰερουσαλὴμ περὶ τοῦ ζητήματος τούτου.
verses.transliteration: genomenēs de staseōs kai zētēseōs ouk oligēs tō Paulō kai tō Barnaba pros autous etaxan anabainein Paulon kai Barnaban kai tinas allous ex autōn pros tous apostolous kai presbyterous eis Ierousalēm peri tou zētēmatos toutou.
verses.study_note: "No small dissension" — Paul defended the gospel publicly (cf. Gal 2:5, "to whom we gave place by subjection, no, not for an hour"). The Antioch church then sends a delegation to Jerusalem — the proper pattern for resolving doctrinal disputes through recognised apostolic authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: Doctrinal controversy is sometimes a duty, not a sin. The gospel must be contended for (Jude 3); silence where the truth is at stake is treason.

verses.kjv_text: And being brought on their way by the church, they passed through Phenice and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles: and they caused great joy unto all the brethren.
verses.original_text: οἱ μὲν οὖν προπεμφθέντες ὑπὸ τῆς ἐκκλησίας διήρχοντο τήν τε Φοινίκην καὶ Σαμάρειαν ἐκδιηγούμενοι τὴν ἐπιστροφὴν τῶν ἐθνῶν, καὶ ἐποίουν χαρὰν μεγάλην πᾶσιν τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς.
verses.transliteration: hoi men oun propemphthentes hypo tēs ekklēsias diērchonto tēn te Phoinikēn kai Samareian ekdiēgoumenoi tēn epistrophēn tōn ethnōn, kai epoioun charan megalēn pasin tois adelphois.
verses.study_note: "Conversion" is epistrophēn — turning, the same word as 14:15. The Samaritan churches (8:14-17) and Phoenician (11:19) had themselves begun in a mixed Jewish-Gentile setting; they rejoice in confirmation of God's wider work.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gentile conversion is not a problem to be lamented but a cause of joy to spiritually healthy saints. The gospel's worldwide triumph stirs every true believer to gladness.

verses.kjv_text: And when they were come to Jerusalem, they were received of the church, and of the apostles and elders, and they declared all things that God had done with them.
verses.original_text: παραγενόμενοι δὲ εἰς Ἰερουσαλὴμ παρεδέχθησαν ἀπὸ τῆς ἐκκλησίας καὶ τῶν ἀποστόλων καὶ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων, ἀνήγγειλάν τε ὅσα ὁ θεὸς ἐποίησεν μετ᾽ αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: paragenomenoi de eis Ierousalēm paredechthēsan apo tēs ekklēsias kai tōn apostolōn kai tōn presbyterōn, anēngeilan te hosa ho theos epoiēsen met autōn.
verses.study_note: The Antioch delegation is welcomed. Note Luke's recurrent phrase — "all that God did with them" (cf. 14:27) — the mission report always glorifies the Lord who works, not the workers.
verses.doctrinal_note: The honour of ministry belongs to God alone; true apostolic reports lift men's eyes from instruments to the sovereign Worker behind them.

verses.kjv_text: But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses.
verses.original_text: ἐξανέστησαν δέ τινες τῶν ἀπὸ τῆς αἱρέσεως τῶν Φαρισαίων πεπιστευκότες, λέγοντες ὅτι δεῖ περιτέμνειν αὐτοὺς παραγγέλλειν τε τηρεῖν τὸν νόμον Μωϋσέως.
verses.transliteration: exanestēsan de tines tōn apo tēs haireseōs tōn Pharisaiōn pepisteukotes, legontes hoti dei peritemnein autous parangellein te tērein ton nomon Mōuseōs.
verses.study_note: "Believed Pharisees" — a real but still Law-entangled group. They think the Gentiles must be "Jew-ized" before they can be "Christianised." The apostolic church must now distinguish the gospel from its Jewish cultural matrix.
verses.doctrinal_note: Even true believers may bring with them deep cultural assumptions that distort the gospel. The Spirit purifies the church's self-understanding by repeated doctrinal crises.

verses.kjv_text: And the apostles and elders came together for to consider of this matter.
verses.original_text: Συνήχθησάν τε οἱ ἀπόστολοι καὶ οἱ πρεσβύτεροι ἰδεῖν περὶ τοῦ λόγου τούτου.
verses.transliteration: Synēchthēsan te hoi apostoloi kai hoi presbyteroi idein peri tou logou toutou.
verses.study_note: The decision is made by apostles together with elders, not by apostles alone — the pattern for conciliar deliberation. "To consider" (idein) means "to see into, examine."
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ governs His church through Spirit-gifted leaders meeting in counsel, weighing Scripture and providence together under the Word.

verses.kjv_text: And when there had been much disputing, Peter rose up, and said unto them, Men and brethren, ye know how that a good while ago God made choice among us, that the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the word of the gospel, and believe.
verses.original_text: πολλῆς δὲ ζητήσεως γενομένης ἀναστὰς Πέτρος εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς· Ἄνδρες ἀδελφοί, ὑμεῖς ἐπίστασθε ὅτι ἀφ᾽ ἡμερῶν ἀρχαίων ἐν ὑμῖν ἐξελέξατο ὁ θεὸς διὰ τοῦ στόματός μου ἀκοῦσαι τὰ ἔθνη τὸν λόγον τοῦ εὐαγγελίου καὶ πιστεῦσαι.
verses.transliteration: pollēs de zētēseōs genomenēs anastas Petros eipen pros autous; Andres adelphoi, hymeis epistasthe hoti aph hēmerōn archaiōn en hymin exelexato ho theos dia tou stomatos mou akousai ta ethnē ton logon tou euangeliou kai pisteusai.
verses.study_note: Peter invokes the Cornelius precedent (Acts 10-11) — God's sovereign election "chose out" (exelexato) that Peter's mouth would open the Gentile door.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's election stands behind every step of mission. The one who chooses the end also chooses the instruments, the time, and the hearers.

verses.kjv_text: And God, which knoweth the hearts, bare them witness, giving them the Holy Ghost, even as he did unto us;
verses.original_text: καὶ ὁ καρδιογνώστης θεὸς ἐμαρτύρησεν αὐτοῖς δοὺς τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον καθὼς καὶ ἡμῖν,
verses.transliteration: kai ho kardiognōstēs theos emartyrēsen autois dous to pneuma to hagion kathōs kai hēmin,
verses.study_note: Kardiognōstēs ("heart-knower") also appears in 1:24, of the Lord choosing an apostle. God reads hearts, and His witness is the giving of the Spirit to Cornelius's household (10:44-47), equally as to the apostles at Pentecost.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gift of the Spirit, not external conformity to Law, is the authentic mark of saving faith. What God equally bestows the church cannot unequally treat.

verses.kjv_text: And put no difference between us and them, purifying their hearts by faith.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐδὲν διέκρινεν μεταξὺ ἡμῶν τε καὶ αὐτῶν, τῇ πίστει καθαρίσας τὰς καρδίας αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: kai ouden diekrinen metaxy hēmōn te kai autōn, tē pistei katharisas tas kardias autōn.
verses.study_note: "No difference" (ouden diekrinen) nullifies every ceremonial barrier. The heart is cleansed "by faith" (dative of means), not by circumcision. This is the doctrine that will saturate Rom 3-4, Gal 2-3, and Eph 2.
verses.doctrinal_note: Justification is by faith alone; heart-cleansing is by faith alone. Ceremonies cannot purify what only the blood of Christ, received by faith, makes clean.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore why tempt ye God, to put a yoke upon the neck of the disciples, which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear?
verses.original_text: νῦν οὖν τί πειράζετε τὸν θεόν, ἐπιθεῖναι ζυγὸν ἐπὶ τὸν τράχηλον τῶν μαθητῶν ὃν οὔτε οἱ πατέρες ἡμῶν οὔτε ἡμεῖς ἰσχύσαμεν βαστάσαι;
verses.transliteration: nyn oun ti peirazete ton theon, epitheinai zygon epi ton trachēlon tōn mathētōn hon oute hoi pateres hēmōn oute hēmeis ischysamen bastasai?
verses.study_note: To add the Law to the gospel is "tempting God" (peirazete) — testing His patience by contradicting His own revealed work. "Yoke" is Jesus' own word for the Pharisaic burden (Matt 23:4). No Israelite could bear it; why impose it on Gentiles?
verses.doctrinal_note: Legalism is not strictness but blasphemy — it demands what God does not, and implicitly accuses God of laying an unbearable yoke on His own people. Christ's yoke alone is easy (Matt 11:30).

verses.kjv_text: But we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ we shall be saved, even as they.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ διὰ τῆς χάριτος τοῦ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ πιστεύομεν σωθῆναι καθ᾽ ὃν τρόπον κἀκεῖνοι.
verses.transliteration: alla dia tēs charitos tou kyriou Iēsou pisteuomen sōthēnai kath hon tropon kakeinoi.
verses.study_note: The key verse — "through the grace of the Lord Jesus" (dia tēs charitos) — is the theological core of Acts 15. Peter declares that Jews are saved in the same way as Gentiles — not Gentiles like Jews, but Jews like Gentiles, by sheer grace through faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: There is one gospel, one way of salvation, for all sinners in every age: by grace, through faith, in Christ alone. The whole Reformation stands on the ground Peter takes here.

verses.kjv_text: Then all the multitude kept silence, and gave audience to Barnabas and Paul, declaring what miracles and wonders God had wrought among the Gentiles by them.
verses.original_text: ἐσίγησεν δὲ πᾶν τὸ πλῆθος, καὶ ἤκουον Βαρναβᾶ καὶ Παύλου ἐξηγουμένων ὅσα ἐποίησεν ὁ θεὸς σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα ἐν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν δι᾽ αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: esigēsen de pan to plēthos, kai ēkouon Barnaba kai Paulou exēgoumenōn hosa epoiēsen ho theos sēmeia kai terata en tois ethnesin di autōn.
verses.study_note: Silence (esigēsen) signals settled conviction. Peter's argument is reinforced by the missionary report — God has authenticated the Gentile mission by the same "signs and wonders" that attested the Twelve.
verses.doctrinal_note: Providential confirmation and apostolic teaching together yield assurance. God vindicates His truth by deed as well as word.

verses.kjv_text: And after they had held their peace, James answered, saying, Men and brethren, hearken unto me:
verses.original_text: Μετὰ δὲ τὸ σιγῆσαι αὐτοὺς ἀπεκρίθη Ἰάκωβος λέγων· Ἄνδρες ἀδελφοί, ἀκούσατέ μου.
verses.transliteration: Meta de to sigēsai autous apekrithē Iakōbos legōn; Andres adelphoi, akousate mou.
verses.study_note: James, the Lord's brother (Gal 1:19), presides as moderator — a man universally respected among Jewish believers. He speaks last because he is to render the decisive judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: Godly order places strongest voices at decisive moments. The one who will pronounce judgment listens longest.

verses.kjv_text: Simeon hath declared how God at the first did visit the Gentiles, to take out of them a people for his name.
verses.original_text: Συμεὼν ἐξηγήσατο καθὼς πρῶτον ὁ θεὸς ἐπεσκέψατο λαβεῖν ἐξ ἐθνῶν λαὸν τῷ ὀνόματι αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Symeōn exēgēsato kathōs prōton ho theos epeskepsato labein ex ethnōn laon tō onomati autou.
verses.study_note: "Simeon" is the Hebrew form of Peter's original name. "Visited" (epeskepsato) is the OT covenant verb (Gen 21:1; Luke 1:68). "A people for his name" — Gentiles are now gathered into covenant peoplehood not as proselytes to Judaism but directly to God.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's purpose from the foundation is to form one people from every nation, called by His name. The Gentile mission is not an afterthought but the fulfilment of ancient promise.

verses.kjv_text: And to this agree the words of the prophets; as it is written,
verses.original_text: καὶ τούτῳ συμφωνοῦσιν οἱ λόγοι τῶν προφητῶν, καθὼς γέγραπται·
verses.transliteration: kai toutō symphōnousin hoi logoi tōn prophētōn, kathōs gegraptai;
verses.study_note: "Agree" (symphōnousin) — harmonize, sound together. James subordinates present experience to written Scripture; the prophets must verify what Peter has recounted.
verses.doctrinal_note: Providence is interpreted by Scripture, not Scripture by providence. The written Word is the final court of appeal.

verses.kjv_text: After this I will return, and will build again the tabernacle of David, which is fallen down; and I will build again the ruins thereof, and I will set it up:
verses.original_text: Μετὰ ταῦτα ἀναστρέψω καὶ ἀνοικοδομήσω τὴν σκηνὴν Δαυὶδ τὴν πεπτωκυῖαν, καὶ τὰ κατεσκαμμένα αὐτῆς ἀνοικοδομήσω καὶ ἀνορθώσω αὐτήν,
verses.transliteration: Meta tauta anastrepsō kai anoikodomēsō tēn skēnēn Dauid tēn peptōkuian, kai ta kateskammena autēs anoikodomēsō kai anorthōsō autēn,
verses.study_note: Amos 9:11-12 (LXX). "Tabernacle of David" = Davidic kingdom-house; its rebuilding is fulfilled in the risen Christ enthroned (13:34) and in the gathering of Gentiles into David's reconstituted kingdom.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ the Son of David is now building His kingdom. Gentile conversion is Davidic restoration — the true Israel renewed around its King.

verses.kjv_text: That the residue of men might seek after the Lord, and all the Gentiles, upon whom my name is called, saith the Lord, who doeth all these things.
verses.original_text: ὅπως ἂν ἐκζητήσωσιν οἱ κατάλοιποι τῶν ἀνθρώπων τὸν κύριον, καὶ πάντα τὰ ἔθνη ἐφ᾽ οὓς ἐπικέκληται τὸ ὄνομά μου ἐπ᾽ αὐτούς, λέγει κύριος ποιῶν ταῦτα
verses.transliteration: hopōs an ekzētēsōsin hoi kataloipoi tōn anthrōpōn ton kyrion, kai panta ta ethnē eph hous epikeklētai to onoma mou ep autous, legei kyrios poiōn tauta
verses.study_note: "All the Gentiles upon whom my name is called" — God's name is now placed on the nations, formerly placed only on Israel (Deut 28:10). The Gentile inclusion fulfils prophecy, not frustrates it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The name-bearing people of God now embraces every nation under heaven. Christ's church is the true heir of the covenant promises to Israel.

verses.kjv_text: Known unto God are all his works from the beginning of the world.
verses.original_text: γνωστὰ ἀπ᾽ αἰῶνος.
verses.transliteration: gnōsta ap aiōnos.
verses.study_note: "Known from the ages" — God's purpose to save Gentiles through Messiah is not a reaction to events but His eternal design.
verses.doctrinal_note: Nothing the church faces is new to God. Every surprise in history is an unfolding of His eternal counsel.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore my sentence is, that we trouble not them, which from among the Gentiles are turned to God:
verses.original_text: διὸ ἐγὼ κρίνω μὴ παρενοχλεῖν τοῖς ἀπὸ τῶν ἐθνῶν ἐπιστρέφουσιν ἐπὶ τὸν θεόν,
verses.transliteration: dio egō krinō mē parenochlein tois apo tōn ethnōn epistrephousin epi ton theon,
verses.study_note: "My sentence" (egō krinō) — James's authoritative verdict. "Trouble not" (parenochlein) — "add further trouble to." The Gentiles already have enough to bear in forsaking their idols; do not add Mosaic ceremony.
verses.doctrinal_note: Pastoral wisdom refuses to heap upon new converts burdens God has not imposed. The church must preserve the simplicity that is in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: But that we write unto them, that they abstain from pollutions of idols, and from fornication, and from things strangled, and from blood.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ ἐπιστεῖλαι αὐτοῖς τοῦ ἀπέχεσθαι τῶν ἀλισγημάτων τῶν εἰδώλων καὶ τῆς πορνείας καὶ τοῦ πνικτοῦ καὶ τοῦ αἵματος.
verses.transliteration: alla episteilai autois tou apechesthai tōn alisgēmatōn tōn eidōlōn kai tēs porneias kai tou pniktou kai tou haimatos.
verses.study_note: Four abstinences: (1) pollutions of idols = meat sacrificed and eaten in idol temples (cf. 1 Cor 8-10); (2) fornication = pagan sexual immorality often linked to temple cults; (3) strangled things = animals not bled properly; (4) blood = consumption of blood. The list echoes Lev 17-18 obligations on the "stranger in the land," preserving table-fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian liberty is never license. Love curbs liberty for the sake of the brother's conscience and the church's unity.

verses.kjv_text: For Moses of old time hath in every city them that preach him, being read in the synagogues every sabbath day.
verses.original_text: Μωϋσῆς γὰρ ἐκ γενεῶν ἀρχαίων κατὰ πόλιν τοὺς κηρύσσοντας αὐτὸν ἔχει, ἐν ταῖς συναγωγαῖς κατὰ πᾶν σάββατον ἀναγινωσκόμενος.
verses.transliteration: Mōusēs gar ek geneōn archaiōn kata polin tous kēryssontas auton echei, en tais synagōgais kata pan sabbaton anaginōskomenos.
verses.study_note: Justification for the decree: Jewish sensibilities are widely represented in every city with a synagogue; these four abstinences preserve fellowship with Jewish brethren without imposing circumcision.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian freedom is exercised with awareness of one's neighbour. Wisdom voluntarily limits what faith enjoys for the sake of the weaker brother.

verses.kjv_text: Then pleased it the apostles and elders, with the whole church, to send chosen men of their own company to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas; namely, Judas surnamed Barsabas, and Silas, chief men among the brethren:
verses.original_text: Τότε ἔδοξε τοῖς ἀποστόλοις καὶ τοῖς πρεσβυτέροις σὺν ὅλῃ τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ ἐκλεξαμένους ἄνδρας ἐξ αὐτῶν πέμψαι εἰς Ἀντιόχειαν σὺν τῷ Παύλῳ καὶ Βαρναβᾷ, Ἰούδαν τὸν καλούμενον Βαρσαββᾶν καὶ Σιλᾶν, ἄνδρας ἡγουμένους ἐν τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς,
verses.transliteration: Tote edoxe tois apostolois kai tois presbyterois syn holē tē ekklēsia eklexamenous andras ex autōn pempsai eis Antiocheian syn tō Paulō kai Barnaba, Ioudan ton kaloumenon Barsabban kai Silan, andras hēgoumenous en tois adelphois,
verses.study_note: "Pleased" (edoxe) — the council's decision receives congregational concurrence. Silas (Silvanus) will become Paul's companion on the second journey (v.40) and co-author of Thessalonians; Judas Barsabbas (perhaps brother of Joseph Barsabbas, 1:23) is otherwise unknown.
verses.doctrinal_note: Apostolic decisions are received by the whole church. Leaders decide, but the body consents — mutual confirmation safeguards the truth.

verses.kjv_text: And they wrote letters by them after this manner; The apostles and elders and brethren send greeting unto the brethren which are of the Gentiles in Antioch and Syria and Cilicia:
verses.original_text: γράψαντες διὰ χειρὸς αὐτῶν· Οἱ ἀπόστολοι καὶ οἱ πρεσβύτεροι ἀδελφοὶ τοῖς κατὰ τὴν Ἀντιόχειαν καὶ Συρίαν καὶ Κιλικίαν ἀδελφοῖς τοῖς ἐξ ἐθνῶν χαίρειν.
verses.transliteration: grapsantes dia cheiros autōn; Hoi apostoloi kai hoi presbyteroi adelphoi tois kata tēn Antiocheian kai Syrian kai Kilikian adelphois tois ex ethnōn chairein.
verses.study_note: The conciliar letter is the NT's first extant synod document. "Brethren of the Gentiles" — the Gentiles are fully embraced as brothers.
verses.doctrinal_note: Kinship in Christ crosses every ethnic line. The church's documents and salutations must assume and promote this unity.

verses.kjv_text: Forasmuch as we have heard, that certain which went out from us have troubled you with words, subverting your souls, saying, Ye must be circumcised, and keep the law: to whom we gave no such commandment:
verses.original_text: Ἐπειδὴ ἠκούσαμεν ὅτι τινὲς ἐξ ἡμῶν ἐτάραξαν ὑμᾶς λόγοις ἀνασκευάζοντες τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν οἷς οὐ διεστειλάμεθα,
verses.transliteration: Epeidē ēkousamen hoti tines ex hēmōn etaraxan hymas logois anaskeuazontes tas psychas hymōn hois ou diesteilametha,
verses.study_note: "Subverting" (anaskeuazontes) — a military term for ransacking or unsettling. The Jerusalem church repudiates the Judaizers: they went out "from us" but carried "no such commandment" from the apostles.
verses.doctrinal_note: Self-appointed teachers, claiming apostolic backing, can devastate young churches. The true apostolic witness must expose and disown false authority.

verses.kjv_text: It seemed good unto us, being assembled with one accord, to send chosen men unto you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,
verses.original_text: ἔδοξεν ἡμῖν γενομένοις ὁμοθυμαδὸν ἐκλεξαμένοις ἄνδρας πέμψαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς σὺν τοῖς ἀγαπητοῖς ἡμῶν Βαρναβᾷ καὶ Παύλῳ,
verses.transliteration: edoxen hēmin genomenois homothymadon eklexamenois andras pempsai pros hymas syn tois agapētois hēmōn Barnaba kai Paulō,
verses.study_note: Homothymadon ("with one accord") is Luke's favourite church-unity word (1:14; 2:46; 4:24). "Beloved" endorses Paul and Barnabas against the slander that they preached a discount gospel.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Spirit produces unanimity in the church where Scripture is rightly weighed. Unanimity is the sweet fruit of truth settled.

verses.kjv_text: Men that have hazarded their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.original_text: ἀνθρώποις παραδεδωκόσι τὰς ψυχὰς αὐτῶν ὑπὲρ τοῦ ὀνόματος τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ.
verses.transliteration: anthrōpois paradedōkosi tas psychas autōn hyper tou onomatos tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou.
verses.study_note: "Hazarded" (paradedōkosi) — "having handed over" their lives. Paul had been stoned at Lystra (14:19); Barnabas had stood with him throughout.
verses.doctrinal_note: Faithful ministers have already staked their lives on the Name. Their testimony of suffering is itself credential of their fidelity.

verses.kjv_text: We have sent therefore Judas and Silas, who shall also tell you the same things by mouth.
verses.original_text: ἀπεστάλκαμεν οὖν Ἰούδαν καὶ Σιλᾶν, καὶ αὐτοὺς διὰ λόγου ἀπαγγέλλοντας τὰ αὐτά.
verses.transliteration: apestalkamen oun Ioudan kai Silan, kai autous dia logou apangellontas ta auta.
verses.study_note: Letter and oral witness together — the decree arrives by both written and spoken confirmation, Judas and Silas voicing in person what the church has written.
verses.doctrinal_note: God-given truth is safest when borne by tested messengers who can vocally confirm the written word. Letter alone can be misread; mouth without letter can be disputed.

verses.kjv_text: For it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things;
verses.original_text: ἔδοξεν γὰρ τῷ πνεύματι τῷ ἁγίῳ καὶ ἡμῖν μηδὲν πλέον ἐπιτίθεσθαι ὑμῖν βάρος πλὴν τῶν ἐπάναγκες τούτων,
verses.transliteration: edoxen gar tō pneumati tō hagiō kai hēmin mēden pleon epitithesthai hymin baros plēn tōn epanankes toutōn,
verses.study_note: "Seemed good to the Holy Ghost and to us" — a staggering claim of conciliar confidence, yet properly qualified: the Spirit guides genuine councils in genuine submission to Scripture.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Spirit works through, not apart from, the ordained assembly of Christ's church. When Scripture is honoured, the Spirit vindicates the verdict.

verses.kjv_text: That ye abstain from meats offered to idols, and from blood, and from things strangled, and from fornication: from which if ye keep yourselves, ye shall do well. Fare ye well.
verses.original_text: ἀπέχεσθαι εἰδωλοθύτων καὶ αἵματος καὶ πνικτῶν καὶ πορνείας· ἐξ ὧν διατηροῦντες ἑαυτοὺς εὖ πράξετε. Ἔρρωσθε.
verses.transliteration: apechesthai eidōlothytōn kai haimatos kai pniktōn kai porneias; ex hōn diatērountes heautous eu praxete. Errōsthe.
verses.study_note: The four abstinences repeated as the operative decree. "Fare ye well" (errōsthe) is the conventional letter closing — but here it carries real weight: obedience to the decree will bring welfare.
verses.doctrinal_note: Loving restraint on Christian liberty yields the church's long-term welfare. Obedient limits make durable unity.

verses.kjv_text: So when they were dismissed, they came to Antioch: and when they had gathered the multitude together, they delivered the epistle:
verses.original_text: Οἱ μὲν οὖν ἀπολυθέντες κατῆλθον εἰς Ἀντιόχειαν, καὶ συναγαγόντες τὸ πλῆθος ἐπέδωκαν τὴν ἐπιστολήν·
verses.transliteration: Hoi men oun apolythentes katēlthon eis Antiocheian, kai synagagontes to plēthos epedōkan tēn epistolēn;
verses.study_note: The full Antiochian assembly receives the decree — a congregational meeting, not a private delivery. The church's peace is restored publicly.
verses.doctrinal_note: What has disturbed the whole church must be settled before the whole church. Transparent restoration is the Spirit's way.

verses.kjv_text: Which when they had read, they rejoiced for the consolation.
verses.original_text: ἀναγνόντες δὲ ἐχάρησαν ἐπὶ τῇ παρακλήσει.
verses.transliteration: anagnontes de echarēsan epi tē paraklēsei.
verses.study_note: "Consolation" (paraklēsis) — literally "calling alongside, encouragement." The decree lifted the burden and relieved anxious consciences.
verses.doctrinal_note: Biblical liberty produces joy. Where grace is rightly preserved, the church sings.

verses.kjv_text: And Judas and Silas, being prophets also themselves, exhorted the brethren with many words, and confirmed them.
verses.original_text: Ἰούδας δὲ καὶ Σιλᾶς, καὶ αὐτοὶ προφῆται ὄντες, διὰ λόγου πολλοῦ παρεκάλεσαν τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς καὶ ἐπεστήριξαν·
verses.transliteration: Ioudas de kai Silas, kai autoi prophētai ontes, dia logou pollou parekalesan tous adelphous kai epestērixan;
verses.study_note: Silas's prophetic gift (cf. 13:1) suits him for apostolic companionship. Parekalesan ("exhorted") + epestērixan ("confirmed") is the classic pastoral pair.
verses.doctrinal_note: Prophetic exhortation strengthens saints. Settled doctrine yields durable courage only when personally applied to conscience.

verses.kjv_text: And after they had tarried there a space, they were let go in peace from the brethren unto the apostles.
verses.original_text: ποιήσαντες δὲ χρόνον ἀπελύθησαν μετ᾽ εἰρήνης ἀπὸ τῶν ἀδελφῶν πρὸς τοὺς ἀποστόλους.
verses.transliteration: poiēsantes de chronon apelythēsan met eirēnēs apo tōn adelphōn pros tous apostolous.
verses.study_note: The dispatch "in peace" signals restored fellowship. The crisis born of error has become an occasion of strengthened unity.
verses.doctrinal_note: Rightly handled controversy binds churches closer in love. Truth and peace go together (Zech 8:19).

verses.kjv_text: Paul also and Barnabas continued in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, with many others also.
verses.original_text: Παῦλος δὲ καὶ Βαρναβᾶς διέτριβον ἐν Ἀντιοχείᾳ διδάσκοντες καὶ εὐαγγελιζόμενοι μετὰ καὶ ἑτέρων πολλῶν τὸν λόγον τοῦ κυρίου.
verses.transliteration: Paulos de kai Barnabas dietribon en Antiocheia didaskontes kai euangelizomenoi meta kai heterōn pollōn ton logon tou kyriou.
verses.study_note: Antioch continues as a robust teaching center with many workers. The settled church is the seedbed of new missions.
verses.doctrinal_note: Mature teaching churches naturally produce missionaries. Where the Word is richly taught, feet are shod to carry it further.

verses.kjv_text: And some days after Paul said unto Barnabas, Let us go again and visit our brethren in every city where we have preached the word of the Lord, and see how they do.
verses.original_text: Μετὰ δέ τινας ἡμέρας εἶπεν πρὸς Βαρναβᾶν Παῦλος· Ἐπιστρέψαντες δὴ ἐπισκεψώμεθα τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς κατὰ πόλιν πᾶσαν ἐν αἷς κατηγγείλαμεν τὸν λόγον τοῦ κυρίου, πῶς ἔχουσιν.
verses.transliteration: Meta de tinas hēmeras eipen pros Barnaban Paulos; Epistrepsantes dē episkepsōmetha tous adelphous kata polin pasan en hais katēngeilamen ton logon tou kyriou, pōs echousin.
verses.study_note: Episkepsōmetha ("let us visit/oversee") — the root of "bishop." Paul's missionary vision includes follow-up of converted believers, not merely conversion.
verses.doctrinal_note: True gospel labour does not end at conversion. The shepherd's heart returns to see how the sheep fare.

verses.kjv_text: And Barnabas determined to take with them John, whose surname was Mark.
verses.original_text: Βαρναβᾶς δὲ ἐβούλετο συμπαραλαβεῖν καὶ τὸν Ἰωάννην τὸν καλούμενον Μᾶρκον·
verses.transliteration: Barnabas de ebouleto symparalabein kai ton Iōannēn ton kaloumenon Markon;
verses.study_note: Barnabas ("son of consolation") acts as his name: a mentor willing to restore the failed. Mark is his cousin (Col 4:10).
verses.doctrinal_note: Restoration of stumbled servants is the church's work. Where a man has fallen, grace-filled mentors must stoop to lift him.

verses.kjv_text: But Paul thought not good to take him with them, who departed from them from Pamphylia, and went not with them to the work.
verses.original_text: Παῦλος δὲ ἠξίου, τὸν ἀποστάντα ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν ἀπὸ Παμφυλίας καὶ μὴ συνελθόντα αὐτοῖς εἰς τὸ ἔργον, μὴ συμπαραλαμβάνειν τοῦτον.
verses.transliteration: Paulos de ēxiou, ton apostanta ap autōn apo Pamphylias kai mē synelthonta autois eis to ergon, mē symparalambanein touton.
verses.study_note: Paul remembered Mark's desertion at Pamphylia (13:13). The two honorable concerns — Barnabas's tender restoration and Paul's rigorous standard for tried workers — collide.
verses.doctrinal_note: Sincere servants may honourably differ on judgment calls. Both mercy and prudence are virtues; neither may absolutize itself.

verses.kjv_text: And the contention was so sharp between them, that they departed asunder one from the other: and so Barnabas took Mark, and sailed unto Cyprus;
verses.original_text: ἐγένετο δὲ παροξυσμὸς ὥστε ἀποχωρισθῆναι αὐτοὺς ἀπ᾽ ἀλλήλων, τόν τε Βαρναβᾶν παραλαβόντα τὸν Μᾶρκον ἐκπλεῦσαι εἰς Κύπρον.
verses.transliteration: egeneto de paroxysmos hōste apochōristhēnai autous ap allēlōn, ton te Barnaban paralabonta ton Markon ekpleusai eis Kypron.
verses.study_note: Paroxysmos ("sharp contention") — a strong word, honest about the heat of the disagreement. Yet both men continue faithful in separate labour; Mark is later fully restored (2 Tim 4:11 — "profitable to me for the ministry") and writes the Gospel bearing his name.
verses.doctrinal_note: Even separations among the saints are overruled by Providence to multiply mission. God makes even disagreement bear fruit when both parties remain true to the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And Paul chose Silas, and departed, being recommended by the brethren unto the grace of God.
verses.original_text: Παῦλος δὲ ἐπιλεξάμενος Σιλᾶν ἐξῆλθεν παραδοθεὶς τῇ χάριτι τοῦ κυρίου ὑπὸ τῶν ἀδελφῶν,
verses.transliteration: Paulos de epilexamenos Silan exēlthen paradotheis tē chariti tou kyriou hypo tōn adelphōn,
verses.study_note: "Recommended to the grace of God" — the same phrase as 14:26, framing the second journey as another grace-enterprise. Silas brings prophetic gift and Roman citizenship (16:37) to the team.
verses.doctrinal_note: Every new apostolic venture begins and ends in grace. The church's commendation is simply to place workers in God's hand.

verses.kjv_text: And he went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the churches.
verses.original_text: διήρχετο δὲ τὴν Συρίαν καὶ Κιλικίαν ἐπιστηρίζων τὰς ἐκκλησίας.
verses.transliteration: diērcheto de tēn Syrian kai Kilikian epistērizōn tas ekklēsias.
verses.study_note: Paul begins the second journey by strengthening the churches of his home region — Cilicia (around Tarsus) had been evangelised during his earlier Tarsus years (9:30).
verses.doctrinal_note: True mission strengthens before it expands. The apostolic mind cares for the churches already planted even while opening new fields.

1.meaning: Be circumcised — OT covenant sign (Gen 17); here wrongly required for salvation of Gentiles.
To be saved — the deliverance from sin and wrath accomplished by Christ, here falsely conditioned on Mosaic obedience.

2.meaning: Dissension, standing-off — heated public disagreement; the gospel sometimes requires confrontation.
Debate, inquiry — formal theological disputation, not mere quarrelling.
Elders — the governing body alongside the apostles; conciliar authority already present.

3.meaning: Conversion, turning — the radical Godward reorientation of pagan lives.

4.meaning: Were welcomed — received with approval and fellowship.

5.meaning: Sect, party — here the Pharisee party within the believing community; not yet pejorative.

6.meaning: To examine, consider — the council's mandate is investigation before pronouncement.

7.meaning: Chose out, elected — God's sovereign selection of Peter as the Gentile-door-opener.

8.meaning: Heart-knower — a divine title; God reads hearts and needs no external rites to judge them.

9.meaning: Distinguished, made a difference — God refused to separate Jew from Gentile in grace.
Having cleansed — moral/spiritual purification; the heart cleansed by faith needs no ritual.

10.meaning: Test, tempt — provoke God by contradicting His revealed grace.
Yoke — the Pharisaic Law-burden (Matt 23:4); Christ's yoke is easy (Matt 11:30).

11.meaning: Grace — the chapter's keyword; God's unmerited saving favor received through faith alone.
We believe — personal trust; the universal means of saving appropriation for all peoples alike.

12.meaning: Signs and wonders — God's public authentication of the Gentile mission (Heb 2:4).

13.meaning: James (the Lord's brother) — respected elder who presides as council moderator (Gal 1:19).

14.meaning: Simeon — Hebrew form of Peter's original name, honouring him as Jewish apostle.
Visited — OT covenant verb (Gen 21:1; Luke 1:68); God has visited the Gentiles in grace.
A people — covenant-people language formerly reserved for Israel, now extended to Gentiles.

15.meaning: Agree, harmonize — prophetic Scripture sounds together with Spirit-given providence.

16.meaning: Tabernacle/tent of David — the fallen Davidic kingdom-house, rebuilt in Christ the Son of David.
Rebuild, restore — Christ is restoring David's kingdom by gathering the nations into His rule.

17.meaning: Earnestly seek out — the Godward seeking of the nations prophesied by Amos.

18.meaning: Known things — God's purposes have been eternally known to Him; nothing is improvised.

19.meaning: I judge — James's authoritative verdict as council chair.
Trouble further, harass — James will not pile additional burdens on Gentile converts.

20.meaning: Abstain, hold oneself away from — the positive command of the decree.
Pollutions of idols — meat sacrificed and eaten in temple-feasts.
Fornication — every form of sexual immorality, especially the cultic prostitution endemic to pagan shrines.
Strangled — animals killed without draining blood, forbidden from Lev 17.
Blood — consumption of blood forbidden in Gen 9:4 and Lev 17:10-14.

22.meaning: With one accord — Luke's signature word for Spirit-wrought church unity.

24.meaning: Subverting, ransacking — violently unsettling souls with false teaching.

26.meaning: Having handed over their lives — a phrase of consecrated risk for Christ's name.

28.meaning: Necessary, indispensable — the four abstinences are the irreducible minimum for fellowship.

31.meaning: Consolation, encouragement — the decree lifted anxious consciences into joy.

32.meaning: Confirmed, strengthened upon — pastoral fortification of faith.

36.meaning: Let us visit/oversee — root of episkopos (bishop); missionary oversight.

39.meaning: Sharp contention — a "paroxysm" of disagreement, strong but not deadly to either servant.

40.meaning: Grace — the commendation formula framing apostolic mission.

41.meaning: Strengthening upon — the recurrent Lukan pastoral verb for follow-up ministry.

meta.overview: Acts 16 opens the second missionary journey's decisive advance: Paul circumcises Timothy at Lystra for strategic witness, strengthens the churches, and, forbidden by the Holy Spirit to enter Asia or Bithynia, is summoned to Macedonia by a night vision. Landing at Neapolis and proceeding to Philippi — a Roman colony — the apostles meet Lydia by the river; her heart is opened by the Lord and she becomes the first European convert. A slave-girl with a python-spirit is delivered; her owners, losing their profits, drag Paul and Silas before the magistrates, who scourge and imprison them. At midnight the apostles sing, an earthquake shakes the foundations, and the jailer, about to kill himself, is converted with his whole household. The magistrates, learning they have beaten Roman citizens, beg the apostles to depart. The European church is born through sovereign guidance, household conversions, and public vindication of God's servants.
meta.theme: The Gospel Crosses into Europe
meta.keyWordMeaning: cross over (diabas) — the Spirit-guided crossing of the gospel from Asia into Europe by way of Macedonia (v.9).

outline.title: Timothy Joins the Mission; Churches Strengthened
The Spirit Forbids; the Macedonian Call
Lydia: the First European Convert
The Python-Spirit Cast Out; Paul and Silas Imprisoned
The Midnight Earthquake and the Jailer's Salvation
Roman Citizenship Vindicated; Departure

verses.kjv_text: Then came he to Derbe and Lystra: and, behold, a certain disciple was there, named Timotheus, the son of a certain woman, which was a Jewess, and believed; but his father was a Greek:
verses.original_text: Κατήντησεν δὲ εἰς Δέρβην καὶ εἰς Λύστραν. καὶ ἰδοὺ μαθητής τις ἦν ἐκεῖ ὀνόματι Τιμόθεος, υἱὸς γυναικὸς Ἰουδαίας πιστῆς, πατρὸς δὲ Ἕλληνος,
verses.transliteration: Katēntēsen de eis Derbēn kai eis Lystran. kai idou mathētēs tis ēn ekei onomati Timotheos, huios gynaikos Ioudaias pistēs, patros de Hellēnos,
verses.study_note: Timothy's mother is Eunice and grandmother Lois (2 Tim 1:5); both believing Jewish women nurtured him in the Scriptures from childhood (2 Tim 3:15). His Greek father explains why he was uncircumcised.
verses.doctrinal_note: Godly mothers and grandmothers are often the hidden architects of church leadership. The faith of unbelieving fathers does not quench what believing mothers sow.

verses.kjv_text: Which was well reported of by the brethren that were at Lystra and Iconium.
verses.original_text: ὃς ἐμαρτυρεῖτο ὑπὸ τῶν ἐν Λύστροις καὶ Ἰκονίῳ ἀδελφῶν.
verses.transliteration: hos emartyreito hypo tōn en Lystrois kai Ikoniō adelphōn.
verses.study_note: "Well reported" (emartyreito) — the Pauline standard for eldership (1 Tim 3:7). Timothy's reputation extended beyond his home city.
verses.doctrinal_note: Public testimony by the brethren is a prerequisite for public ministry. The church's verdict on a man's character outweighs his own self-assessment.

verses.kjv_text: Him would Paul have to go forth with him; and took and circumcised him because of the Jews which were in those quarters: for they knew all that his father was a Greek.
verses.original_text: τοῦτον ἠθέλησεν ὁ Παῦλος σὺν αὐτῷ ἐξελθεῖν, καὶ λαβὼν περιέτεμεν αὐτὸν διὰ τοὺς Ἰουδαίους τοὺς ὄντας ἐν τοῖς τόποις ἐκείνοις, ᾔδεισαν γὰρ ἅπαντες ὅτι Ἕλλην ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ ὑπῆρχεν.
verses.transliteration: touton ēthelēsen ho Paulos syn autō exelthein, kai labōn perietemen auton dia tous Ioudaious tous ontas en tois topois ekeinois, ēdeisan gar hapantes hoti Hellēn ho patēr autou hypērchen.
verses.study_note: Strategic, not legal. Paul had just refused to circumcise Titus as a Gentile at Jerusalem (Gal 2:3); here he circumcises Timothy, half-Jewish by his mother, to remove a needless barrier to synagogue witness. "I am made all things to all men" (1 Cor 9:20-22).
verses.doctrinal_note: Doctrine forbids circumcision as necessary for salvation; mission may accept it as expedient for a hearing. Liberty in Christ uses liberty for the gospel, not against it.

verses.kjv_text: And as they went through the cities, they delivered them the decrees for to keep, that were ordained of the apostles and elders which were at Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ διεπορεύοντο τὰς πόλεις, παρεδίδοσαν αὐτοῖς φυλάσσειν τὰ δόγματα τὰ κεκριμένα ὑπὸ τῶν ἀποστόλων καὶ πρεσβυτέρων τῶν ἐν Ἱεροσολύμοις.
verses.transliteration: hōs de dieporeuonto tas poleis, paredidosan autois phylassein ta dogmata ta kekrimena hypo tōn apostolōn kai presbyterōn tōn en Hierosolymois.
verses.study_note: "Decrees" (dogmata) — the rulings of the Jerusalem Council (ch. 15). Paul actively propagates the conciliar decisions, demonstrating his submission to the church's ordered authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle to the Gentiles honoured decisions made conciliarly at Jerusalem. Rightly ordered church authority is the friend, not enemy, of missionary liberty.

verses.kjv_text: And so were the churches established in the faith, and increased in number daily.
verses.original_text: αἱ μὲν οὖν ἐκκλησίαι ἐστερεοῦντο τῇ πίστει καὶ ἐπερίσσευον τῷ ἀριθμῷ καθ᾽ ἡμέραν.
verses.transliteration: hai men oun ekklēsiai estereounto tē pistei kai eperisseuon tō arithmō kath hēmeran.
verses.study_note: Double growth — doctrinal stability (estereounto, "made firm") and numerical increase. Luke's progress reports (cf. 2:47; 9:31; 12:24) mark each phase.
verses.doctrinal_note: Healthy churches grow simultaneously in depth and in breadth. Doctrinal establishment and evangelistic multiplication are twin fruits of the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: Now when they had gone throughout Phrygia and the region of Galatia, and were forbidden of the Holy Ghost to preach the word in Asia,
verses.original_text: Διῆλθον δὲ τὴν Φρυγίαν καὶ Γαλατικὴν χώραν, κωλυθέντες ὑπὸ τοῦ ἁγίου πνεύματος λαλῆσαι τὸν λόγον ἐν τῇ Ἀσίᾳ·
verses.transliteration: Diēlthon de tēn Phrygian kai Galatikēn chōran, kōlythentes hypo tou hagiou pneumatos lalēsai ton logon en tē Asia;
verses.study_note: The Spirit closes doors as well as opens them. "Asia" here is the Roman province on the western Anatolian coast, including Ephesus — which will be opened on the third journey (19:1).
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine "no" is as much a revelation of Christ's will as divine "yes." Closed doors prepare for appointed doors.

verses.kjv_text: After they were come to Mysia, they assayed to go into Bithynia: but the Spirit suffered them not.
verses.original_text: ἐλθόντες δὲ κατὰ τὴν Μυσίαν ἐπείραζον εἰς τὴν Βιθυνίαν πορευθῆναι, καὶ οὐκ εἴασεν αὐτοὺς τὸ πνεῦμα Ἰησοῦ.
verses.transliteration: elthontes de kata tēn Mysian epeirazon eis tēn Bithynian poreuthēnai, kai ouk eiasen autous to pneuma Iēsou.
verses.study_note: The oldest texts read "Spirit of Jesus" (to pneuma Iēsou), a striking Christological witness — the risen Lord guides His mission through His Spirit.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Spirit of Jesus superintends every step of Christ's mission. Sovereign restraint is Christly tenderness toward both apostle and appointed flock.

verses.kjv_text: And they passing by Mysia came down to Troas.
verses.original_text: παρελθόντες δὲ τὴν Μυσίαν κατέβησαν εἰς Τρῳάδα.
verses.transliteration: parelthontes de tēn Mysian katebēsan eis Trōada.
verses.study_note: Troas, a seaport facing Europe across the Aegean, is the last major Asian station. Here Luke may join Paul — the "we" narratives begin in v.10.
verses.doctrinal_note: Providence funnels servants to exactly the place where the Master's next word will meet them.

verses.kjv_text: And a vision appeared to Paul in the night; There stood a man of Macedonia, and prayed him, saying, Come over into Macedonia, and help us.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅραμα διὰ νυκτὸς τῷ Παύλῳ ὤφθη, ἀνὴρ Μακεδών τις ἑστὼς καὶ παρακαλῶν αὐτὸν καὶ λέγων· Διαβὰς εἰς Μακεδονίαν βοήθησον ἡμῖν.
verses.transliteration: kai horama dia nyktos tō Paulō ōphthē, anēr Makedōn tis hestōs kai parakalōn auton kai legōn; Diabas eis Makedonian boēthēson hēmin.
verses.study_note: The Macedonian call (diabas — "cross over") redraws the map of salvation history: the gospel leaves Asia and enters Europe. "Help us" (boēthēson) is a cry of desperate need answered by sovereign grace.
verses.doctrinal_note: The West's gospel heritage began as answer to a midnight plea. The church owes no nation its message as payment; it owes every nation its message as debt (Rom 1:14).

verses.kjv_text: And after he had seen the vision, immediately we endeavoured to go into Macedonia, assuredly gathering that the Lord had called us for to preach the gospel unto them.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ τὸ ὅραμα εἶδεν, εὐθέως ἐζητήσαμεν ἐξελθεῖν εἰς Μακεδονίαν, συμβιβάζοντες ὅτι προσκέκληται ἡμᾶς ὁ θεὸς εὐαγγελίσασθαι αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: hōs de to horama eiden, eutheōs ezētēsamen exelthein eis Makedonian, symbibazontes hoti proskeklētai hēmas ho theos euangelisasthai autous.
verses.study_note: The first "we" passage begins — Luke is now with the party. Symbibazontes, "joining together" the evidences, is the patient deduction of God's will from His guidance.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine guidance is discerned by assembling revelation, providence, and Spirit-led reasoning. God does not bypass the mind; He informs it.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore loosing from Troas, we came with a straight course to Samothracia, and the next day to Neapolis;
verses.original_text: Ἀναχθέντες οὖν ἀπὸ Τρῳάδος εὐθυδρομήσαμεν εἰς Σαμοθρᾴκην, τῇ δὲ ἐπιούσῃ εἰς Νέαν Πόλιν,
verses.transliteration: Anachthentes oun apo Trōados euthydromēsamen eis Samothrakēn, tē de epiousē eis Nean Polin,
verses.study_note: Euthydromēsamen, "ran a straight course" — a favourable wind delivered them rapidly. The return voyage later (20:6) took five days against headwinds.
verses.doctrinal_note: Providence signs its purposes in favourable winds and in favourable laws, in public vindications and in private earthquakes. God prospers what He appoints.

verses.kjv_text: And from thence to Philippi, which is the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a colony: and we were in that city abiding certain days.
verses.original_text: κἀκεῖθεν εἰς Φιλίππους, ἥτις ἐστὶν πρώτη τῆς μερίδος Μακεδονίας πόλις, κολωνία. ἦμεν δὲ ἐν αὐτῇ τῇ πόλει διατρίβοντες ἡμέρας τινάς.
verses.transliteration: kakeithen eis Philippous, hētis estin prōtē tēs meridos Makedonias polis, kolōnia. ēmen de en autē tē polei diatribontes hēmeras tinas.
verses.study_note: Philippi was a Roman colony since 42 BC, populated by veterans; inhabitants held Roman citizenship. Paul's later letter to this church reflects warm memory of its origins.
verses.doctrinal_note: God chose a Roman military town for Europe's first church — demonstrating that no stronghold of human empire is fortified against the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And on the sabbath we went out of the city by a river side, where prayer was wont to be made; and we sat down, and spake unto the women which resorted thither.
verses.original_text: τῇ τε ἡμέρᾳ τῶν σαββάτων ἐξήλθομεν ἔξω τῆς πόλεως παρὰ ποταμὸν οὗ ἐνομίζομεν προσευχὴν εἶναι, καὶ καθίσαντες ἐλαλοῦμεν ταῖς συνελθούσαις γυναιξίν.
verses.transliteration: tē te hēmera tōn sabbatōn exēlthomen exō tēs poleōs para potamon hou enomizomen proseuchēn einai, kai kathisantes elaloumen tais synelthousais gynaixin.
verses.study_note: No synagogue existed (10 adult Jewish men were required to form one), so Paul found the informal "place of prayer" (proseuchēn) by the Gangites river. Only women had gathered; the gospel comes to them first.
verses.doctrinal_note: God dignifies Europe's first converts as women. The gospel honours what the ancient world often despised; grace seeks the humblest lip to plant itself.

verses.kjv_text: And a certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple, of the city of Thyatira, which worshipped God, heard us: whose heart the Lord opened, that she attended unto the things which were spoken of Paul.
verses.original_text: καί τις γυνὴ ὀνόματι Λυδία, πορφυρόπωλις πόλεως Θυατείρων σεβομένη τὸν θεόν, ἤκουεν, ἧς ὁ κύριος διήνοιξεν τὴν καρδίαν προσέχειν τοῖς λαλουμένοις ὑπὸ τοῦ Παύλου.
verses.transliteration: kai tis gynē onomati Lydia, porphyropōlis poleōs Thyateirōn sebomenē ton theon, ēkouen, hēs ho kyrios diēnoixen tēn kardian prosechein tois laloumenois hypo tou Paulou.
verses.study_note: Lydia was a Gentile God-fearer (sebomenē), a wealthy trader in purple dye from Thyatira. Diēnoixen, "opened" — the same word used of Jesus opening the disciples' understanding (Luke 24:45). Regeneration is the Lord's sovereign work upon the heart, preceding and producing attentive faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: Salvation begins with God's unilateral heart-opening. No preacher can pry open a sinner's heart; only the Lord opens what sin has shut.

verses.kjv_text: And when she was baptized, and her household, she besought us, saying, If ye have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house, and abide there. And she constrained us.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ἐβαπτίσθη καὶ ὁ οἶκος αὐτῆς, παρεκάλεσεν λέγουσα· Εἰ κεκρίκατέ με πιστὴν τῷ κυρίῳ εἶναι, εἰσελθόντες εἰς τὸν οἶκόν μου μένετε· καὶ παρεβιάσατο ἡμᾶς.
verses.transliteration: hōs de ebaptisthē kai ho oikos autēs, parekalesen legousa; Ei kekrikate me pistēn tō kyriō einai, eiselthontes eis ton oikon mou menete; kai parebiasato hēmas.
verses.study_note: Household baptism follows conversion (cf. 16:33; 18:8). Parebiasato, "constrained," is urgent insistence — the first fruit of grace is eager hospitality.
verses.doctrinal_note: Conversion reorders the home. Grace always expresses itself in open doors, opened purses, and a hospitality bent on sheltering Christ's servants.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as we went to prayer, a certain damsel possessed with a spirit of divination met us, which brought her masters much gain by soothsaying:
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ πορευομένων ἡμῶν εἰς τὴν προσευχὴν παιδίσκην τινὰ ἔχουσαν πνεῦμα πύθωνα ὑπαντῆσαι ἡμῖν, ἥτις ἐργασίαν πολλὴν παρεῖχεν τοῖς κυρίοις αὐτῆς μαντευομένη.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de poreuomenōn hēmōn eis tēn proseuchēn paidiskēn tina echousan pneuma pythōna hypantēsai hēmin, hētis ergasian pollēn pareichen tois kyriois autēs manteuomenē.
verses.study_note: Pythōna — the python-spirit of the Delphic oracle, linked to Apollo. Pagan divination is consistently traced by Scripture to demonic sources (Deut 18:10-12; 1 Cor 10:20).
verses.doctrinal_note: Occult powers are real, unclean, and enslaving. The gospel confronts them not by negotiation but by expulsion in Christ's name.

verses.kjv_text: The same followed Paul and us, and cried, saying, These men are the servants of the most high God, which shew unto us the way of salvation.
verses.original_text: αὕτη κατακολουθοῦσα τῷ Παύλῳ καὶ ἡμῖν ἔκραζεν λέγουσα· Οὗτοι οἱ ἄνθρωποι δοῦλοι τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ὑψίστου εἰσίν, οἵτινες καταγγέλλουσιν ὑμῖν ὁδὸν σωτηρίας.
verses.transliteration: hautē katakolouthousa tō Paulō kai hēmin ekrazen legousa; Houtoi hoi anthrōpoi douloi tou theou tou hypsistou eisin, hoitines katangellousin hymin hodon sōtērias.
verses.study_note: Demonic testimony can be factually accurate yet morally corrupt (cf. Mark 1:24-25). Paul refuses Satan's advertising as Jesus refused the demons'.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel does not need the endorsement of Satan's mouthpieces. Truth advertised by devils is still devilish; Christ's witnesses are a church-made voice.

verses.kjv_text: And this did she many days. But Paul, being grieved, turned and said to the spirit, I command thee in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her. And he came out the same hour.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἐποίει ἐπὶ πολλὰς ἡμέρας. διαπονηθεὶς δὲ Παῦλος καὶ ἐπιστρέψας τῷ πνεύματι εἶπεν· Παραγγέλλω σοι ἐν ὀνόματι Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐξελθεῖν ἀπ᾽ αὐτῆς· καὶ ἐξῆλθεν αὐτῇ τῇ ὥρᾳ.
verses.transliteration: touto de epoiei epi pollas hēmeras. diaponētheis de Paulos kai epistrepsas tō pneumati eipen; Parangellō soi en onomati Iēsou Christou exelthein ap autēs; kai exēlthen autē tē hōra.
verses.study_note: Diaponētheis, "being distressed/annoyed through and through," marks Paul's moral burden. The exorcism is instantaneous — "the same hour."
verses.doctrinal_note: Christ's name, on the lips of His servants, routs the strongest demons. Authority belongs to the Name, not the man who names it.

verses.kjv_text: And when her masters saw that the hope of their gains was gone, they caught Paul and Silas, and drew them into the marketplace unto the rulers,
verses.original_text: ἰδόντες δὲ οἱ κύριοι αὐτῆς ὅτι ἐξῆλθεν ἡ ἐλπὶς τῆς ἐργασίας αὐτῶν, ἐπιλαβόμενοι τὸν Παῦλον καὶ τὸν Σιλᾶν εἵλκυσαν εἰς τὴν ἀγορὰν ἐπὶ τοὺς ἄρχοντας,
verses.transliteration: idontes de hoi kyrioi autēs hoti exēlthen hē elpis tēs ergasias autōn, epilabomenoi ton Paulon kai ton Silan heilkysan eis tēn agoran epi tous archontas,
verses.study_note: The motive is purely commercial — idolatry and avarice conspire wherever the gospel threatens exploitation. Note "hope of their gains was gone": Christ dethrones mammon by delivering its victims.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel threatens any economic system built on human bondage. Where Christ reigns, false markets collapse.

verses.kjv_text: And brought them to the magistrates, saying, These men, being Jews, do exceedingly trouble our city,
verses.original_text: καὶ προσαγαγόντες αὐτοὺς τοῖς στρατηγοῖς εἶπαν· Οὗτοι οἱ ἄνθρωποι ἐκταράσσουσιν ἡμῶν τὴν πόλιν Ἰουδαῖοι ὑπάρχοντες,
verses.transliteration: kai prosagagontes autous tois stratēgois eipan; Houtoi hoi anthrōpoi ektarassousin hēmōn tēn polin Ioudaioi hyparchontes,
verses.study_note: Stratēgoi (literally "generals") — the Roman duoviri who governed the colony. The accusation exploits Roman suspicion of Jewish agitators (cf. Claudius's expulsion, 18:2).
verses.doctrinal_note: Gospel faithfulness often meets false charges dressed as patriotism. Satan accuses with political pretext where he cannot refute with truth.

verses.kjv_text: And teach customs, which are not lawful for us to receive, neither to observe, being Romans.
verses.original_text: καὶ καταγγέλλουσιν ἔθη ἃ οὐκ ἔξεστιν ἡμῖν παραδέχεσθαι οὐδὲ ποιεῖν Ῥωμαίοις οὖσιν.
verses.transliteration: kai katangellousin ethē ha ouk exestin hēmin paradechesthai oude poiein Rhōmaiois ousin.
verses.study_note: Roman citizens were technically forbidden to adopt foreign religions that undermined state cult. The charge, though exaggerated, had legal teeth in a colony.
verses.doctrinal_note: The world often makes Christian loyalty appear a political liability. Yet Christ's true subjects are the safest citizens of every commonwealth.

verses.kjv_text: And the multitude rose up together against them: and the magistrates rent off their clothes, and commanded to beat them.
verses.original_text: καὶ συνεπέστη ὁ ὄχλος κατ᾽ αὐτῶν, καὶ οἱ στρατηγοὶ περιρήξαντες αὐτῶν τὰ ἱμάτια ἐκέλευον ῥαβδίζειν,
verses.transliteration: kai synepestē ho ochlos kat autōn, kai hoi stratēgoi perirēxantes autōn ta himatia ekeleuon rhabdizein,
verses.study_note: Rhabdizein is the Roman flogging with rods, carried out by lictors. Paul alludes to this in 2 Cor 11:25 ("thrice was I beaten with rods"). The magistrates acted without due process.
verses.doctrinal_note: Where mob passion rules, justice is trampled. The Lord permits His servants to suffer even institutional miscarriage to magnify His name.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had laid many stripes upon them, they cast them into prison, charging the jailor to keep them safely:
verses.original_text: πολλάς τε ἐπιθέντες αὐτοῖς πληγὰς ἔβαλον εἰς φυλακήν, παραγγείλαντες τῷ δεσμοφύλακι ἀσφαλῶς τηρεῖν αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: pollas te epithentes autois plēgas ebalon eis phylakēn, parangeilantes tō desmophylaki asphalōs tērein autous.
verses.study_note: "Many stripes" — no legal limit. The wounds would be untreated; their backs would be raw when cast into the inner cell.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Christian is sometimes delivered from suffering, sometimes delivered through it. Either way the Lord is exalted.

verses.kjv_text: Who, having received such a charge, thrust them into the inner prison, and made their feet fast in the stocks.
verses.original_text: ὃς παραγγελίαν τοιαύτην λαβὼν ἔβαλεν αὐτοὺς εἰς τὴν ἐσωτέραν φυλακὴν καὶ τοὺς πόδας ἠσφαλίσατο αὐτῶν εἰς τὸ ξύλον.
verses.transliteration: hos parangelian toiautēn labōn ebalen autous eis tēn esōteran phylakēn kai tous podas ēsphalisato autōn eis to xylon.
verses.study_note: The inner prison was typically windowless and damp. Xylon (wooden stocks) forced the legs painfully apart. This is the cruel night that becomes a singing night.
verses.doctrinal_note: No darkness is dark enough to quench the hymn of faith. The inner prison becomes the outer concert-hall of praise.

verses.kjv_text: And at midnight Paul and Silas prayed, and sang praises unto God: and the prisoners heard them.
verses.original_text: Κατὰ δὲ τὸ μεσονύκτιον Παῦλος καὶ Σιλᾶς προσευχόμενοι ὕμνουν τὸν θεόν, ἐπηκροῶντο δὲ αὐτῶν οἱ δέσμιοι·
verses.transliteration: Kata de to mesonyktion Paulos kai Silas proseuchomenoi hymnoun ton theon, epēkroōnto de autōn hoi desmioi;
verses.study_note: Hymnoun, "hymning" — formal praise, perhaps psalms. Epēkroōnto, "listened attentively," suggests rapt silence rather than mere overhearing; the prisoners had never heard music in a dungeon before.
verses.doctrinal_note: Midnight worship in bonds is a sharper sermon than daylight eloquence from pulpits. Suffering saints singing to God evangelize those who cannot yet speak His name.

verses.kjv_text: And suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken: and immediately all the doors were opened, and every one's bands were loosed.
verses.original_text: ἄφνω δὲ σεισμὸς ἐγένετο μέγας ὥστε σαλευθῆναι τὰ θεμέλια τοῦ δεσμωτηρίου, ἠνεῴχθησαν δὲ παραχρῆμα αἱ θύραι πᾶσαι, καὶ πάντων τὰ δεσμὰ ἀνέθη.
verses.transliteration: aphnō de seismos egeneto megas hōste saleuthēnai ta themelia tou desmōtēriou, ēneōchthēsan de parachrēma hai thyrai pasai, kai pantōn ta desma anethē.
verses.study_note: The earthquake is selectively miraculous — doors opened, chains loosed, yet no one fled (v.28). This is the finger of God, not geology alone.
verses.doctrinal_note: God shakes the foundations of prisons to free His people and reach His lost. Natural forces obey the prayers of saints.

verses.kjv_text: And the keeper of the prison awaking out of his sleep, and seeing the prison doors open, he drew out his sword, and would have killed himself, supposing that the prisoners had been fled.
verses.original_text: ἔξυπνος δὲ γενόμενος ὁ δεσμοφύλαξ καὶ ἰδὼν ἀνεῳγμένας τὰς θύρας τῆς φυλακῆς, σπασάμενος τὴν μάχαιραν ἤμελλεν ἑαυτὸν ἀναιρεῖν, νομίζων ἐκπεφευγέναι τοὺς δεσμίους.
verses.transliteration: exypnos de genomenos ho desmophylax kai idōn aneōgmenas tas thyras tēs phylakēs, spasamenos tēn machairan ēmellen heauton anairein, nomizōn ekpepheugenai tous desmious.
verses.study_note: A Roman soldier who lost prisoners forfeited his own life (cf. 12:19). Suicide seemed honour. In this moment of terror the gospel arrives.
verses.doctrinal_note: Despair is the doorway through which grace often walks. God brings men to the edge of self-destruction that He may save them by His Son.

verses.kjv_text: But Paul cried with a loud voice, saying, Do thyself no harm: for we are all here.
verses.original_text: ἐφώνησεν δὲ μεγάλῃ φωνῇ ὁ Παῦλος λέγων· Μηδὲν πράξῃς σεαυτῷ κακόν, ἅπαντες γάρ ἐσμεν ἐνθάδε.
verses.transliteration: ephōnēsen de megalē phōnē ho Paulos legōn; Mēden praxēs seautō kakon, hapantes gar esmen enthade.
verses.study_note: Paul's first words to a dying man: "Do thyself no harm." The apostle's concern is not his own freedom but the jailer's life.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian mercy stays escape for the sake of a hostile stranger. The gospel so turns men that they love their enemies more than their own liberty.

verses.kjv_text: Then he called for a light, and sprang in, and came trembling, and fell down before Paul and Silas,
verses.original_text: αἰτήσας δὲ φῶτα εἰσεπήδησεν καὶ ἔντρομος γενόμενος προσέπεσεν τῷ Παύλῳ καὶ τῷ Σιλᾷ,
verses.transliteration: aitēsas de phōta eisepēdēsen kai entromos genomenos prosepesen tō Paulō kai tō Sila,
verses.study_note: The jailer, who hours before had thrust them into stocks, now trembles at their feet. Eisepēdēsen, "leaped in" — urgency of awakened conscience.
verses.doctrinal_note: Awakened sinners do not saunter to Christ; they fall at His feet. Trembling is the right posture before holiness.

verses.kjv_text: And brought them out, and said, Sirs, what must I do to be saved?
verses.original_text: καὶ προαγαγὼν αὐτοὺς ἔξω ἔφη· Κύριοι, τί με δεῖ ποιεῖν ἵνα σωθῶ;
verses.transliteration: kai proagagōn autous exō ephē; Kyrioi, ti me dei poiein hina sōthō;
verses.study_note: The quintessential seeker's question. He already knows he needs salvation — the earthquake has shaken more than the walls.
verses.doctrinal_note: When the Spirit convicts, the question is no longer "is there a God?" but "what must I do to be saved?" God answers that question with the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, Believe on the Lord Jesus Christ, and thou shalt be saved, and thy house.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ εἶπαν· Πίστευσον ἐπὶ τὸν κύριον Ἰησοῦν, καὶ σωθήσῃ σὺ καὶ ὁ οἶκός σου.
verses.transliteration: hoi de eipan; Pisteuson epi ton kyrion Iēsoun, kai sōthēsē sy kai ho oikos sou.
verses.study_note: The NT's simplest gospel summary. Pisteuson ("believe") is aorist imperative — decisive trust. The promise extends to the whole household on condition of their own believing (cf. v.34).
verses.doctrinal_note: Salvation is by faith alone in Christ alone. Believing is the only "must" for the sinner; Christ is the only object; household blessing follows household faith.

verses.kjv_text: And they spake unto him the word of the Lord, and to all that were in his house.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλάλησαν αὐτῷ τὸν λόγον τοῦ κυρίου σὺν πᾶσιν τοῖς ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ αὐτοῦ.
verses.transliteration: kai elalēsan autō ton logon tou kyriou syn pasin tois en tē oikia autou.
verses.study_note: Faith is not a blind leap; it is grounded in "the word of the Lord" explained to the household. Preaching follows the call to believe.
verses.doctrinal_note: True conversion requires true teaching. The household must hear what it is to trust before it can trust what it has heard.

verses.kjv_text: And he took them the same hour of the night, and washed their stripes; and was baptized, he and all his, straightway.
verses.original_text: καὶ παραλαβὼν αὐτοὺς ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ὥρᾳ τῆς νυκτὸς ἔλουσεν ἀπὸ τῶν πληγῶν, καὶ ἐβαπτίσθη αὐτὸς καὶ οἱ αὐτοῦ πάντες παραχρῆμα,
verses.transliteration: kai paralabōn autous en ekeinē tē hōra tēs nyktos elousen apo tōn plēgōn, kai ebaptisthē autos kai hoi autou pantes parachrēma,
verses.study_note: A double washing: the jailer washes the apostles' wounds, then is himself washed in baptism. Mercy and sacrament in the same midnight hour.
verses.doctrinal_note: Conversion yields immediate obedience. The new convert tends the wounds he once inflicted, and submits to the sign Christ commanded.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had brought them into his house, he set meat before them, and rejoiced, believing in God with all his house.
verses.original_text: ἀναγαγών τε αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸν οἶκον παρέθηκεν τράπεζαν, καὶ ἠγαλλιάσατο πανοικεὶ πεπιστευκὼς τῷ θεῷ.
verses.transliteration: anagagōn te autous eis ton oikon parethēken trapezan, kai ēgalliasato panoikei pepisteukōs tō theō.
verses.study_note: Panoikei, "with the whole household," and pepisteukōs, perfect participle — settled faith. Each member believed; each member rejoiced.
verses.doctrinal_note: Saving faith begets household joy. Grace transforms a Roman jail-keeper's dinner table into a scene of Eucharistic delight.

verses.kjv_text: And when it was day, the magistrates sent the serjeants, saying, Let those men go.
verses.original_text: Ἡμέρας δὲ γενομένης ἀπέστειλαν οἱ στρατηγοὶ τοὺς ῥαβδούχους λέγοντες· Ἀπόλυσον τοὺς ἀνθρώπους ἐκείνους.
verses.transliteration: Hēmeras de genomenēs apesteilan hoi stratēgoi tous rhabdouchous legontes; Apolyson tous anthrōpous ekeinous.
verses.study_note: Perhaps the earthquake gave the magistrates second thoughts, or perhaps news of their illegal beating reached them. Either way, providence is arranging Paul's vindication.
verses.doctrinal_note: Unjust rulers cannot resist the hand of God that overrules even their panic for His servants' dignity.

verses.kjv_text: And the keeper of the prison told this saying to Paul, The magistrates have sent to let you go: now therefore depart, and go in peace.
verses.original_text: ἀπήγγειλεν δὲ ὁ δεσμοφύλαξ τοὺς λόγους τούτους πρὸς τὸν Παῦλον ὅτι Ἀπέσταλκαν οἱ στρατηγοὶ ἵνα ἀπολυθῆτε· νῦν οὖν ἐξελθόντες πορεύεσθε ἐν εἰρήνῃ.
verses.transliteration: apēngeilen de ho desmophylax tous logous toutous pros ton Paulon hoti Apestalkan hoi stratēgoi hina apolythēte; nyn oun exelthontes poreuesthe en eirēnē.
verses.study_note: The now-believing jailer happily reports freedom. "Go in peace" carries a gospel edge: the jailer blesses those he once chained.
verses.doctrinal_note: The transformed heart blesses those it once bound. Grace reverses relational polarity.

verses.kjv_text: But Paul said unto them, They have beaten us openly uncondemned, being Romans, and have cast us into prison; and now do they thrust us out privily? nay verily; but let them come themselves and fetch us out.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Παῦλος ἔφη πρὸς αὐτούς· Δείραντες ἡμᾶς δημοσίᾳ ἀκατακρίτους, ἀνθρώπους Ῥωμαίους ὑπάρχοντας, ἔβαλαν εἰς φυλακήν· καὶ νῦν λάθρᾳ ἡμᾶς ἐκβάλλουσιν; οὐ γάρ, ἀλλὰ ἐλθόντες αὐτοὶ ἡμᾶς ἐξαγαγέτωσαν.
verses.transliteration: ho de Paulos ephē pros autous; Deirantes hēmas dēmosia akatakritous, anthrōpous Rhōmaious hyparchontas, ebalan eis phylakēn; kai nyn lathra hēmas ekballousin? ou gar, alla elthontes autoi hēmas exagagetōsan.
verses.study_note: Roman citizens could not lawfully be beaten untried (Lex Porcia, Lex Valeria). Paul insists on public vindication, not for personal vengeance, but for the young church's future safety.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian humility does not surrender Christian rights when the gospel's future is at stake. Use the law to protect the flock you leave behind.

verses.kjv_text: And the serjeants told these words unto the magistrates: and they feared, when they heard that they were Romans.
verses.original_text: ἀπήγγειλαν δὲ τοῖς στρατηγοῖς οἱ ῥαβδοῦχοι τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα· ἐφοβήθησαν δὲ ἀκούσαντες ὅτι Ῥωμαῖοί εἰσιν,
verses.transliteration: apēngeilan de tois stratēgois hoi rhabdouchoi ta rhēmata tauta; ephobēthēsan de akousantes hoti Rhōmaioi eisin,
verses.study_note: Magistrates who flog Roman citizens without trial risk their careers. The tables are turned.
verses.doctrinal_note: The civil sword is in God's hand, bent by Him to protect the Christian witness when and where He pleases.

verses.kjv_text: And they came and besought them, and brought them out, and desired them to depart out of the city.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐλθόντες παρεκάλεσαν αὐτούς, καὶ ἐξαγαγόντες ἠρώτων ἀπελθεῖν ἀπὸ τῆς πόλεως.
verses.transliteration: kai elthontes parekalesan autous, kai exagagontes ērōtōn apelthein apo tēs poleōs.
verses.study_note: The magistrates now "beseech" the apostles — the reversal is complete. Yet they still ask them to leave, fearing further disturbance.
verses.doctrinal_note: Public vindication of Christ's servants is often followed by polite ejection. The world will apologize and still ask the gospel to move along.

verses.kjv_text: And they went out of the prison, and entered into the house of Lydia: and when they had seen the brethren, they comforted them, and departed.
verses.original_text: ἐξελθόντες δὲ ἀπὸ τῆς φυλακῆς εἰσῆλθον πρὸς τὴν Λυδίαν, καὶ ἰδόντες τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς παρεκάλεσαν αὐτοὺς καὶ ἐξῆλθαν.
verses.transliteration: exelthontes de apo tēs phylakēs eisēlthon pros tēn Lydian, kai idontes tous adelphous parekalesan autous kai exēlthan.
verses.study_note: The apostles, far from being intimidated, stop at Lydia's house to strengthen the newborn church. Luke apparently stays (the "we" passages pause until 20:5), possibly remaining as pastor at Philippi.
verses.doctrinal_note: A young church left behind is tenderly catechized. The apostle's last act before departure is encouragement, not complaint.

1.meaning: Timothy — "honouring God"; Paul's dearly loved spiritual son (1 Tim 1:2).

2.meaning: Was well-reported-of — the Pauline standard for eldership (1 Tim 3:7).

3.meaning: He circumcised — here done strategically for mission, not for salvation.

4.meaning: Decrees, ordinances — the Jerusalem Council's conciliar rulings.

5.meaning: Were made firm — doctrinal stability of churches.

6.meaning: Having been forbidden/hindered — Spirit-given closed doors.

7.meaning: Spirit of Jesus — the risen Lord's Spirit directing His mission.

9.meaning: Vision — a supernatural sight granted for specific guidance.
Crossing over — the keyword of the chapter; gospel crossing from Asia to Europe.
Help! — a cry of desperate need, met by sovereign mission.

10.meaning: Joining together, concluding — the Spirit-led reasoning that confirms God's call.

11.meaning: Ran a straight course — favourable sailing wind as providential confirmation.

12.meaning: Colony — Roman military colony with citizen status.

13.meaning: Place of prayer — informal Jewish meeting where no synagogue existed.

14.meaning: Seller of purple — wealthy trader in expensive dyed cloth.
Worshipper of God — technical term for Gentile God-fearer attached to synagogue.
Opened thoroughly — sovereign regeneration (same verb Luke 24:45).

15.meaning: Constrained, urgently pressed — hospitality as evidence of conversion.

16.meaning: Python — the Delphic oracle spirit, demonic divination source.
Soothsaying, practising divination — forbidden occult activity (Deut 18:10).

17.meaning: Most High — divine title, here used by demon with accurate but unwelcome testimony.

18.meaning: Being greatly distressed — moral burden leading to exorcism.
I command/charge — authoritative apostolic order in Christ's name.

19.meaning: Gain, profit-making — commercial exploitation of bondage.

20.meaning: Magistrates (duoviri) — chief civic officials of a Roman colony.

22.meaning: To beat with rods — Roman punishment by lictors (2 Cor 11:25).

24.meaning: Inner prison — the deepest, most secure cell, typically windowless.
Wood, stocks — instrument of painful restraint forcing legs apart.

25.meaning: Were hymning — formal praise-singing in the inner dungeon.
Were listening attentively — the prisoners held rapt by midnight worship.

26.meaning: Earthquake — selectively miraculous, opening only what mission required.

27.meaning: To kill, make away with — despair driving the jailer to suicide.

29.meaning: Trembling — physical response of awakened conscience before God.

30.meaning: I may be saved — aorist subjunctive; decisive deliverance sought.

31.meaning: Believe! — aorist imperative; decisive trust in the Lord Jesus.
Lord Jesus — the object of saving faith; divine sovereign and Saviour.

33.meaning: He washed — tending the very wounds he had inflicted; evidence of conversion.
He was baptized — immediate obedience to Christ's command.

34.meaning: Rejoiced greatly — eschatological joy bursting into a midnight home.
With the whole household — household solidarity of faith and joy.

37.meaning: Romans — citizens protected by Lex Porcia from unjust flogging.
Uncondemned — beaten without trial, a grave legal violation.

38.meaning: They feared — magistrates terrified of imperial consequences.

meta.overview: Acts 17 advances the second journey through three cities, each revealing a different gospel audience. At Thessalonica Paul reasons for three sabbaths from the Scriptures that the Christ must suffer and rise; many believe, but jealous Jews stir a mob and drag Jason before the rulers. At Berea the Jews are more noble, receiving the word with readiness and searching the Scriptures daily; many believe, until the Thessalonian opposition follows. Paul is escorted to Athens, where his spirit is stirred by an idolatrous city. He disputes in the synagogue and marketplace until Stoics and Epicureans bring him to Mars Hill. There he preaches the masterful Areopagus sermon: the Creator who needs nothing, who has made of one blood all nations, who has appointed a day of judgment, who has furnished proof by raising a man from the dead — Jesus. Some mock, some defer, but certain believe, including Dionysius the Areopagite and Damaris.
meta.theme: The Unknown God Made Known in the Risen Christ
meta.keyWordMeaning: ignorant (agnoountes) — the darkened state of pagan worship, now confronted by the revelation of the true God in Christ (v.23).

outline.title: Thessalonica: Three Sabbaths, Then the Uproar
Berea: More Noble; Searching the Scriptures
Athens: Stirred in Spirit, Summoned to Mars Hill
The Areopagus Sermon: Creator, Kinship, and Judgment
Mockery, Delay, and Some Who Believed

verses.kjv_text: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where was a synagogue of the Jews:
verses.original_text: Διοδεύσαντες δὲ τὴν Ἀμφίπολιν καὶ τὴν Ἀπολλωνίαν ἦλθον εἰς Θεσσαλονίκην, ὅπου ἦν συναγωγὴ τῶν Ἰουδαίων.
verses.transliteration: Diodeusantes de tēn Amphipolin kai tēn Apollōnian ēlthon eis Thessalonikēn, hopou ēn synagōgē tōn Ioudaiōn.
verses.study_note: Thessalonica, capital of Macedonia, lay on the Via Egnatia — a strategic hub whose converts would radiate the gospel throughout the province (1 Thess 1:7-8).
verses.doctrinal_note: Strategic cities are not chosen by the ambition of ministers but by the providence of the Lord who appoints both the cities and the converts there.

verses.kjv_text: And Paul, as his manner was, went in unto them, and three sabbath days reasoned with them out of the scriptures,
