verses.original_text: κατὰ δὲ τὸ εἰωθὸς τῷ Παύλῳ εἰσῆλθεν πρὸς αὐτοὺς καὶ ἐπὶ σάββατα τρία διελέξατο αὐτοῖς ἀπὸ τῶν γραφῶν,
verses.transliteration: kata de to eiōthos tō Paulō eisēlthen pros autous kai epi sabbata tria dielexato autois apo tōn graphōn,
verses.study_note: "His manner" (to eiōthos) — the synagogue-first pattern remains invariable. Dielexato, "dialogued, reasoned," describes structured argument from Scripture, not mere proclamation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gospel preaching is rational, exegetical, and scriptural — reasoning from the sacred text, not simply asserting from inward authority.

verses.kjv_text: Opening and alleging, that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead; and that this Jesus, whom I preach unto you, is Christ.
verses.original_text: διανοίγων καὶ παρατιθέμενος ὅτι τὸν χριστὸν ἔδει παθεῖν καὶ ἀναστῆναι ἐκ νεκρῶν, καὶ ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ χριστὸς Ἰησοῦς, ὃν ἐγὼ καταγγέλλω ὑμῖν.
verses.transliteration: dianoigōn kai paratithemenos hoti ton christon edei pathein kai anastēnai ek nekrōn, kai hoti houtos estin ho christos Iēsous, hon egō katangellō hymin.
verses.study_note: Dianoigōn, "opening up," the Scriptures; paratithemenos, "setting before them" proofs. Edei ("it was necessary") — the divine necessity that the Messiah suffer (Luke 24:26).
verses.doctrinal_note: The cross and resurrection of Christ are not tragic accidents but the predetermined divine necessity written in every OT page. True preaching unfolds the suffering-and-rising Christ from Scripture itself.

verses.kjv_text: And some of them believed, and consorted with Paul and Silas; and of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and of the chief women not a few.
verses.original_text: καί τινες ἐξ αὐτῶν ἐπείσθησαν καὶ προσεκληρώθησαν τῷ Παύλῳ καὶ τῷ Σιλᾷ, τῶν τε σεβομένων Ἑλλήνων πλῆθος πολύ, γυναικῶν τε τῶν πρώτων οὐκ ὀλίγαι.
verses.transliteration: kai tines ex autōn epeisthēsan kai proseklērōthēsan tō Paulō kai tō Sila, tōn te sebomenōn Hellēnōn plēthos poly, gynaikōn te tōn prōtōn ouk oligai.
verses.study_note: Proseklērōthēsan, "were allotted with" — the language of divine assignment: these believers were God's appointed portion for Paul and Silas. "Chief women" suggests the upper-class patronesses common to Macedonian cities.
verses.doctrinal_note: Converts are allotted by God to His servants. Believing is not primarily the sinner's decision but the Lord's appointment executed through preaching.

verses.kjv_text: But the Jews which believed not, moved with envy, took unto them certain lewd fellows of the baser sort, and gathered a company, and set all the city on an uproar, and assaulted the house of Jason, and sought to bring them out to the people.
verses.original_text: Ζηλώσαντες δὲ οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ προσλαβόμενοι τῶν ἀγοραίων ἄνδρας τινὰς πονηροὺς καὶ ὀχλοποιήσαντες ἐθορύβουν τὴν πόλιν, καὶ ἐπιστάντες τῇ οἰκίᾳ Ἰάσονος ἐζήτουν αὐτοὺς προαγαγεῖν εἰς τὸν δῆμον·
verses.transliteration: Zēlōsantes de hoi Ioudaioi kai proslabomenoi tōn agoraiōn andras tinas ponērous kai ochlopoiēsantes ethoryboun tēn polin, kai epistantes tē oikia Iasonos ezētoun autous proagagein eis ton dēmon;
verses.study_note: Zēlōsantes — envious zeal, not holy zeal. They rent a mob from the marketplace (agoraiōn — "loiterers"). Jason, Paul's host, becomes the target.
verses.doctrinal_note: Envy is a religious sin that conscripts irreligious allies. The gospel's success often intensifies its opponents into unholy alliance with the world.

verses.kjv_text: And when they found them not, they drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers of the city, crying, These that have turned the world upside down are come hither also;
verses.original_text: μὴ εὑρόντες δὲ αὐτοὺς ἔσυρον Ἰάσονα καί τινας ἀδελφοὺς ἐπὶ τοὺς πολιτάρχας, βοῶντες ὅτι Οἱ τὴν οἰκουμένην ἀναστατώσαντες οὗτοι καὶ ἐνθάδε πάρεισιν,
verses.transliteration: mē heurontes de autous esyron Iasona kai tinas adelphous epi tous politarchas, boōntes hoti Hoi tēn oikoumenēn anastatōsantes houtoi kai enthade pareisin,
verses.study_note: Politarchēs — an office attested in Thessalonian inscriptions; Luke's precision confirms his historical reliability. "Turned the world upside down" (anastatōsantes) is an unwitting compliment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel does indeed overturn the world's order. Where Christ reigns, idols fall, values invert, and the last become first.

verses.kjv_text: Whom Jason hath received: and these all do contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, one Jesus.
verses.original_text: οὓς ὑποδέδεκται Ἰάσων· καὶ οὗτοι πάντες ἀπέναντι τῶν δογμάτων Καίσαρος πράσσουσι, βασιλέα ἕτερον λέγοντες εἶναι Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: hous hypodedektai Iasōn; kai houtoi pantes apenanti tōn dogmatōn Kaisaros prassousi, basilea heteron legontes einai Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: The charge of treason — proclaiming another king — is both false (politically) and true (ultimately). Jesus is indeed King of kings, but His kingdom does not rival Caesar's along imperial lines.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian allegiance ultimately relativizes every human throne. The King named Jesus reigns over every Caesar who thinks himself supreme.

verses.kjv_text: And they troubled the people and the rulers of the city, when they heard these things.
verses.original_text: ἐτάραξαν δὲ τὸν ὄχλον καὶ τοὺς πολιτάρχας ἀκούοντας ταῦτα,
verses.transliteration: etaraxan de ton ochlon kai tous politarchas akouontas tauta,
verses.study_note: The charge of treason, plausible under Claudius (who had expelled Jews from Rome), alarms even the magistrates.
verses.doctrinal_note: False accusation drapes itself in the language of loyalty to the state. Christ's servants must bear this form of suffering patiently.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had taken security of Jason, and of the other, they let them go.
verses.original_text: καὶ λαβόντες τὸ ἱκανὸν παρὰ τοῦ Ἰάσονος καὶ τῶν λοιπῶν ἀπέλυσαν αὐτούς.
verses.transliteration: kai labontes to hikanon para tou Iasonos kai tōn loipōn apelysan autous.
verses.study_note: "Security" (to hikanon) — a financial bond guaranteeing peace. This may explain why Paul could not return immediately ("Satan hindered us," 1 Thess 2:18).
verses.doctrinal_note: Even the enemy's legal tools bind God's purposes only for a season. Hindered missionaries are not defeated ones.

verses.kjv_text: And the brethren immediately sent away Paul and Silas by night unto Berea: who coming thither went into the synagogue of the Jews.
verses.original_text: Οἱ δὲ ἀδελφοὶ εὐθέως διὰ νυκτὸς ἐξέπεμψαν τόν τε Παῦλον καὶ τὸν Σιλᾶν εἰς Βέροιαν, οἵτινες παραγενόμενοι εἰς τὴν συναγωγὴν τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἀπῄεσαν·
verses.transliteration: Hoi de adelphoi eutheōs dia nyktos exepempsan ton te Paulon kai ton Silan eis Beroian, hoitines paragenomenoi eis tēn synagōgēn tōn Ioudaiōn apēesan;
verses.study_note: Night escort by the brethren — a new church protecting its apostles. Berea lay some 50 miles southwest.
verses.doctrinal_note: The young church's first love expresses itself as protective action for the apostles who planted them. Spiritual parenthood is a two-way bond.

verses.kjv_text: These were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, and searched the scriptures daily, whether those things were so.
verses.original_text: οὗτοι δὲ ἦσαν εὐγενέστεροι τῶν ἐν Θεσσαλονίκῃ, οἵτινες ἐδέξαντο τὸν λόγον μετὰ πάσης προθυμίας, καθ᾽ ἡμέραν ἀνακρίνοντες τὰς γραφὰς εἰ ἔχοι ταῦτα οὕτως.
verses.transliteration: houtoi de ēsan eugenesteroi tōn en Thessalonikē, hoitines edexanto ton logon meta pasēs prothymias, kath hēmeran anakrinontes tas graphas ei echoi tauta houtōs.
verses.study_note: Eugenesteroi, "more noble-born," metaphorically "more open-minded." Prothymias, "eagerness." Anakrinontes, "examining judicially." The Bereans neither swallow the apostle's word uncritically nor reject it without hearing — they measure it by Scripture.
verses.doctrinal_note: True nobility of soul welcomes the word eagerly yet tests it by Scripture. The Bereans set the perpetual standard: receive with readiness, examine with the written Word.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore many of them believed; also of honourable women which were Greeks, and of men, not a few.
verses.original_text: πολλοὶ μὲν οὖν ἐξ αὐτῶν ἐπίστευσαν, καὶ τῶν Ἑλληνίδων γυναικῶν τῶν εὐσχημόνων καὶ ἀνδρῶν οὐκ ὀλίγοι.
verses.transliteration: polloi men oun ex autōn episteusan, kai tōn Hellēnidōn gynaikōn tōn euschēmonōn kai andrōn ouk oligoi.
verses.study_note: The fruit of Scripture-searching is faith. Study and belief are friends, not foes, when Scripture is the object.
verses.doctrinal_note: Faith born of Scripture-examination is durable faith. Doubt that leads a man to the Book is on the road to belief.

verses.kjv_text: But when the Jews of Thessalonica had knowledge that the word of God was preached of Paul at Berea, they came thither also, and stirred up the people.
verses.original_text: Ὡς δὲ ἔγνωσαν οἱ ἀπὸ τῆς Θεσσαλονίκης Ἰουδαῖοι ὅτι καὶ ἐν τῇ Βεροίᾳ κατηγγέλη ὑπὸ τοῦ Παύλου ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ, ἦλθον κἀκεῖ σαλεύοντες καὶ ταράσσοντες τοὺς ὄχλους.
verses.transliteration: Hōs de egnōsan hoi apo tēs Thessalonikēs Ioudaioi hoti kai en tē Beroia katēngelē hypo tou Paulou ho logos tou theou, ēlthon kakei saleuontes kai tarassontes tous ochlous.
verses.study_note: The Thessalonian opposition travels 50 miles to continue the pursuit. Saleuontes, "shaking," the same verb as "earthquake" (16:26) — a satanic counter-movement against the shaking that delivered the jailer.
verses.doctrinal_note: Persistent enemies pursue the gospel from city to city. Satan's malice is tireless; Christ's grace is more tireless still.

verses.kjv_text: And then immediately the brethren sent away Paul to go as it were to the sea: but Silas and Timotheus abode there still.
verses.original_text: εὐθέως δὲ τότε τὸν Παῦλον ἐξαπέστειλαν οἱ ἀδελφοὶ πορεύεσθαι ἕως ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν, ὑπέμειναν δὲ ὅ τε Σιλᾶς καὶ ὁ Τιμόθεος ἐκεῖ.
verses.transliteration: eutheōs de tote ton Paulon exapesteilan hoi adelphoi poreuesthai heōs epi tēn thalassan, hypemeinan de ho te Silas kai ho Timotheos ekei.
verses.study_note: Silas and Timothy remain to shepherd the young church; Paul is the chief target and is moved southward.
verses.doctrinal_note: The seasoned apostle withdraws; the younger ministers stand firm. The church's care of her leaders sometimes separates to protect and preserve.

verses.kjv_text: And they that conducted Paul brought him unto Athens: and receiving a commandment unto Silas and Timotheus for to come to him with all speed, they departed.
verses.original_text: οἱ δὲ καθιστάνοντες τὸν Παῦλον ἤγαγον ἕως Ἀθηνῶν, καὶ λαβόντες ἐντολὴν πρὸς τὸν Σιλᾶν καὶ τὸν Τιμόθεον ἵνα ὡς τάχιστα ἔλθωσιν πρὸς αὐτὸν ἐξῄεσαν.
verses.transliteration: hoi de kathistanontes ton Paulon ēgagon heōs Athēnōn, kai labontes entolēn pros ton Silan kai ton Timotheon hina hōs tachista elthōsin pros auton exēesan.
verses.study_note: Athens, though no longer the political capital, remained the intellectual center of the Greek world. Paul waits alone among its altars.
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle who breathes best in the furnace of opposition must sometimes wait, alone and grieved, in the city of ideas. Faithfulness includes solitary waiting.

verses.kjv_text: Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was stirred in him, when he saw the city wholly given to idolatry.
verses.original_text: Ἐν δὲ ταῖς Ἀθήναις ἐκδεχομένου αὐτοὺς τοῦ Παύλου, παρωξύνετο τὸ πνεῦμα αὐτοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ θεωροῦντος κατείδωλον οὖσαν τὴν πόλιν.
verses.transliteration: En de tais Athēnais ekdechomenou autous tou Paulou, parōxyneto to pneuma autou en autō theōrountos kateidōlon ousan tēn polin.
verses.study_note: Parōxyneto — "was paroxysmed," sharply stirred. The same root as paroxysmos (15:39). Kateidōlon ("full of idols") is a unique NT word. Contemporary witnesses estimated 30,000 images in Athens.
verses.doctrinal_note: Holy love for Christ cannot walk calmly through a forest of idols. The saint's heart beats hot where God's glory is stolen.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore disputed he in the synagogue with the Jews, and with the devout persons, and in the market daily with them that met with him.
verses.original_text: διελέγετο μὲν οὖν ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις καὶ τοῖς σεβομένοις καὶ ἐν τῇ ἀγορᾷ κατὰ πᾶσαν ἡμέραν πρὸς τοὺς παρατυγχάνοντας.
verses.transliteration: dielegeto men oun en tē synagōgē tois Ioudaiois kai tois sebomenois kai en tē agora kata pasan hēmeran pros tous paratynchanontas.
verses.study_note: Paul does both synagogue exposition and agora dialogue — formal theology to God-fearers and public conversation to any who happened by. Socrates himself had disputed in this same marketplace 450 years earlier.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian apologetic speaks in every forum — religious and secular, scheduled and spontaneous. The whole world is the apostle's classroom.

verses.kjv_text: Then certain philosophers of the Epicureans, and of the Stoicks, encountered him. And some said, What will this babbler say? other some, He seemeth to be a setter forth of strange gods: because he preached unto them Jesus, and the resurrection.
verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ καὶ τῶν Ἐπικουρείων καὶ Στοϊκῶν φιλοσόφων συνέβαλλον αὐτῷ, καί τινες ἔλεγον· Τί ἂν θέλοι ὁ σπερμολόγος οὗτος λέγειν; οἱ δέ· Ξένων δαιμονίων δοκεῖ καταγγελεὺς εἶναι· ὅτι τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ τὴν ἀνάστασιν εὐηγγελίζετο.
verses.transliteration: tines de kai tōn Epikoureiōn kai Stoikōn philosophōn syneballon autō, kai tines elegon; Ti an theloi ho spermologos houtos legein? hoi de; Xenōn daimoniōn dokei katangeleus einai; hoti ton Iēsoun kai tēn anastasin euēngelizeto.
verses.study_note: Epicureans pursued tranquil pleasure and denied providence; Stoics pursued virtue through reason and equated God with nature's soul. "Babbler" (spermologos) = "seed-picker," a scavenger-bird; a contemptuous epithet.
verses.doctrinal_note: Every age offers the two dominant errors — hedonism and moralism. Both must hear Christ crucified and risen; both are equally dead without Him.

verses.kjv_text: And they took him, and brought him unto Areopagus, saying, May we know what this new doctrine, whereof thou speakest, is?
verses.original_text: ἐπιλαβόμενοί τε αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὸν Ἄρειον πάγον ἤγαγον λέγοντες· Δυνάμεθα γνῶναι τίς ἡ καινὴ αὕτη ἡ ὑπὸ σοῦ λαλουμένη διδαχή;
verses.transliteration: epilabomenoi te autou epi ton Areion pagon ēgagon legontes; Dynametha gnōnai tis hē kainē hautē hē hypo sou laloumenē didachē?
verses.study_note: The Areopagus ("Mars Hill") was the ancient Athenian council that supervised religion, morals, and education. Paul is brought for inquiry, not trial.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel is willing to be heard by any council. Christ does not fear cross-examination; He invites it.

verses.kjv_text: For thou bringest certain strange things to our ears: we would know therefore what these things mean.
verses.original_text: ξενίζοντα γάρ τινα εἰσφέρεις εἰς τὰς ἀκοὰς ἡμῶν· βουλόμεθα οὖν γνῶναι τίνα θέλει ταῦτα εἶναι.
verses.transliteration: xenizonta gar tina eispherеis eis tas akoas hēmōn; boulometha oun gnōnai tina thelei tauta einai.
verses.study_note: Xenizonta, "strange, foreign-sounding." Athenian curiosity, not yet hostile, affords the apostle his hearing.
verses.doctrinal_note: Even shallow curiosity opens doors the Spirit fills with weight. The apostle preaches through any door providence opens.

verses.kjv_text: (For all the Athenians and strangers which were there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell, or to hear some new thing.)
verses.original_text: Ἀθηναῖοι δὲ πάντες καὶ οἱ ἐπιδημοῦντες ξένοι εἰς οὐδὲν ἕτερον ηὐκαίρουν ἢ λέγειν τι ἢ ἀκούειν τι καινότερον.
verses.transliteration: Athēnaioi de pantes kai hoi epidēmountes xenoi eis ouden heteron ēukairoun ē legein ti ē akouein ti kainoteron.
verses.study_note: Luke's parenthetical ethnography. Athens's intellectual fashion was addicted to novelty — the same modern sickness in academic garb.
verses.doctrinal_note: Novelty is the enemy of truth wherever curiosity replaces conviction. The gospel is ancient and new at once; it answers the itch it does not indulge.

verses.kjv_text: Then Paul stood in the midst of Mars' hill, and said, Ye men of Athens, I perceive that in all things ye are too superstitious.
verses.original_text: Σταθεὶς δὲ Παῦλος ἐν μέσῳ τοῦ Ἀρείου πάγου ἔφη· Ἄνδρες Ἀθηναῖοι, κατὰ πάντα ὡς δεισιδαιμονεστέρους ὑμᾶς θεωρῶ·
verses.transliteration: Statheis de Paulos en mesō tou Areiou pagou ephē; Andres Athēnaioi, kata panta hōs deisidaimonesterous hymas theōrō;
verses.study_note: Deisidaimonesterous is ambiguous — "very religious" or "very superstitious." Paul opens diplomatically, acknowledging their reverence without yet endorsing it.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gospel wisdom begins where people are. The preacher acknowledges genuine religious longing before confronting error.

verses.kjv_text: For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you.
verses.original_text: διερχόμενος γὰρ καὶ ἀναθεωρῶν τὰ σεβάσματα ὑμῶν εὗρον καὶ βωμὸν ἐν ᾧ ἐπεγέγραπτο· Ἀγνώστῳ θεῷ. ὃ οὖν ἀγνοοῦντες εὐσεβεῖτε, τοῦτο ἐγὼ καταγγέλλω ὑμῖν.
verses.transliteration: dierchomenos gar kai anatheōrōn ta sebasmata hymōn heuron kai bōmon en hō epegegrapto; Agnōstō theō. ho oun agnoountes eusebeite, touto egō katangellō hymin.
verses.study_note: Altars "to unknown gods" were attested in Athens by Pausanias (2nd c. AD). Paul seizes their confessed ignorance as his contact-point. Agnoountes, "being ignorant," names the problem their altar acknowledges.
verses.doctrinal_note: The truest human religion confesses its own ignorance. Revelation alone can fill the unknown; Christ alone is the God now made known.

verses.kjv_text: God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands;
verses.original_text: ὁ θεὸς ὁ ποιήσας τὸν κόσμον καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτῷ, οὗτος οὐρανοῦ καὶ γῆς ὑπάρχων κύριος οὐκ ἐν χειροποιήτοις ναοῖς κατοικεῖ,
verses.transliteration: ho theos ho poiēsas ton kosmon kai panta ta en autō, houtos ouranou kai gēs hyparchōn kyrios ouk en cheiropoiētois naois katoikei,
verses.study_note: Paul begins with Creator-Lord, echoing Gen 1:1 and Isa 66:1-2. "Made with hands" (cheiropoiētois) recalls Stephen's sermon (7:48) — a direct blow to the Parthenon in view.
verses.doctrinal_note: The God who made all cannot be housed in any. All human religion that confines God to shrines is a diminishment of His majesty.

verses.kjv_text: Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things;
verses.original_text: οὐδὲ ὑπὸ χειρῶν ἀνθρωπίνων θεραπεύεται προσδεόμενός τινος, αὐτὸς διδοὺς πᾶσι ζωὴν καὶ πνοὴν καὶ τὰ πάντα·
verses.transliteration: oude hypo cheirōn anthrōpinōn therapeuetai prosdeomenos tinos, autos didous pasi zōēn kai pnoēn kai ta panta;
verses.study_note: God is aseity itself — existing independently, needing nothing (Ps 50:9-12). He gives; He does not need to be given to.
verses.doctrinal_note: The true God is infinitely self-sufficient. Worship is not supplying Him but receiving from Him with thankful hands.

verses.kjv_text: And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation;
verses.original_text: ἐποίησέν τε ἐξ ἑνὸς πᾶν ἔθνος ἀνθρώπων κατοικεῖν ἐπὶ παντὸς προσώπου τῆς γῆς, ὁρίσας προστεταγμένους καιροὺς καὶ τὰς ὁροθεσίας τῆς κατοικίας αὐτῶν,
verses.transliteration: epoiēsen te ex henos pan ethnos anthrōpōn katoikein epi pantos prosōpou tēs gēs, horisas prostetagmenous kairous kai tas horothesias tēs katoikias autōn,
verses.study_note: "Of one blood" (ex henos, "from one") — Paul affirms the unity of humanity in Adam, cutting against Greek ethnic pride that disdained barbarians. God has ordered "times" (epochs) and "bounds" (national borders).
verses.doctrinal_note: All nations share one blood and one Maker. Rise and fall of empires, chart of every continent, is ordained by God to bring men to seek Him.

verses.kjv_text: That they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him, and find him, though he be not far from every one of us:
verses.original_text: ζητεῖν τὸν θεὸν εἰ ἄρα γε ψηλαφήσειαν αὐτὸν καὶ εὕροιεν, καί γε οὐ μακρὰν ἀπὸ ἑνὸς ἑκάστου ἡμῶν ὑπάρχοντα.
verses.transliteration: zētein ton theon ei ara ge psēlaphēseian auton kai heuroien, kai ge ou makran apo henos hekastou hēmōn hyparchonta.
verses.study_note: Psēlaphēseian, "might grope" — the blind reaching of the human heart after unseen reality. God's nearness to every man is real; man's ignorance of that nearness is tragic.
verses.doctrinal_note: God has so ordered history that the nations might grope after Him. He is near to every man; only sin blinds man to the One who is closer than breath.

verses.kjv_text: For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring.
verses.original_text: ἐν αὐτῷ γὰρ ζῶμεν καὶ κινούμεθα καὶ ἐσμέν· ὡς καί τινες τῶν καθ᾽ ὑμᾶς ποιητῶν εἰρήκασιν· Τοῦ γὰρ καὶ γένος ἐσμέν.
verses.transliteration: en autō gar zōmen kai kinoumetha kai esmen; hōs kai tines tōn kath hymas poiētōn eirēkasin; Tou gar kai genos esmen.
verses.study_note: Paul quotes Epimenides of Crete and Aratus's Phaenomena — pagan poets who dimly saw the truth. Common grace has left partial insights even in unbelievers; these may be borrowed to witness to Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Christian may appropriate pagan truth wherever truth is found, redirecting it to the Author of truth. Creation's witness to God outlasts even idolatry.

verses.kjv_text: Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device.
verses.original_text: γένος οὖν ὑπάρχοντες τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ὀφείλομεν νομίζειν χρυσῷ ἢ ἀργύρῳ ἢ λίθῳ, χαράγματι τέχνης καὶ ἐνθυμήσεως ἀνθρώπου, τὸ θεῖον εἶναι ὅμοιον.
verses.transliteration: genos oun hyparchontes tou theou ouk opheilomen nomizein chrysō ē argyrō ē lithō, charagmati technēs kai enthymēseōs anthrōpou, to theion einai homoion.
verses.study_note: The argument from the greater to the lesser: if we His offspring are spirit and intellect, how much less can the God who made us be mere metal or stone? Cf. Isa 44:9-20.
verses.doctrinal_note: Man made in God's image cannot rightly worship what is less than himself. Every idol is a self-contradiction of the very image it perverts.

verses.kjv_text: And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent:
verses.original_text: τοὺς μὲν οὖν χρόνους τῆς ἀγνοίας ὑπεριδὼν ὁ θεὸς τὰ νῦν παραγγέλλει τοῖς ἀνθρώποις πάντας πανταχοῦ μετανοεῖν,
verses.transliteration: tous men oun chronous tēs agnoias hyperidōn ho theos ta nyn parangellei tois anthrōpois pantas pantachou metanoein,
verses.study_note: Hyperidōn, "having overlooked" — not approved, but patiently borne (cf. 14:16; Rom 3:25). The universal summons to repent (metanoein) is issued "now" because of the finished work of Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: Common grace toleration of past pagan ignorance is now superseded by a universal gospel summons. The cross and resurrection have changed the terms of history.

verses.kjv_text: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that he hath raised him from the dead.
verses.original_text: καθότι ἔστησεν ἡμέραν ἐν ᾗ μέλλει κρίνειν τὴν οἰκουμένην ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ ἐν ἀνδρὶ ᾧ ὥρισεν, πίστιν παρασχὼν πᾶσιν ἀναστήσας αὐτὸν ἐκ νεκρῶν.
verses.transliteration: kathoti estēsen hēmeran en hē mellei krinein tēn oikoumenēn en dikaiosynē en andri hō hōrisen, pistin paraschōn pasin anastēsas auton ek nekrōn.
verses.study_note: The sermon climaxes with judgment and resurrection. God has "furnished proof" (pistin paraschōn) by raising Jesus. The resurrection is God's public evidence that the Judge has been appointed.
verses.doctrinal_note: The resurrection is the centerpiece of history. It is both the guarantee of coming judgment and the only credential worth trusting for the One who will sit in it.

verses.kjv_text: And when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked: and others said, We will hear thee again of this matter.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσαντες δὲ ἀνάστασιν νεκρῶν οἱ μὲν ἐχλεύαζον, οἱ δὲ εἶπαν· Ἀκουσόμεθά σου περὶ τούτου καὶ πάλιν.
verses.transliteration: akousantes de anastasin nekrōn hoi men echleuazon, hoi de eipan; Akousometha sou peri toutou kai palin.
verses.study_note: Three responses: mockery, deferral, and belief (v.34). Greek philosophy found bodily resurrection absurd (cf. "ghosts," "shades"). Mockery often cloaks fear.
verses.doctrinal_note: The resurrection dividing point settles every hearer. Laughter, postponement, and faith are the three doors out of every pulpit.

verses.kjv_text: So Paul departed from among them.
verses.original_text: οὕτως ὁ Παῦλος ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ μέσου αὐτῶν·
verses.transliteration: houtōs ho Paulos exēlthen ek mesou autōn;
verses.study_note: The apostle does not linger to debate. When the hearing is concluded, he leaves, letting the word do its work.
verses.doctrinal_note: Ministers preach, then yield the verdict. The Spirit, not persuasion, saves.

verses.kjv_text: Howbeit certain men clave unto him, and believed: among the which was Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them.
verses.original_text: τινὲς δὲ ἄνδρες κολληθέντες αὐτῷ ἐπίστευσαν, ἐν οἷς καὶ Διονύσιος ὁ Ἀρεοπαγίτης καὶ γυνὴ ὀνόματι Δάμαρις καὶ ἕτεροι σὺν αὐτοῖς.
verses.transliteration: tines de andres kollēthentes autō episteusan, en hois kai Dionysios ho Areopagitēs kai gynē onomati Damaris kai heteroi syn autois.
verses.study_note: Kollēthentes, "glued" — unbreakable attachment. Dionysius was a member of the Areopagus council itself; tradition makes him first bishop of Athens. Damaris is the only woman named in the conversions of chapter 17.
verses.doctrinal_note: Even amid mockery and deferral, sovereign grace reaches the heart of an Areopagite. The gospel never preaches in vain; the Word claims its elect in every city.

2.meaning: Custom, habit — Paul's invariable pattern of going first to the synagogue.
Reasoned, dialogued — structured exegetical argument from Scripture.

3.meaning: Opening up, unlocking — opening the Scriptures as Christ did in Luke 24:45.
Setting before, presenting — laying out evidence methodically.
It was necessary to suffer — the divine necessity of the cross (Luke 24:26).

4.meaning: Were allotted with — divinely assigned as the apostle's portion.

5.meaning: Being envious — religious envy stirring opposition to the gospel.
Loafers of the marketplace — lowlife rabble hired for mob work.

6.meaning: Politarchs — civic rulers; title attested only in Thessalonian inscriptions.
Turned upside down, overturned — unwitting testimony to the gospel's world-transforming power.

7.meaning: Another king — the charge of political sedition leveled against Christ's kingship.

9.meaning: Sufficient, security bond — financial surety for peace.

11.meaning: More noble-minded — open-minded but not credulous.
Eagerness, readiness — the glad receptivity of the true hearer.
Examining judicially — testing by Scripture as the final court.

13.meaning: Agitating, shaking — satanic counter-earthquake against God's quake of deliverance.

16.meaning: Was provoked/stirred — holy indignation at idolatry.
Full of idols — unique NT word describing saturated idolatry.

17.meaning: Reasoned, dialogued — sustained public argument for Christ.

18.meaning: Epicureans — philosophers of pleasure, denying providence and afterlife.
Stoics — pantheistic moralists seeking virtue through impassivity.
Seed-picker, babbler — contemptuous term for a scrounging scavenger.

19.meaning: Mars Hill/Areopagus — the ancient Athenian council of elders.

20.meaning: Strange, foreign — novel to Athenian ears.

23.meaning: To the unknown god — Athenian altar inscription becoming gospel contact-point.
Being ignorant — chapter's keyword, pagan worship confessed as blind.

24.meaning: Made with hands — OT phrase for idols (Lev 26:1); echoes Stephen (7:48).

25.meaning: Needing in addition — God lacks nothing; He is absolutely self-sufficient.
Breath — echoes Gen 2:7, God breathing life into man.

26.meaning: From one — the unity of the human race in Adam.
Boundaries — the fixed limits of nations sovereignly determined.

27.meaning: Might grope — the blind reaching of darkened humanity after God.

28.meaning: Offspring, kind — general creatorship (not salvific sonship).

29.meaning: Engraving, image — fashioned idol made by hand and mind.

30.meaning: Having overlooked — past toleration of ignorance now ended.
To repent — mind-change toward God; universal summons now issued.

31.meaning: Has appointed a day — fixed divine verdict-day of world judgment.
Having furnished proof/assurance — resurrection as public evidence.
Having raised up — the resurrection is the divine vindication of Jesus.

32.meaning: Mocked, scoffed — typical philosophical dismissal of bodily resurrection.

34.meaning: Glued, joined — inseparable attachment of true converts.
Areopagite — member of the Athenian high council.

meta.overview: Acts 18 tells of Paul's eighteen-month ministry at Corinth — a wealthy, immoral port city where he lodges with Aquila and Priscilla, tentmakers exiled from Rome by Claudius. Reasoning weekly in the synagogue, he is opposed and blasphemed; he shakes his raiment and turns to the Gentiles, dwelling next door at the house of Justus. Crispus, ruler of the synagogue, believes with his household, and many Corinthians are converted and baptized. The Lord speaks to Paul by night: "Be not afraid, for I have much people in this city." After eighteen months, the Jews drag Paul before Gallio, proconsul of Achaia, who refuses to judge a religious dispute. Paul then sails for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila, leaves them at Ephesus, keeps a vow at Cenchrea, visits Jerusalem, and returns to Antioch, ending the second journey. The chapter closes by introducing Apollos, the eloquent Alexandrian Jew whose partial knowledge is completed by Priscilla and Aquila, opening the third journey.
meta.theme: The Lord's Many People in a Hard City
meta.keyWordMeaning: people (laos) — the chosen covenant-people of God, elect from every city including Corinth, guaranteed by the Lord's own promise (v.10).

outline.title: Corinth: Paul with Aquila and Priscilla, Tentmaking and Reasoning
Turning to the Gentiles; the Lord's Promise by Night
Gallio's Refusal: the Religious Charge Dismissed
Return Journey: Cenchrea, Ephesus, Jerusalem, Antioch
Third Journey Begins; Apollos Taught the Way More Perfectly

verses.kjv_text: After these things Paul departed from Athens, and came to Corinth;
verses.original_text: Μετὰ ταῦτα χωρισθεὶς ἐκ τῶν Ἀθηνῶν ἦλθεν εἰς Κόρινθον.
verses.transliteration: Meta tauta chōristheis ek tōn Athēnōn ēlthen eis Korinthon.
verses.study_note: From Athens — city of philosophers — to Corinth — city of commerce and lust. Corinth, a seaport of 200,000, hosted the temple of Aphrodite with its 1,000 cult prostitutes. Paul arrived "in weakness, and in fear, and in much trembling" (1 Cor 2:3).
verses.doctrinal_note: God sends His servants not only to intellectual strongholds but to sensual ones. No city is beyond the reach of the gospel, though every city has its own fortress.

verses.kjv_text: And found a certain Jew named Aquila, born in Pontus, lately come from Italy, with his wife Priscilla; (because that Claudius had commanded all Jews to depart from Rome) and came unto them.
verses.original_text: καὶ εὑρών τινα Ἰουδαῖον ὀνόματι Ἀκύλαν, Ποντικὸν τῷ γένει, προσφάτως ἐληλυθότα ἀπὸ τῆς Ἰταλίας καὶ Πρίσκιλλαν γυναῖκα αὐτοῦ, διὰ τὸ διατεταχέναι Κλαύδιον χωρίζεσθαι πάντας τοὺς Ἰουδαίους ἀπὸ τῆς Ῥώμης, προσῆλθεν αὐτοῖς·
verses.transliteration: kai heurōn tina Ioudaion onomati Akylan, Pontikon tō genei, prosphatōs elēlythota apo tēs Italias kai Priskillan gynaika autou, dia to diatetachenai Klaudion chōrizesthai pantas tous Ioudaious apo tēs Rhōmēs, prosēlthen autois;
verses.study_note: Suetonius (Claudius 25.4) records Claudius expelled Jews from Rome c. AD 49 because of riots "at the instigation of Chrestus" — probably synagogue disputes about Christ. Priscilla is always named first in three of six NT references, suggesting prominence.
verses.doctrinal_note: Imperial edicts serve God's providential purposes. The very expulsion that displaced Aquila and Priscilla placed them beside Paul in Corinth, then in Ephesus, then in Rome — strategic fruit no emperor intended.

verses.kjv_text: And because he was of the same craft, he abode with them, and wrought: for by their occupation they were tentmakers.
verses.original_text: καὶ διὰ τὸ ὁμότεχνον εἶναι ἔμενεν παρ᾽ αὐτοῖς καὶ ἠργάζετο· ἦσαν γὰρ σκηνοποιοὶ τῇ τέχνῃ.
verses.transliteration: kai dia to homotechnon einai emenen par autois kai ērgazeto; ēsan gar skēnopoioi tē technē.
verses.study_note: Skēnopoioi, "tentmakers," likely worked in cilicium (goat-hair cloth from Cilicia) for tents, sails, and cloaks. Rabbis traditionally supported themselves by a trade. Paul later points to this manual labour (1 Cor 4:12; 9:6; 1 Thess 2:9; 2 Thess 3:8).
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle labours with his own hands that he might not burden his hearers. Honest craft is no dishonour to ministry; bivocational mission preserves integrity where patrons could corrupt it.

verses.kjv_text: And he reasoned in the synagogue every sabbath, and persuaded the Jews and the Greeks.
verses.original_text: διελέγετο δὲ ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ κατὰ πᾶν σάββατον, ἔπειθέν τε Ἰουδαίους καὶ Ἕλληνας.
verses.transliteration: dielegeto de en tē synagōgē kata pan sabbaton, epeithen te Ioudaious kai Hellēnas.
verses.study_note: The same word "reasoned" (dielegeto) and weekly pattern as Thessalonica and Athens. "Persuaded" (epeithen) is imperfect — ongoing effort.
verses.doctrinal_note: The preacher's task is not merely to state but to persuade. Week by week the Word must press upon both Jew and Greek until conscience yields to Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And when Silas and Timotheus were come from Macedonia, Paul was pressed in the spirit, and testified to the Jews that Jesus was Christ.
verses.original_text: Ὡς δὲ κατῆλθον ἀπὸ τῆς Μακεδονίας ὅ τε Σιλᾶς καὶ ὁ Τιμόθεος, συνείχετο τῷ λόγῳ ὁ Παῦλος, διαμαρτυρόμενος τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις εἶναι τὸν χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: Hōs de katēlthon apo tēs Makedonias ho te Silas kai ho Timotheos, syneicheto tō logō ho Paulos, diamartyromenos tois Ioudaiois einai ton christon Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: Syneicheto, "was constrained/gripped," by the word. The Macedonian gift (2 Cor 11:9; Phil 4:15) allowed Paul to lay aside tentmaking and preach full-time.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gospel labour deserves gospel support. Gifts from faithful churches release apostles from secular toil and multiply their gospel output.

verses.kjv_text: And when they opposed themselves, and blasphemed, he shook his raiment, and said unto them, Your blood be upon your own heads; I am clean: from henceforth I will go unto the Gentiles.
verses.original_text: ἀντιτασσομένων δὲ αὐτῶν καὶ βλασφημούντων ἐκτιναξάμενος τὰ ἱμάτια εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς· Τὸ αἷμα ὑμῶν ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν ὑμῶν· καθαρὸς ἐγώ· ἀπὸ τοῦ νῦν εἰς τὰ ἔθνη πορεύσομαι.
verses.transliteration: antitassomenōn de autōn kai blasphēmountōn ektinaxamenos ta himatia eipen pros autous; To haima hymōn epi tēn kephalēn hymōn; katharos egō; apo tou nyn eis ta ethnē poreusomai.
verses.study_note: Shaking garments — the prophetic sign of disassociation and judgment (cf. Neh 5:13). "Your blood be upon your own heads" — watchman-language from Ezek 33:4. Paul had discharged his responsibility.
verses.doctrinal_note: The preacher is a watchman. When he has faithfully warned and is rejected, responsibility falls on the hearer. The blood of souls is on apostate lips that refuse the gospel.

verses.kjv_text: And he departed thence, and entered into a certain man's house, named Justus, one that worshipped God, whose house joined hard to the synagogue.
verses.original_text: καὶ μεταβὰς ἐκεῖθεν ἦλθεν εἰς οἰκίαν τινὸς ὀνόματι Τιτίου Ἰούστου σεβομένου τὸν θεόν, οὗ ἡ οἰκία ἦν συνομοροῦσα τῇ συναγωγῇ.
verses.transliteration: kai metabas ekeithen ēlthen eis oikian tinos onomati Titiou Ioustou sebomenou ton theon, hou hē oikia ēn synomorousa tē synagōgē.
verses.study_note: Poetic judgment: the new Christian meeting-place is right next door to the synagogue. Titius Justus (Roman name, God-fearer) opens his home.
verses.doctrinal_note: When one door closes, the Lord opens another directly adjacent. Household hospitality is often the first infrastructure of a new church plant.

verses.kjv_text: And Crispus, the chief ruler of the synagogue, believed on the Lord with all his house; and many of the Corinthians hearing believed, and were baptized.
verses.original_text: Κρίσπος δὲ ὁ ἀρχισυνάγωγος ἐπίστευσεν τῷ κυρίῳ σὺν ὅλῳ τῷ οἴκῳ αὐτοῦ, καὶ πολλοὶ τῶν Κορινθίων ἀκούοντες ἐπίστευον καὶ ἐβαπτίζοντο.
verses.transliteration: Krispos de ho archisynagōgos episteusen tō kyriō syn holō tō oikō autou, kai polloi tōn Korinthiōn akouontes episteuon kai ebaptizonto.
verses.study_note: Crispus — the synagogue ruler! — converts with his whole household. Paul baptized him personally (1 Cor 1:14). Many Corinthians follow.
verses.doctrinal_note: Grace reaches the very top of synagogue hierarchy. When a ruler's heart bows, household salvation often follows, and the influence ripples through the whole city.

verses.kjv_text: Then spake the Lord to Paul in the night by a vision, Be not afraid, but speak, and hold not thy peace:
verses.original_text: Εἶπεν δὲ ὁ κύριος ἐν νυκτὶ δι᾽ ὁράματος τῷ Παύλῳ· Μὴ φοβοῦ, ἀλλὰ λάλει καὶ μὴ σιωπήσῃς,
verses.transliteration: Eipen de ho kyrios en nykti di horamatos tō Paulō; Mē phobou, alla lalei kai mē siōpēsēs,
verses.study_note: Fear is a real temptation even to apostles. The Lord's "fear not" comes in a night-vision — the same format as the Macedonian call (16:9).
verses.doctrinal_note: The Lord does not shame His servants' fears; He meets them with His presence and a renewed command. Courage is not the absence of fear but the obedience that outlasts it.

verses.kjv_text: For I am with thee, and no man shall set on thee to hurt thee: for I have much people in this city.
verses.original_text: διότι ἐγώ εἰμι μετὰ σοῦ καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐπιθήσεταί σοι τοῦ κακῶσαί σε, διότι λαός ἐστίν μοι πολὺς ἐν τῇ πόλει ταύτῃ.
verses.transliteration: dioti egō eimi meta sou kai oudeis epithēsetai soi tou kakōsai se, dioti laos estin moi polys en tē polei tautē.
verses.study_note: Three promises: (1) "I am with thee" — divine presence; (2) personal protection; (3) "I have much people" — the elect, already named as His before they believe. Laos is OT covenant-people language (Exod 6:7), now applied to elect Corinthians.
verses.doctrinal_note: Election guarantees mission. Before a soul comes to Christ, Christ already calls them His. The preacher is not trying to make God's people; he is gathering those already chosen.

verses.kjv_text: And he continued there a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.
verses.original_text: Ἐκάθισεν δὲ ἐνιαυτὸν καὶ μῆνας ἓξ διδάσκων ἐν αὐτοῖς τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Ekathisen de eniauton kai mēnas hex didaskōn en autois ton logon tou theou.
verses.study_note: Eighteen months — Paul's longest settled ministry apart from Ephesus (three years) and Rome. Out of this labour came the Corinthian church, a future pastoral epistle, and the start of Romans.
verses.doctrinal_note: Long patient teaching yields lasting churches. Roots must go deep before fruit can hang heavy.

verses.kjv_text: And when Gallio was the deputy of Achaia, the Jews made insurrection with one accord against Paul, and brought him to the judgment seat,
verses.original_text: Γαλλίωνος δὲ ἀνθυπάτου ὄντος τῆς Ἀχαΐας κατεπέστησαν ὁμοθυμαδὸν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι τῷ Παύλῳ καὶ ἤγαγον αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὸ βῆμα,
verses.transliteration: Galliōnos de anthypatou ontos tēs Achaias katepestēsan homothymadon hoi Ioudaioi tō Paulō kai ēgagon auton epi to bēma,
verses.study_note: Gallio (L. Junius Annaeus Gallio, brother of Seneca) served as proconsul of Achaia July 51–52 AD — the Delphi inscription (Claudius letter) fixes this date, anchoring Pauline chronology. The bēma (judgment seat) still stands visible at Corinth today.
verses.doctrinal_note: Providence positions just the right magistrate for the right moment. Gallio's even-handed ruling effectively made Christianity a legal religion throughout Achaia.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, This fellow persuadeth men to worship God contrary to the law.
verses.original_text: λέγοντες ὅτι Παρὰ τὸν νόμον ἀναπείθει οὗτος τοὺς ἀνθρώπους σέβεσθαι τὸν θεόν.
verses.transliteration: legontes hoti Para ton nomon anapeithei houtos tous anthrōpous sebesthai ton theon.
verses.study_note: "Law" is deliberately ambiguous — Roman law (where Judaism was protected) or Mosaic law? The accusers hope Gallio will treat Christianity as an unlicensed new religion.
verses.doctrinal_note: Ambiguous legal charges against Christians are ancient. The gospel must be defended before magistrates with wisdom as well as courage.

verses.kjv_text: And when Paul was now about to open his mouth, Gallio said unto the Jews, If it were a matter of wrong or wicked lewdness, O ye Jews, reason would that I should bear with you:
verses.original_text: μέλλοντος δὲ τοῦ Παύλου ἀνοίγειν τὸ στόμα εἶπεν ὁ Γαλλίων πρὸς τοὺς Ἰουδαίους· Εἰ μὲν ἦν ἀδίκημά τι ἢ ῥᾳδιούργημα πονηρόν, ὦ Ἰουδαῖοι, κατὰ λόγον ἂν ἀνεσχόμην ὑμῶν·
verses.transliteration: mellontos de tou Paulou anoigein to stoma eipen ho Galliōn pros tous Ioudaious; Ei men ēn adikēma ti ē rhadiourgēma ponēron, ō Ioudaioi, kata logon an aneschomēn hymōn;
verses.study_note: Gallio intervenes before Paul can speak, perceiving the charge as religious, not criminal. "Wrong" (adikēma) vs. "mere words" — the Roman distinction between actionable crime and religious squabble.
verses.doctrinal_note: When the world accidentally defends the gospel, the Christian may quietly benefit. God turns even pagan indifference into protective shield for His church.

verses.kjv_text: But if it be a question of words and names, and of your law, look ye to it; for I will be no judge of such matters.
verses.original_text: εἰ δὲ ζητήματά ἐστιν περὶ λόγου καὶ ὀνομάτων καὶ νόμου τοῦ καθ᾽ ὑμᾶς, ὄψεσθε αὐτοί· κριτὴς ἐγὼ τούτων οὐ βούλομαι εἶναι.
verses.transliteration: ei de zētēmata estin peri logou kai onomatōn kai nomou tou kath hymas, opsesthe autoi; kritēs egō toutōn ou boulomai einai.
verses.study_note: Gallio dismisses the matter as internal Jewish dispute. "Look ye to it" — handle it yourselves. A magistrate who refuses to arbitrate theology frees the apostolate to teach.
verses.doctrinal_note: Separation of the civil and the religious courts, when well-kept, is a friend to the gospel. Where Caesar declines to judge Christ, Christ's subjects enjoy liberty to preach.

verses.kjv_text: And he drave them from the judgment seat.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπήλασεν αὐτοὺς ἀπὸ τοῦ βήματος.
verses.transliteration: kai apēlasen autous apo tou bēmatos.
verses.study_note: Apēlasen — "drove off." A definitive dismissal. The precedent holds for subsequent governors in Achaia.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Lord's word "no man shall set on thee to hurt thee" (v.10) is kept by an indifferent Roman judge.

verses.kjv_text: Then all the Greeks took Sosthenes, the chief ruler of the synagogue, and beat him before the judgment seat. And Gallio cared for none of those things.
verses.original_text: ἐπιλαβόμενοι δὲ πάντες Σωσθένην τὸν ἀρχισυνάγωγον ἔτυπτον ἔμπροσθεν τοῦ βήματος· καὶ οὐδὲν τούτων τῷ Γαλλίωνι ἔμελεν.
verses.transliteration: epilabomenoi de pantes Sōsthenēn ton archisynagōgon etypton emprosthen tou bēmatos; kai ouden toutōn tō Galliōni emelen.
verses.study_note: A Greek mob beats Sosthenes — Crispus's successor as synagogue ruler — and Gallio ignores the anti-Semitic violence. Likely this is the same Sosthenes later found beside Paul as "our brother" (1 Cor 1:1) — evidence that trials purify, and that the gospel reaches even persecutors.
verses.doctrinal_note: Even persecutors become brethren. The one who beats the synagogue ruler today may find him kneeling at Christ's feet tomorrow.

verses.kjv_text: And Paul after this tarried there yet a good while, and then took his leave of the brethren, and sailed thence into Syria, and with him Priscilla and Aquila; having shorn his head in Cenchrea: for he had a vow.
verses.original_text: Ὁ δὲ Παῦλος ἔτι προσμείνας ἡμέρας ἱκανὰς τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς ἀποταξάμενος ἐξέπλει εἰς τὴν Συρίαν, καὶ σὺν αὐτῷ Πρίσκιλλα καὶ Ἀκύλας, κειράμενος ἐν Κεγχρεαῖς τὴν κεφαλήν, εἶχεν γὰρ εὐχήν.
verses.transliteration: Ho de Paulos eti prosmeinas hēmeras hikanas tois adelphois apotaxamenos exeplei eis tēn Syrian, kai syn autō Priskilla kai Akylas, keiramenos en Kenchreais tēn kephalēn, eichen gar euchēn.
verses.study_note: Cenchrea is Corinth's east port. The vow (possibly a Nazirite-type, Num 6) was likely taken in thanksgiving for the Lord's preservation at Corinth. Head-shaving marked the vow's completion.
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle who taught liberty from the Law used Jewish pious customs voluntarily when they served gratitude. Freedom includes the freedom to fulfil vows.

verses.kjv_text: And he came to Ephesus, and left them there: but he himself entered into the synagogue, and reasoned with the Jews.
verses.original_text: κατήντησαν δὲ εἰς Ἔφεσον, κἀκείνους κατέλιπεν αὐτοῦ, αὐτὸς δὲ εἰσελθὼν εἰς τὴν συναγωγὴν διελέξατο τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις.
verses.transliteration: katēntēsan de eis Epheson, kakeinous katelipen autou, autos de eiselthōn eis tēn synagōgēn dielexato tois Ioudaiois.
verses.study_note: Ephesus — future field of Paul's third journey. He leaves Priscilla and Aquila there to prepare the way, himself stopping only briefly.
verses.doctrinal_note: Strategic advance work by lay-workers prepares the ground for later apostolic labour. Not every founder plants the whole field personally.

verses.kjv_text: When they desired him to tarry longer time with them, he consented not;
verses.original_text: ἐρωτώντων δὲ αὐτῶν ἐπὶ πλείονα χρόνον μεῖναι οὐκ ἐπένευσεν,
verses.transliteration: erōtōntōn de autōn epi pleiona chronon meinai ouk epeneusen,
verses.study_note: Paul refuses the Ephesian invitation now but will return for three years (19:10). Strategic patience — the harvest is Ephesian but the time is not yet.
verses.doctrinal_note: The servant of God is timed to the Master's calendar. Saying "no" now is sometimes how one says "yes" later.

verses.kjv_text: But bade them farewell, saying, I must by all means keep this feast that cometh in Jerusalem: but I will return again unto you, if God will. And he sailed from Ephesus.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ ἀποταξάμενος καὶ εἰπών· Πάλιν ἀνακάμψω πρὸς ὑμᾶς τοῦ θεοῦ θέλοντος ἀνήχθη ἀπὸ τῆς Ἐφέσου·
verses.transliteration: alla apotaxamenos kai eipōn; Palin anakampsō pros hymas tou theou thelontos anēchthē apo tēs Ephesou;
verses.study_note: "If God will" (tou theou thelontos) — the Pauline surrender to divine planning (cf. Jas 4:15). Subsequent plans are always contingent on God's decree.
verses.doctrinal_note: Every servant of God must hold his plans loosely. "If the Lord will" is not pious politeness but honest creaturely dependence.

verses.kjv_text: And when he had landed at Caesarea, and gone up, and saluted the church, he went down to Antioch.
verses.original_text: καὶ κατελθὼν εἰς Καισάρειαν, ἀναβὰς καὶ ἀσπασάμενος τὴν ἐκκλησίαν, κατέβη εἰς Ἀντιόχειαν.
verses.transliteration: kai katelthōn eis Kaisareian, anabas kai aspasamenos tēn ekklēsian, katebē eis Antiocheian.
verses.study_note: "Went up" implies Jerusalem — the mother church is greeted before Paul descends to Antioch. The second journey ends where it began (15:40).
verses.doctrinal_note: Missionary journeys begin and end in accountability to the sending church. Apostolic autonomy is not independence; it is ordered connection.

verses.kjv_text: And after he had spent some time there, he departed, and went over all the country of Galatia and Phrygia in order, strengthening all the disciples.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ποιήσας χρόνον τινὰ ἐξῆλθεν, διερχόμενος καθεξῆς τὴν Γαλατικὴν χώραν καὶ Φρυγίαν, ἐπιστηρίζων πάντας τοὺς μαθητάς.
verses.transliteration: Kai poiēsas chronon tina exēlthen, dierchomenos kathexēs tēn Galatikēn chōran kai Phrygian, epistērizōn pantas tous mathētas.
verses.study_note: The third journey begins. "Strengthening" (epistērizōn) — pastoral follow-up through the same regions evangelised earlier.
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle never forgets a flock he has planted. Gospel mission is not merely opening doors but strengthening those who have entered.

verses.kjv_text: And a certain Jew named Apollos, born at Alexandria, an eloquent man, and mighty in the scriptures, came to Ephesus.
verses.original_text: Ἰουδαῖος δέ τις Ἀπολλὼς ὀνόματι, Ἀλεξανδρεὺς τῷ γένει, ἀνὴρ λόγιος, κατήντησεν εἰς Ἔφεσον, δυνατὸς ὢν ἐν ταῖς γραφαῖς.
verses.transliteration: Ioudaios de tis Apollōs onomati, Alexandreus tō genei, anēr logios, katēntēsen eis Epheson, dynatos ōn en tais graphais.
verses.study_note: Alexandria was home to a massive Jewish community and the LXX. "Eloquent" (logios) + "mighty in the Scriptures" — a rare combination of rhetorical skill and biblical mastery. Apollos later labours in Corinth (1 Cor 1:12; 3:6).
verses.doctrinal_note: God equips His church with varied gifts. Eloquence joined to Scripture is a formidable instrument; neither alone will do.

verses.kjv_text: This man was instructed in the way of the Lord; and being fervent in the spirit, he spake and taught diligently the things of the Lord, knowing only the baptism of John.
verses.original_text: οὗτος ἦν κατηχημένος τὴν ὁδὸν τοῦ κυρίου, καὶ ζέων τῷ πνεύματι ἐλάλει καὶ ἐδίδασκεν ἀκριβῶς τὰ περὶ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ, ἐπιστάμενος μόνον τὸ βάπτισμα Ἰωάννου.
verses.transliteration: houtos ēn katēchēmenos tēn hodon tou kyriou, kai zeōn tō pneumati elalei kai edidasken akribōs ta peri tou Iēsou, epistamenos monon to baptisma Iōannou.
verses.study_note: Katēchēmenos, "catechised" (root of "catechism"). Zeōn, "boiling hot," of spiritual fervour (cf. Rom 12:11). His message was true as far as it went — centred on Jesus — but he knew only John's baptism (repentance), lacking the full revelation of the Spirit's coming at Pentecost.
verses.doctrinal_note: Partial truth, fervently taught, may accomplish much; fuller truth, humbly received, accomplishes more. Spiritual zeal with theological gaps must be corrected, not quenched.

verses.kjv_text: And he began to speak boldly in the synagogue: whom when Aquila and Priscilla had heard, they took him unto them, and expounded unto him the way of God more perfectly.
verses.original_text: οὗτός τε ἤρξατο παρρησιάζεσθαι ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ· ἀκούσαντες δὲ αὐτοῦ Πρίσκιλλα καὶ Ἀκύλας προσελάβοντο αὐτὸν καὶ ἀκριβέστερον αὐτῷ ἐξέθεντο τὴν ὁδὸν τοῦ θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: houtos te ērxato parrēsiazesthai en tē synagōgē; akousantes de autou Priskilla kai Akylas proselabonto auton kai akribesteron autō exethento tēn hodon tou theou.
verses.study_note: Priscilla and Aquila, tentmakers and laypeople, instruct a man "mighty in the Scriptures." Private, gracious correction — not public rebuke. "More perfectly" (akribesteron) — more accurately.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gifted teachers need humble mentors. A great preacher can sit under a laywoman's instruction without shame when gospel fidelity is at stake.

verses.kjv_text: And when he was disposed to pass into Achaia, the brethren wrote, exhorting the disciples to receive him: who, when he was come, helped them much which had believed through grace:
verses.original_text: βουλομένου δὲ αὐτοῦ διελθεῖν εἰς τὴν Ἀχαΐαν προτρεψάμενοι οἱ ἀδελφοὶ ἔγραψαν τοῖς μαθηταῖς ἀποδέξασθαι αὐτόν· ὃς παραγενόμενος συνεβάλετο πολὺ τοῖς πεπιστευκόσιν διὰ τῆς χάριτος·
verses.transliteration: boulomenou de autou dielthein eis tēn Achaian protrepsamenoi hoi adelphoi egrapsan tois mathētais apodexasthai auton; hos paragenomenos synebaleto poly tois pepisteukosin dia tēs charitos;
verses.study_note: Letter of commendation (cf. Rom 16:1; 2 Cor 3:1) — the NT pattern for inter-church ministry recognition. "Through grace" (dia tēs charitos) — the Corinthians' faith and Apollos's help are both gifts of grace.
verses.doctrinal_note: Commended ministers build up what God has already established by grace. The church's health depends on recognizable, accountable movement of gifted teachers between congregations.

verses.kjv_text: For he mightily convinced the Jews, and that publickly, shewing by the scriptures that Jesus was Christ.
verses.original_text: εὐτόνως γὰρ τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις διακατηλέγχετο δημοσίᾳ ἐπιδεικνὺς διὰ τῶν γραφῶν εἶναι τὸν χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: eutonōs gar tois Ioudaiois diakatēlencheto dēmosia epideiknys dia tōn graphōn einai ton christon Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: Eutonōs, "with tension/intensity." Diakatēlencheto is a doubly-intensified verb — "thoroughly refuted." Apollos's scriptural demonstration that Jesus is the Christ leaves no Jewish argument standing.
verses.doctrinal_note: Rightly handled Scripture is unanswerable to the candid conscience. The Christ of the prophets defends Himself when His servants simply open the Book.

1.meaning: Corinth — wealthy Achaian seaport, infamous for immorality and commerce.

2.meaning: Aquila — Pontian Jew, tentmaker, co-labourer with Paul in three cities.
Priscilla — wife of Aquila, often named first, denoting prominence in ministry.

3.meaning: Tentmakers — probably leather or cilicium workers; Paul's bivocational trade.

4.meaning: Was persuading — ongoing rational argument until conviction.

5.meaning: Was gripped/constrained — by the urgency of the preached word.
Testifying solemnly — full weight of a divine witness.

6.meaning: Having shaken out — prophetic sign of disassociation from guilt (Neh 5:13).

8.meaning: Ruler of the synagogue — chief administrative officer; Crispus (1 Cor 1:14).

9.meaning: Do not fear — Christ's reassurance in the face of opposition.

10.meaning: People — OT covenant term (Exod 6:7); elect converts not yet gathered.

11.meaning: Settled, sat down — long-term ministry rather than transit.

12.meaning: Proconsul — senatorial governor of Achaia; Gallio in AD 51-52.
Judgment seat — elevated platform from which the proconsul ruled.

14.meaning: Wrong, crime — actionable offense versus mere religious dispute.

15.meaning: Questions, disputes — matters of internal religious debate.

16.meaning: Drove away — decisive dismissal from court.

17.meaning: Sosthenes — synagogue ruler, likely later converted (1 Cor 1:1).

18.meaning: Cenchrea — eastern port of Corinth; site of local church (Rom 16:1).

21.meaning: God willing — Pauline surrender to divine sovereignty in planning.

23.meaning: Strengthening — recurrent Lukan verb for pastoral follow-up.

24.meaning: Alexandrian — from Alexandria, center of Hellenistic Jewish learning.
Eloquent, learned — skilled in speech and argument.
Mighty in the Scriptures — rare combination of rhetoric and exegesis.

25.meaning: Catechised, orally instructed — systematic discipleship rooted in the Way.
Boiling/fervent in spirit — the zealous heart for Christ.
Accurately, diligently — careful teaching of Jesus as far as he knew.

26.meaning: To speak boldly — with Spirit-given freedom of speech.
They took to themselves — welcomed into private instruction.
More accurately — fuller clarity added to an already-true foundation.

27.meaning: To welcome, receive fully — inter-church hospitality by letter.
Through grace — divine enabling of both converts and teachers.

28.meaning: Vigorously, with intensity — Apollos's powerful public argument.

meta.overview: Acts 19 records Paul's three-year ministry at Ephesus, the most fruitful and contested of his campaigns. He finds twelve disciples baptized only into John's baptism; upon hearing of Christ and being baptized in His name, the Spirit comes upon them. Paul reasons three months in the synagogue, then withdraws to the school of Tyrannus, where he lectures daily for two years, so that all Asia hears the word. God works special miracles — handkerchiefs from Paul's body heal the sick — and the seven sons of Sceva, attempting to exploit the Name, are overpowered by an evil spirit, so that the name of the Lord Jesus is magnified. Many burn their magical books, worth 50,000 pieces of silver. Finally Demetrius, a silversmith whose trade depends on Artemis shrines, stirs the city; a riot fills the theatre for two hours with the cry "Great is Diana of the Ephesians!" The town clerk quiets the crowd with reasoned Roman law. The word of God has so grown and prevailed that the economic engine of pagan worship is shaken to its foundations.
meta.theme: The Name of the Lord Jesus Magnified
meta.keyWordMeaning: was magnified (emegalyneto) — the name of the Lord Jesus exalted over all competing powers, names, and economies (v.17).

outline.title: Twelve Ephesian Disciples: From John's Baptism to the Holy Spirit
Synagogue to the School of Tyrannus: Two Years of Daily Teaching
Special Miracles, the Sons of Sceva, Magic Books Burned
Paul's Further Plans: Macedonia, Achaia, Jerusalem, Rome
Demetrius Stirs the Silversmiths; Riot in the Theatre
The Town Clerk Disperses the Assembly

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, while Apollos was at Corinth, Paul having passed through the upper coasts came to Ephesus: and finding certain disciples,
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ τὸν Ἀπολλῶ εἶναι ἐν Κορίνθῳ Παῦλον διελθόντα τὰ ἀνωτερικὰ μέρη κατελθεῖν εἰς Ἔφεσον καὶ εὑρεῖν τινας μαθητάς,
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō ton Apollō einai en Korinthō Paulon dielthonta ta anōterika merē katelthein eis Epheson kai heurein tinas mathētas,
verses.study_note: While Apollos labours in Corinth, Paul moves overland through the "upper country" (Phrygian highlands) to the Aegean coast. "Certain disciples" — their status is unclear until further inquiry; they have some knowledge of Christ but incomplete.
verses.doctrinal_note: Two men, two cities, one Lord — Apollos watering at Corinth, Paul planting at Ephesus, the Spirit giving the increase (1 Cor 3:6).

verses.kjv_text: He said unto them, Have ye received the Holy Ghost since ye believed? And they said unto him, We have not so much as heard whether there be any Holy Ghost.
verses.original_text: εἶπέν τε πρὸς αὐτούς· Εἰ πνεῦμα ἅγιον ἐλάβετε πιστεύσαντες; οἱ δὲ πρὸς αὐτόν· Ἀλλ᾽ οὐδὲ εἰ πνεῦμα ἅγιον ἔστιν ἠκούσαμεν.
verses.transliteration: eipen te pros autous; Ei pneuma hagion elabete pisteusantes? hoi de pros auton; All oude ei pneuma hagion estin ēkousamen.
verses.study_note: Their reply does not deny Spirit's existence (the OT they knew speaks often of Him) but confesses ignorance of His Pentecostal descent. They were disciples of John's preparatory ministry, not yet of the risen and ascended Christ who has poured out the Spirit.
verses.doctrinal_note: Reception of the Spirit is the hallmark of NT salvation, not an optional second blessing. Wherever faith rests on the ascended Christ, the Spirit is given.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Unto what then were ye baptized? And they said, Unto John's baptism.
verses.original_text: εἶπέν τε· Εἰς τί οὖν ἐβαπτίσθητε; οἱ δὲ εἶπαν· Εἰς τὸ Ἰωάννου βάπτισμα.
verses.transliteration: eipen te; Eis ti oun ebaptisthēte? hoi de eipan; Eis to Iōannou baptisma.
verses.study_note: Paul's diagnostic question: "into what were ye baptized?" The Christian baptism is "into Christ" (Gal 3:27; Rom 6:3) — theirs was only into John's preparatory rite of repentance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The object of baptism determines its meaning. True Christian baptism confesses faith in the crucified, risen, and Spirit-sending Christ.

verses.kjv_text: Then said Paul, John verily baptized with the baptism of repentance, saying unto the people, that they should believe on him which should come after him, that is, on Christ Jesus.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ Παῦλος· Ἰωάννης ἐβάπτισεν βάπτισμα μετανοίας, τῷ λαῷ λέγων εἰς τὸν ἐρχόμενον μετ᾽ αὐτὸν ἵνα πιστεύσωσιν, τοῦτ᾽ ἔστιν εἰς τὸν Ἰησοῦν.
verses.transliteration: eipen de Paulos; Iōannēs ebaptisen baptisma metanoias, tō laō legōn eis ton erchomenon met auton hina pisteusōsin, tout estin eis ton Iēsoun.
verses.study_note: John's own preaching pointed forward to One greater (Luke 3:16). Paul's brief summary places Jesus as the fulfilled object of John's forward-looking message.
verses.doctrinal_note: The old covenant points to Christ; the new covenant rests in Him. Any message that stops at repentance without arriving at faith in Jesus is incomplete.

verses.kjv_text: When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.
verses.original_text: ἀκούσαντες δὲ ἐβαπτίσθησαν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ.
verses.transliteration: akousantes de ebaptisthēsan eis to onoma tou kyriou Iēsou.
verses.study_note: Baptism "into the name of the Lord Jesus" — the only instance of explicit rebaptism in the NT, because the earlier baptism was into an incomplete revelation. The baptismal formula names Jesus as the Lord of the new covenant.
verses.doctrinal_note: Christian baptism belongs to the name of Jesus. Where faith comes to Christ more fully than before, baptism into His name is the natural consequence.

verses.kjv_text: And when Paul had laid his hands upon them, the Holy Ghost came on them; and they spake with tongues, and prophesied.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπιθέντος αὐτοῖς τοῦ Παύλου τὰς χεῖρας ἦλθεν τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον ἐπ᾽ αὐτούς, ἐλάλουν τε γλώσσαις καὶ ἐπροφήτευον.
verses.transliteration: kai epithentos autois tou Paulou tas cheiras ēlthen to pneuma to hagion ep autous, elaloun te glōssais kai eprophēteuon.
verses.study_note: Laying on of hands mediates the apostolic recognition. Tongues and prophecy are the visible sign — the Ephesian "Pentecost" parallels the Jerusalem, Samaritan (8:17), and Caesarean (10:46) ones, bringing the remaining OT category (disciples of John) fully into the new covenant.
verses.doctrinal_note: God graciously gave public sign-manifestations at each major frontier of the Spirit's outpouring. The uniform grace has a uniform witness, binding the one church across every former barrier.

verses.kjv_text: And all the men were about twelve.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν δὲ οἱ πάντες ἄνδρες ὡσεὶ δώδεκα.
verses.transliteration: ēsan de hoi pantes andres hōsei dōdeka.
verses.study_note: The number twelve is suggestive but not symbolic — Luke says "about twelve." They are a remnant of John's disciples now fully folded into the Christian church.
verses.doctrinal_note: Every disciple belongs either to the still-incomplete gospel or to the fulfilled one. Christ completes what John announced.

verses.kjv_text: And he went into the synagogue, and spake boldly for the space of three months, disputing and persuading the things concerning the kingdom of God.
verses.original_text: Εἰσελθὼν δὲ εἰς τὴν συναγωγὴν ἐπαρρησιάζετο ἐπὶ μῆνας τρεῖς διαλεγόμενος καὶ πείθων περὶ τῆς βασιλείας τοῦ θεοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Eiselthōn de eis tēn synagōgēn eparrēsiazeto epi mēnas treis dialegomenos kai peithōn peri tēs basileias tou theou.
verses.study_note: Three months, a longer synagogue tenure than anywhere else — evidence of initial Ephesian openness. Paul's theme is "the kingdom of God" — the reign inaugurated by Christ's resurrection (28:31).
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel is the message of the kingdom of God in Christ. Where rightly preached, it always seeks persuasion, not mere declaration.

verses.kjv_text: But when divers were hardened, and believed not, but spake evil of that way before the multitude, he departed from them, and separated the disciples, disputing daily in the school of one Tyrannus.
verses.original_text: ὡς δέ τινες ἐσκληρύνοντο καὶ ἠπείθουν κακολογοῦντες τὴν ὁδὸν ἐνώπιον τοῦ πλήθους, ἀποστὰς ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν ἀφώρισεν τοὺς μαθητάς, καθ᾽ ἡμέραν διαλεγόμενος ἐν τῇ σχολῇ Τυράννου.
verses.transliteration: hōs de tines esklērynonto kai ēpeithoun kakologountes tēn hodon enōpion tou plēthous, apostas ap autōn aphōrisen tous mathētas, kath hēmeran dialegomenos en tē scholē Tyrannou.
verses.study_note: "That way" — the Christian movement's self-designation (cf. 9:2). Schole Tyrannou — a public lecture hall rented for instruction. Some texts add "from the fifth to the tenth hour" (11am-4pm) — the siesta when the hall was idle and people free.
verses.doctrinal_note: Where hearts harden, the apostle separates disciples without bitterness. Ongoing teaching is the backbone of every enduring church.

verses.kjv_text: And this continued by the space of two years; so that all they which dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord Jesus, both Jews and Greeks.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἐγένετο ἐπὶ ἔτη δύο, ὥστε πάντας τοὺς κατοικοῦντας τὴν Ἀσίαν ἀκοῦσαι τὸν λόγον τοῦ κυρίου, Ἰουδαίους τε καὶ Ἕλληνας.
verses.transliteration: touto de egeneto epi etē dyo, hōste pantas tous katoikountas tēn Asian akousai ton logon tou kyriou, Ioudaious te kai Hellēnas.
verses.study_note: "All Asia" — the Roman province of western Asia Minor. From Ephesus as hub, converts fanned out to evangelise Colossae, Laodicea, Hierapolis (Col 1:7; 4:13), and possibly other of the Seven Churches of Rev 2-3.
verses.doctrinal_note: God uses strategic cities as megaphones of the gospel. Faithful ministry in a regional capital echoes through every surrounding town.

verses.kjv_text: And God wrought special miracles by the hands of Paul:
verses.original_text: Δυνάμεις τε οὐ τὰς τυχούσας ὁ θεὸς ἐποίει διὰ τῶν χειρῶν Παύλου,
verses.transliteration: Dynameis te ou tas tychousas ho theos epoiei dia tōn cheirōn Paulou,
verses.study_note: Ou tas tychousas — literally "not the ordinary ones," extraordinary miracles. Ephesus was the world-capital of magic; God's power must visibly transcend theirs.
verses.doctrinal_note: Where occult powers are strongest, God often shows His supremacy most openly. Special circumstances call for extraordinary confirmations of the true gospel.

verses.kjv_text: So that from his body were brought unto the sick handkerchiefs or aprons, and the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out of them.
verses.original_text: ὥστε καὶ ἐπὶ τοὺς ἀσθενοῦντας ἀποφέρεσθαι ἀπὸ τοῦ χρωτὸς αὐτοῦ σουδάρια ἢ σιμικίνθια καὶ ἀπαλλάσσεσθαι ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν τὰς νόσους, τά τε πνεύματα τὰ πονηρὰ ἐκπορεύεσθαι.
verses.transliteration: hōste kai epi tous asthenountas apopheresthai apo tou chrōtos autou soudaria ē simikinthia kai apallassesthai ap autōn tas nosous, ta te pneumata ta ponēra ekporeuesthai.
verses.study_note: Soudaria (sweat-cloths) and simikinthia (work-aprons) — items from Paul's tentmaking trade, carried to the sick. Not magical amulets; rather, tokens of apostolic authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: God condescends to human need by tangible means. The virtue is His, not the cloth's — yet He graciously uses physical means to confirm His word.

verses.kjv_text: Then certain of the vagabond Jews, exorcists, took upon them to call over them which had evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, We adjure you by Jesus whom Paul preacheth.
verses.original_text: Ἐπεχείρησαν δέ τινες καὶ τῶν περιερχομένων Ἰουδαίων ἐξορκιστῶν ὀνομάζειν ἐπὶ τοὺς ἔχοντας τὰ πνεύματα τὰ πονηρὰ τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ λέγοντες· Ὁρκίζω ὑμᾶς τὸν Ἰησοῦν ὃν Παῦλος κηρύσσει.
verses.transliteration: Epecheirēsan de tines kai tōn perierchomenōn Ioudaiōn exorkistōn onomazein epi tous echontas ta pneumata ta ponēra to onoma tou kyriou Iēsou legontes; Horkizō hymas ton Iēsoun hon Paulos kēryssei.
verses.study_note: Itinerant Jewish exorcists exploit Jesus' name as magical formula. "Jesus whom Paul preacheth" — they invoke it secondhand, without personal faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Name of Christ is not a magical charm to be manipulated. Power flows where faith flows; where faith is absent, the Name burns the borrower.

verses.kjv_text: And there were seven sons of one Sceva, a Jew, and chief of the priests, which did so.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν δέ τινος Σκευᾶ Ἰουδαίου ἀρχιερέως ἑπτὰ υἱοὶ τοῦτο ποιοῦντες.
verses.transliteration: ēsan de tinos Skeua Ioudaiou archiereōs hepta huioi touto poiountes.
verses.study_note: Sceva is called "chief priest" — likely a self-styled title or a member of a chief-priestly family exiled from Jerusalem. The seven sons made their living by exorcism.
verses.doctrinal_note: Religious pedigree gives no power without personal faith. Seven sons of a priestly house cannot conjure what one believing apostle exercises.

verses.kjv_text: And the evil spirit answered and said, Jesus I know, and Paul I know; but who are ye?
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθὲν δὲ τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ πονηρὸν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς· Τὸν Ἰησοῦν γινώσκω καὶ τὸν Παῦλον ἐπίσταμαι, ὑμεῖς δὲ τίνες ἐστέ;
verses.transliteration: apokrithen de to pneuma to ponēron eipen autois; Ton Iēsoun ginōskō kai ton Paulon epistamai, hymeis de tines este?
verses.study_note: The demon knows Jesus by a full knowing (ginōskō — relational knowledge of the Conqueror) and Paul by acquaintance (epistamai — personal knowledge of the ambassador), but not the sons. The indictment is devastating.
verses.doctrinal_note: Hell knows who belongs to Christ. The demon respects only a Name it has met on its own field — and a man who bears that Name by true faith.

verses.kjv_text: And the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped on them, and overcame them, and prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐφαλόμενος ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἐπ᾽ αὐτοὺς ἐν ᾧ ἦν τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ πονηρὸν κατακυριεύσας ἀμφοτέρων ἴσχυσεν κατ᾽ αὐτῶν, ὥστε γυμνοὺς καὶ τετραυματισμένους ἐκφυγεῖν ἐκ τοῦ οἴκου ἐκείνου.
verses.transliteration: kai ephalomenos ho anthrōpos ep autous en hō ēn to pneuma to ponēron katakyrieusas amphoterōn ischysen kat autōn, hōste gymnous kai tetraumatismenous ekphygein ek tou oikou ekeinou.
verses.study_note: Amphoterōn, "both," probably meaning all of them collectively (NT Greek sometimes uses it of more than two). They flee stripped and wounded — the opposite of the liberation the gospel brings.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Name abused humiliates those who wield it falsely. When grace is treated as technique, it turns against the trickster.

verses.kjv_text: And this was known to all the Jews and Greeks also dwelling at Ephesus; and fear fell on them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.
verses.original_text: τοῦτο δὲ ἐγένετο γνωστὸν πᾶσιν Ἰουδαίοις τε καὶ Ἕλλησιν τοῖς κατοικοῦσιν τὴν Ἔφεσον, καὶ ἐπέπεσεν φόβος ἐπὶ πάντας αὐτούς, καὶ ἐμεγαλύνετο τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ.
verses.transliteration: touto de egeneto gnōston pasin Ioudaiois te kai Hellēsin tois katoikousin tēn Epheson, kai epepesen phobos epi pantas autous, kai emegalyneto to onoma tou kyriou Iēsou.
verses.study_note: Emegalyneto — "was magnified." The failed exorcism paradoxically exalts Christ's name; it alone carries real authority, and only true believers bear it rightly.
verses.doctrinal_note: God magnifies the name of Christ by rebuking those who counterfeit it. Even failed attempts to use Christ's name without faith serve His glory.

verses.kjv_text: And many that believed came, and confessed, and shewed their deeds.
verses.original_text: Πολλοί τε τῶν πεπιστευκότων ἤρχοντο ἐξομολογούμενοι καὶ ἀναγγέλλοντες τὰς πράξεις αὐτῶν.
verses.transliteration: Polloi te tōn pepisteukotōn ērchonto exomologoumenoi kai anangellontes tas praxeis autōn.
verses.study_note: The magnifying of Christ's name drives believers to confession. Some had evidently held onto residual occult practices; revival exposes secret sins.
verses.doctrinal_note: True revival shines into every corner. Renewed fear of God produces plain confession and practical renunciation of hidden compromises.

verses.kjv_text: Many of them also which used curious arts brought their books together, and burned them before all men: and they counted the price of them, and found it fifty thousand pieces of silver.
verses.original_text: ἱκανοὶ δὲ τῶν τὰ περίεργα πραξάντων συνενέγκαντες τὰς βίβλους κατέκαιον ἐνώπιον πάντων· καὶ συνεψήφισαν τὰς τιμὰς αὐτῶν καὶ εὗρον ἀργυρίου μυριάδας πέντε.
verses.transliteration: hikanoi de tōn ta perierga praxantōn synenenkantes tas biblous katekaion enōpion pantōn; kai synepsēphisan tas timas autōn kai heuron argyriou myriadas pente.
verses.study_note: "Curious arts" (perierga) = magical and occult practices. Ephesus was famous for magical texts (the "Ephesian writings," Ephesia grammata). Fifty thousand silver drachmas = roughly 50,000 days' wages — an enormous public destruction of valuable property.
verses.doctrinal_note: Genuine conversion costs. Burning magical books is visible, expensive repudiation of the old life — the pattern for every true convert who carries idolatrous things from his past.

verses.kjv_text: So mightily grew the word of God and prevailed.
verses.original_text: οὕτως κατὰ κράτος τοῦ κυρίου ὁ λόγος ηὔξανεν καὶ ἴσχυεν.
verses.transliteration: houtōs kata kratos tou kyriou ho logos ēuxanen kai ischyen.
verses.study_note: One of Luke's recurring progress reports (cf. 6:7; 12:24). "Mightily" (kata kratos) is a military metaphor — the word advances as a conquering force.
verses.doctrinal_note: The preached word, when truly the Lord's, grows and prevails. Nothing the world, flesh, or Satan throws at it can ultimately stop its advance.

verses.kjv_text: After these things were ended, Paul purposed in the spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem, saying, After I have been there, I must also see Rome.
verses.original_text: Ὡς δὲ ἐπληρώθη ταῦτα, ἔθετο ὁ Παῦλος ἐν τῷ πνεύματι διελθὼν τὴν Μακεδονίαν καὶ Ἀχαΐαν πορεύεσθαι εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα εἰπών· Ὅτι μετὰ τὸ γενέσθαι με ἐκεῖ δεῖ με καὶ Ῥώμην ἰδεῖν.
verses.transliteration: Hōs de eplērōthē tauta, etheto ho Paulos en tō pneumati dielthōn tēn Makedonian kai Achaian poreuesthai eis Hierosolyma eipōn; Hoti meta to genesthai me ekei dei me kai Rhōmēn idein.
verses.study_note: "I must see Rome" (dei) — divine necessity. Rome was the heart of the empire, the natural terminus of apostolic witness to the Gentiles. The remainder of Acts (20-28) narrates the journey to Rome in chains.
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle's ambitions are shaped by divine imperative, not mere human planning. The Lord's "must" becomes the servant's commitment.

verses.kjv_text: So he sent into Macedonia two of them that ministered unto him, Timotheus and Erastus; but he himself stayed in Asia for a season.
verses.original_text: ἀποστείλας δὲ εἰς τὴν Μακεδονίαν δύο τῶν διακονούντων αὐτῷ, Τιμόθεον καὶ Ἔραστον, αὐτὸς ἐπέσχεν χρόνον εἰς τὴν Ἀσίαν.
verses.transliteration: aposteilas de eis tēn Makedonian dyo tōn diakonountōn autō, Timotheon kai Eraston, autos epeschen chronon eis tēn Asian.
verses.study_note: Erastus is possibly the Corinthian city treasurer (Rom 16:23); Timothy is Paul's faithful young lieutenant. They are sent ahead to prepare the collection for Jerusalem (1 Cor 16; 2 Cor 8-9).
verses.doctrinal_note: The apostle delegates. Ministry is team-work; God grows leaders by entrusting them with real responsibility in the master's absence.

verses.kjv_text: And the same time there arose no small stir about that way.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ κατὰ τὸν καιρὸν ἐκεῖνον τάραχος οὐκ ὀλίγος περὶ τῆς ὁδοῦ.
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de kata ton kairon ekeinon tarachos ouk oligos peri tēs hodou.
verses.study_note: Litotes — "no small stir" = a great uproar. "That way" again = the Christian movement.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel's success creates opposition proportionate to its impact. Where Christ dethrones an economic idol, the city shakes.

verses.kjv_text: For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, which made silver shrines for Diana, brought no small gain unto the craftsmen;
verses.original_text: Δημήτριος γάρ τις ὀνόματι, ἀργυροκόπος, ποιῶν ναοὺς ἀργυροῦς Ἀρτέμιδος παρείχετο τοῖς τεχνίταις οὐκ ὀλίγην ἐργασίαν,
verses.transliteration: Dēmētrios gar tis onomati, argyrokopos, poiōn naous argyrous Artemidos pareicheto tois technitais ouk oligēn ergasian,
verses.study_note: Silver shrines — miniature models of the Artemision (temple of Artemis/Diana), one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, sold as souvenirs. Demetrius is a craft-guild leader whose business the gospel is destroying.
verses.doctrinal_note: Mammon and idolatry are perpetual partners. When Christ threatens profit built on false worship, the idol's friends reveal themselves.

verses.kjv_text: Whom he called together with the workmen of like occupation, and said, Sirs, ye know that by this craft we have our wealth.
verses.original_text: οὓς συναθροίσας καὶ τοὺς περὶ τὰ τοιαῦτα ἐργάτας εἶπεν· Ἄνδρες, ἐπίστασθε ὅτι ἐκ ταύτης τῆς ἐργασίας ἡ εὐπορία ἡμῖν ἐστιν,
verses.transliteration: hous synathroisas kai tous peri ta toiauta ergatas eipen; Andres, epistasthe hoti ek tautēs tēs ergasias hē euporia hēmin estin,
verses.study_note: Demetrius is frank: the issue is economic. Euporia, "prosperity." The defense of Artemis is cover for the defense of revenue.
verses.doctrinal_note: Attacks on the gospel frequently dress up economic self-interest as religious zeal. Follow the money and you often find the true motive.

verses.kjv_text: Moreover ye see and hear, that not alone at Ephesus, but almost throughout all Asia, this Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people, saying that they be no gods, which are made with hands:
verses.original_text: καὶ θεωρεῖτε καὶ ἀκούετε ὅτι οὐ μόνον Ἐφέσου ἀλλὰ σχεδὸν πάσης τῆς Ἀσίας ὁ Παῦλος οὗτος πείσας μετέστησεν ἱκανὸν ὄχλον λέγων ὅτι οὐκ εἰσὶν θεοὶ οἱ διὰ χειρῶν γινόμενοι.
verses.transliteration: kai theōreite kai akouete hoti ou monon Ephesou alla schedon pasēs tēs Asias ho Paulos houtos peisas metestēsen hikanon ochlon legōn hoti ouk eisin theoi hoi dia cheirōn ginomenoi.
verses.study_note: The enemy acknowledges what Luke has been asserting (v.10) — Paul's ministry has reached "almost all Asia." Demetrius summarises the gospel accurately: "they be no gods which are made with hands."
verses.doctrinal_note: The gospel's slanderers often summarise it better than its friends. When enemies report the wide impact, it testifies that the word has grown mightily.

verses.kjv_text: So that not only this our craft is in danger to be set at nought; but also that the temple of the great goddess Diana should be despised, and her magnificence should be destroyed, whom all Asia and the world worshippeth.
verses.original_text: οὐ μόνον δὲ τοῦτο κινδυνεύει ἡμῖν τὸ μέρος εἰς ἀπελεγμὸν ἐλθεῖν, ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸ τῆς μεγάλης θεᾶς Ἀρτέμιδος ἱερὸν εἰς οὐθὲν λογισθῆναι, μέλλειν τε καὶ καθαιρεῖσθαι τῆς μεγαλειότητος αὐτῆς, ἣν ὅλη ἡ Ἀσία καὶ ἡ οἰκουμένη σέβεται.
verses.transliteration: ou monon de touto kindyneuei hēmin to meros eis apelegmon elthein, alla kai to tēs megalēs theas Artemidos hieron eis outhen logisthēnai, mellein te kai kathaireisthai tēs megaleiotētos autēs, hēn holē hē Asia kai hē oikoumenē sebetai.
verses.study_note: Demetrius cleverly redirects focus from profits (his real concern) to the goddess's honour (the crowd's passion). Artemis of Ephesus was a fertility-goddess with a multi-breasted cult image, her temple 4× the size of the Parthenon.
